name: JavaScript
blurb: The language of the web — types, scope, closures, and the event loop.

cards.question: What is JavaScript?
cards.answer:
A high-level scripting language originally built to make web pages interactive.

High-level — abstracts away hardware. You write human-readable logic; the engine handles memory, CPU, and binary translation.

LevelExamples
HighJavaScript, Python, Java, C#
LowAssembly, C, C++

Script — plain-text instructions executed directly by the engine. No compilation, no build step.

// script.js — runs top to bottom as-is
console.log("Hello");   // engine reads and executes immediately

cards.question: Where did the name "JavaScript" come from?
cards.answer:
Originally called LiveScript — renamed to JavaScript purely for marketing, to ride Java's popularity at the time.

Despite the name, JavaScript has no relation to Java. Completely independent languages with different designs and purposes.

cards.question: What is ECMAScript?
cards.answer:
ECMAScript is the official specification — the rulebook — that JavaScript follows. JavaScript came first (1995); the first ECMAScript edition was published in 1997 to standardize it.

The name exists because "JavaScript" is Oracle's trademark — ECMA International used "ECMAScript" to avoid legal issues.

TermDefinition
ECMAScriptThe specification that defines how the language should work
JavaScriptThe actual language developers use, which implements that specification

Maintained by TC39. Since 2015 a new edition ships every year, each June. ES5 added strict mode and JSON. ES2015 (better known as ES6) brought classes, arrow functions, modules.

cards.question: How does a JavaScript engine work?
cards.answer:
A JavaScript engine reads your code in three steps:

  1. Parse — reads the script and checks the syntax
  2. Compile — converts it to machine code
  3. Execute — runs the machine code, fast

The engine doesn't stop there — it watches the code as it runs, analyzes the data flowing through it, and keeps optimizing the machine code on the fly.

Well-known engines: V8 (Chrome, Node.js), SpiderMonkey (Firefox).

This is a simplified view — engines are far more complex under the hood.

cards.question: What is JIT compilation?
cards.answer:
JIT (Just-In-Time) compilation translates code into native machine code while the program is running.

It is the middle ground between the two classic approaches:

ApproachHow it works
CompilerCompiles everything upfront, then runs
InterpreterRuns line by line, no compilation
JITCompiles at runtime, right when the code is needed

This is why JavaScript is fast despite being a scripting language. The engine also watches which parts run most often and optimizes those hotspots further.

cards.question: Is JavaScript interpreted or compiled?
cards.answer:
Neither purely. JavaScript is often called interpreted because there's no separate compile step — you write it and run it directly, unlike C or Java.

But under the hood, modern engines compile the code at runtime — that's the JIT compilation from the previous card.

The correct answer: JavaScript is JIT-compiled at runtime.

cards.question: What can JavaScript do in the browser?
cards.answer:
JavaScript in the browser can:

  • Manipulate the page — add, change, or remove HTML and CSS
  • React to user actions — clicks, key presses, mouse movements
  • Communicate with servers — send and receive data without reloading the page (AJAX)
  • Manage data — read and write cookies, use localStorage
  • Access devices — camera, microphone (with user permission)

cards.question: What can't JavaScript do in the browser?
cards.answer:
JavaScript runs in a sandbox — it can't touch the OS or other sites freely.

  • File system — can't read or write files on your computer. Only gets file access if the user explicitly selects one.
  • Other tabs — a page can't read data from a tab on a different domain. evil.com can't peek at your open gmail.com tab.
  • Cross-domain requests — a script can freely talk to its own server. Talking to a different domain requires that server to explicitly allow it (CORS headers).

These restrictions exist only in the browser. Node.js has none of them.

cards.question: What makes JavaScript unique?
cards.answer:
JavaScript is the only browser technology that combines native HTML/CSS integration, simplicity for everyday tasks, and out-of-the-box support in every major browser.

  • Native HTML/CSS integration — built into the browser alongside them, no setup needed.
  • Simple things stay simple — basic tasks don't need boilerplate.
  • Runs everywhere by default — supported in all major browsers, enabled out of the box.

cards.question: What are transpiled languages?
cards.answer:
Some languages compile down to JavaScript so they can run in the browser. You write in another language, a tool converts it to JS automatically.

LanguageWhat it adds
TypeScriptStrict types on top of JS (Microsoft)
CoffeeScriptCleaner, shorter syntax — popular with Ruby devs
FlowType annotations on top of JS (Facebook) — stripped out when converted to JS
DartStandalone language by Google, can target the browser
KotlinModern language, can compile to JS

Babel is not one of these — it's a tool, not a language. It transpiles modern JavaScript into older JavaScript so old browsers can run it.

Whatever language you use, the browser still runs JavaScript underneath — that's why understanding JS itself matters.

cards.question: What is a variable?
cards.answer:
A variable is a named storage for data — a place to keep a value so you can refer to it later by name.

let message = 'Hello';
message = 'World';     // the stored value can be replaced
console.log(message);  // World

You can picture it as a box with a name sticker on it: the box holds the value, and the name lets you find it again.

cards.question: What are the three ways to declare a variable?
cards.answer:
With one of three keywords: const, let, or the older var.

const pi = 3.14;
let count = 0;
var old = true;   // the pre-2015 form

Today let and const are used for everything; var is the older form, kept for backward compatibility. The keywords differ in where the variable is visible and whether it can change — the next cards cover that.

cards.question: const, let, var — which can be reassigned or redeclared?
cards.answer:
let can be reassigned and may be declared without a value. const must be given a value when declared and cannot be reassigned afterward. Neither can be declared twice in the same scope; var can.

const a;     // SyntaxError — const needs a value
let b;       // ok — b is undefined

let x = 1;
x = 2;       // ok — reassigned
let x = 3;   // SyntaxError — already declared

var y = 1;
var y = 2;   // ok — var allows redeclaration

Redeclaring the same name is usually a mistake, so let and const report it as an error.

cards.question: Does const make the value immutable?
cards.answer:
const stops the variable from being reassigned — it does not make the value itself read-only. If a const holds an object or array, its contents can still be changed.

The variable is a sticker on a box: const stops you moving the sticker to another box, but the box can still be filled and emptied.

const arr = [1, 2];
arr.push(3);   // ok — changing the contents
arr = [];      // TypeError — reassigning the variable

To freeze the contents as well, use Object.freeze.

cards.question: What are the rules and conventions for naming variables?
cards.answer:
A name may contain only letters, digits, and the symbols $ and _, and the first character cannot be a digit. Names are case-sensitive, and reserved words like let or class cannot be used.

let userName;   // ok
let $el, _x;    // ok
let 1st;        // SyntaxError — starts with a digit
let name, Name; // two different variables

By convention, use camelCase for variables and UPPER_CASE for hard-coded constants.

cards.question: Which declaration should you reach for by default, and why?
cards.answer:
Use const by default, and let only for values that need to change. Avoid var.

Defaulting to const makes it clear which values are meant to stay fixed, and both let and const avoid the function-scope and hoisting quirks of var.

cards.question: What is the difference between a statement and an expression?
cards.answer:
An expression is any code that produces a value. A statement is an instruction that performs an action. A program is a list of statements.

2 + 2          // expression → produces 4
let x = 5;     // statement → performs a declaration

Expressions appear inside statements: in let x = 2 + 2; the right side is an expression, the whole line is a statement.

cards.question: What is an expression statement?
cards.answer:
When an expression stands on its own as a statement, it is an expression statement: its value is computed and then discarded, so it is only useful for its side effect.

greet();   // function call — kept for its side effect
x = 3;     // assignment expression used as a statement
x + 1;     // valid, but pointless — the value is thrown away

cards.question: How do you choose between two values inline, without an if?
cards.answer:
Statements such as if and for do not produce values, so they cannot be used where a value is expected. Use an expression form instead: the ternary ? :, or && / ||.

const max = a > b ? a : b;   // ok — ternary is an expression
const max = if (a > b) a;    // SyntaxError — if is a statement

cards.question: When does { start a code block, and when an object literal?
cards.answer:
Position decides. At the start of a statement { begins a block; in an expression position the same { begins an object literal.

{}              // an empty block
const o = {};   // an object literal

This is why an object literal returned from an arrow function must be wrapped: () => ({}).

cards.question: What is strict mode and how do you enable it?
cards.answer:
Strict mode is a restricted variant of JavaScript that makes the engine stricter about mistakes. You enable it with the directive 'use strict' at the top of a script or a function body.

'use strict';
// ... the rest of the file runs in strict mode

Modern code rarely writes it: ES modules and class bodies are in strict mode automatically.

The directive must be at the very top — only comments may appear above it, otherwise it is ignored. And once enabled it cannot be cancelled: there is no "no use strict".

cards.question: What does strict mode change?
cards.answer:
It turns silent mistakes into thrown errors and removes some unsafe behavior.

  • Assigning to an undeclared variable throws, instead of silently creating a global.
  • Writing to a read-only property throws instead of failing quietly.
  • Duplicate function parameter names become a syntax error.
  • this is undefined in a plain function call, not the global object.
  • The old with statement is forbidden.

cards.question: Why does strict mode exist, and should you use it?
cards.answer:
It surfaces bugs early by refusing to ignore questionable code, and it disables legacy behavior that made code harder to optimize and reason about.

In practice you get it for free — modules and classes are always strict — so you only add 'use strict' in old-style, non-module scripts.

cards.question: What are unary, binary, and ternary operators?
cards.answer:
An operator acts on values called operands. Operators are grouped by how many operands they take: unary (one), binary (two), and the single ternary (three).

-x          // unary minus (one operand)
a + b       // binary plus (two operands)
a ? b : c   // the ternary operator (three operands)

cards.question: What are the arithmetic operators, and how does + treat strings?
cards.answer:
The arithmetic operators are + - * / plus % (remainder) and ** (exponentiation). They all convert their operands to numbers — except +, which concatenates if either operand is a string.

2 ** 3     // 8
10 % 3     // 1
'1' + 2    // '12'  — string concatenation
'5' - 2    // 3     — converted to numbers

cards.question: What do the compound assignment operators like += and *= do?
cards.answer:
x += y is shorthand for x = x + y. Every arithmetic operator has a compound form: += -= *= /= %= **=.

let n = 2;
n += 3;    // 5  (n = n + 3)
n *= 2;    // 10

let s = 'Hi';
s += '!';  // 'Hi!' — += concatenates strings too

They have the same low precedence as plain assignment, so the right side is computed first: n *= 3 + 2 means n = n * 5.

cards.question: What do the logical operators && || ! return, and what is short-circuiting?
cards.answer:
The logical operators are && (and), || (or), and ! (not). && and || do not return a boolean — they return one of the operands, and they short-circuit, stopping as soon as the result is known.

a && b   // a if a is falsy, otherwise b
a || b   // a if a is truthy, otherwise b
'' || 'default'   // 'default'

This lets you write user.name || 'Guest' or isReady && render().

cards.question: What are operator precedence and associativity?
cards.answer:
Precedence decides which operator runs first (* before +). Associativity decides the order when operators share precedence: most are left-to-right, but assignment is right-to-left. Parentheses override both.

2 + 3 * 4    // 14, not 20
a = b = 5    // right-to-left: b = 5, then a = 5

cards.question: What's the difference between x++ and ++x?
cards.answer:
++ and -- change a variable by one. Prefix (++x) changes it and returns the new value; postfix (x++) returns the old value and then changes it.

let a = 1;
let b = a++;   // b = 1, a = 2
let c = ++a;   // c = 3, a = 3

cards.question: What does the nullish coalescing operator ?? do?
cards.answer:
a ?? b returns b only when a is null or undefined. It is like ||, but it does not trigger on other falsy values such as 0 or ''.

0 || 5     // 5  — || treats 0 as missing
0 ?? 5     // 0  — ?? only fills in null / undefined

Use ?? for defaults when 0, '', or false are valid values.

For safety, ?? cannot be mixed with || or && in one expression — that is a SyntaxError. Add parentheses to state the order yourself:

a && b ?? c     // SyntaxError
(a && b) ?? c   // ok

cards.question: What does optional chaining ?. do?
cards.answer:
a?.b reads b only if a is not null or undefined; otherwise the whole expression short-circuits to undefined instead of throwing. It works for properties, calls, and dynamic keys.

user?.address?.city   // undefined if user or address is missing
user.getName?.()      // called only if getName exists
arr?.[0]              // index access, guarded

Use it for genuinely optional parts — not to paper over data that should always be there.

?. is for reading and calling, not writing — it cannot be the target of an assignment. And the variable before it must be declared; ?. only guards against null/undefined values, not missing variables:

user?.name = 'John';   // SyntaxError — can't assign to user?.name
noSuchVar?.x;          // ReferenceError — noSuchVar was never declared

cards.question: What do the logical assignment operators ??= ||= &&= do?
cards.answer:
They combine a logical operator with assignment. x ??= y assigns y only if x is null/undefined; ||= assigns if x is falsy; &&= assigns if x is truthy.

let opts = {};
opts.size ??= 'M';   // set only if not already set

Each short-circuits, so the right side runs only when the assignment actually happens.

cards.question: What are the conditional statements?
cards.answer:
if runs a block when its condition is truthy; else and else if handle the alternatives. To choose between two values, the ternary cond ? a : b is the expression form.

if (score >= 50) pass();
else fail();

const grade = score >= 50 ? 'pass' : 'fail';

cards.question: How does switch work, and what is fall-through?
cards.answer:
switch compares one value against several case labels using strict equality (===). Execution falls through into the next case unless you break, and default handles no match.

switch (day) {
  case 6:
  case 0:
    return 'weekend';   // shared via fall-through
  default:
    return 'weekday';
}

Forgetting break is a classic bug — execution keeps running into the next case.

cards.question: What are the loop statements: while, do...while, and for?
cards.answer:
while repeats while its condition holds, checked before each pass. do...while checks after the body, so it always runs at least once. for packs setup, condition, and step into one header.

for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) { /* ... */ }
while (cond) { /* ... */ }
do { /* ... */ } while (cond);

cards.question: What's the difference between for...of and for...in?
cards.answer:
for...of loops over the values of an iterable (array, string, Map, Set). for...in loops over the keys of an object — including inherited ones — and is not meant for arrays.

for (const x of [10, 20]) { } // 10, 20  (values)
for (const k in obj) { }      // 'a', 'b' (keys)

cards.question: What do break and continue do, and what are labels?
cards.answer:
break stops a loop entirely; continue skips to the next iteration. A label lets break or continue target an outer loop from inside a nested one.

outer:
for (const row of grid) {
  for (const cell of row) {
    if (cell === target) break outer;
  }
}

cards.question: Are semicolons required in JavaScript?
cards.answer:
Mostly no. JavaScript has Automatic Semicolon Insertion (ASI): the parser inserts semicolons at most line breaks, so you can usually omit them.

let a = 1   // works — ASI adds the semicolon
let b = 2

cards.question: When does ASI cause bugs?
cards.answer:
ASI follows fixed rules, and a few cases break code. The worst is return with its value on the next line — ASI ends the statement right after return, so it returns undefined.

return
  value;   // returns undefined, not value

Lines starting with ( [ or a backtick can also merge with the previous line.

cards.question: What's the practical rule for semicolons?
cards.answer:
Pick one style and stay consistent. If you omit semicolons, guard the risky lines by starting any line that begins with ( or [ with a semicolon.

;[1, 2].forEach(fn)   // leading ; stops it merging with the line above

Most teams let a formatter (Prettier) settle the choice automatically.

cards.question: Does JavaScript have types?
cards.answer:
Yes. JavaScript is dynamically typed, not untyped: the type lives on the value, not the variable.

In statically typed languages a variable is declared with a type and can only hold values of that type. In JavaScript a variable is just a name — it can hold any type, and you can reassign it to a value of a different type at any moment:

let x = 42;        // x holds a number
x = "hello";       // the same variable now holds a string
x = true;          // and now a boolean

But every value always has exactly one type. 42 is always a number, "hello" is always a string. "JS is untyped" is a myth — only the variables are type-free, the values never are.

cards.question: What types does JavaScript have?
cards.answer:
Eight: seven primitive types plus object, the only non-primitive.

TypeExample values
undefinedlet x; // x is undefined
nulllet x = null;
booleantrue, false
number42, 3.14, NaN, Infinity
string"hi", 'hi', `hi`
symbolSymbol("id")
bigint42n
object{}, [], function(){}

The object type is broader than it looks: arrays, functions, dates, regexes, Maps and Sets are all objects. Everything that is not one of the seven primitives is an object.

cards.question: What does it mean that primitives are immutable?
cards.answer:
A primitive value can never be changed. Every operation on a primitive produces a new value; the original stays untouched.

let s = "hi";
let upper = s.toUpperCase();
console.log(upper); // "HI" — a new string
console.log(s);     // "hi" — the original is untouched

There is no method that modifies a string, number, or boolean in place. When you write s = "bye", you are not changing the string "hi"; you are pointing the variable at a different value. Reassigning a variable and mutating a value are two different things.

Objects are the opposite: they are mutable. You can add, change, and delete their properties after creation.

cards.question: How do primitives and objects behave differently on assignment?
cards.answer:
Primitives are copied by value: each variable gets its own independent copy. Objects are shared by reference: variables point at the same object.

let a = "hi";
let b = a;
b = "bye";
console.log(a); // "hi"  — primitives are copied by value

let x = { n: 1 };
let y = x;
y.n = 2;
console.log(x.n); // 2  — objects are shared by reference

After let b = a, the variables hold two separate strings — changing one cannot affect the other. After let y = x, both variables hold a reference to the same object, so a mutation through y is visible through x.

The same rule applies to function calls: passing an object to a function passes the reference, so the function can mutate the caller's object.

cards.question: What does the typeof operator do?
cards.answer:
typeof returns a string naming the type of a value. It is always a string, even for nonsense input.

The full result list:

typeof undefined     // "undefined"
typeof true          // "boolean"
typeof 42            // "number"
typeof 42n           // "bigint"
typeof "hi"          // "string"
typeof Symbol()      // "symbol"
typeof {}            // "object"
typeof []            // "object"   (arrays are objects)
typeof null          // "object"   (a historical bug)
typeof function(){}  // "function" (functions are callable objects)
typeof undeclaredVar // "undefined" — does not throw

One special case: functions get "function" even though they are objects — you can attach properties to them like any object. The spec calls them "callable objects", and typeof carves them out as a separate case. For exhaustive type checks, treat "function" as a subtype of "object".

cards.question: Why does typeof null return "object"?
cards.answer:
It is a bug from 1995, kept because fixing it would break too much existing code on the web.

null is its own primitive type — it is not an object. But typeof has reported "object" for it since the first version of JavaScript, and the result is now frozen into the language.

So mentally treat null as its own type, and never use typeof to detect it. Check with strict equality instead:

let value = null;

typeof value    // "object" — the bug
value === null  // true     — the correct check

cards.question: Why doesn't typeof throw on undeclared variables?
cards.answer:
It is intentional: typeof is the one safe way to check whether an identifier exists at all.

Any other reference to an undeclared variable throws ReferenceError. typeof just returns "undefined" instead, which makes it useful for feature-detecting globals that may or may not exist in the current environment:

if (typeof someGlobal !== "undefined") {
  // safe to use someGlobal
}

someGlobal; // ReferenceError — any other access throws

cards.question: How do you tell an array from a plain object?
cards.answer:
Use Array.isArray(). typeof can't help — arrays are objects, so it returns "object" for both.

typeof []   // "object"
typeof {}   // "object" — typeof can't tell them apart

Array.isArray([])  // true  — use this
Array.isArray({})  // false

instanceof Array also works, but it checks against the Array constructor of the current realm. An array created in another iframe (or another realm) has a different Array, so the check fails for it:

[] instanceof Array // true, but breaks across iframes/realms

Array.isArray() has no such limitation — prefer it.

cards.question: What are the three states that all look like undefined?
cards.answer:
Three distinct states all look like undefined:

StateMeans
undefined (the value)Declared but holds no value
UndeclaredThe identifier doesn't exist at all
Uninitialized (TDZ)A let/const before its declaration line

cards.question: What happens when you access an undefined, undeclared, or TDZ variable?
cards.answer:
StateOn access
undefined (the value)You get undefined; test with v === undefined or typeof v === "undefined"
UndeclaredDirect access throws ReferenceError; typeof name is safe → "undefined"
Uninitialized (TDZ)Any access throws ReferenceError — even typeof
 
let x;
console.log(x);        // undefined   — declared, never assigned
console.log(typeof y); // "undefined" — y is undeclared; typeof is safe
console.log(typeof z); // ReferenceError: Cannot access 'z' before initialization
let z = 1;             // z exists above, but is in its dead zone until here

cards.question: What's the semantic difference between null and undefined?
cards.answer:
undefined is what the engine assigns when something has no value yet; null is what the programmer assigns to say "intentionally empty".

Convention: never assign undefined yourself — reserve null for deliberate emptiness, so the two stay distinct.

cards.question: Where does the engine produce undefined?
cards.answer:
Everywhere a value is simply missing: an unassigned variable, a function with no return, an argument that wasn't passed. You write null yourself when emptiness is deliberate.

let a;
function f() {}
console.log(a);     // undefined — variable never assigned
console.log(f());   // undefined — function returns nothing

let user = null;    // "no user" — intentional, set by you

cards.question: Are null and undefined equal?
cards.answer:
Loosely equal, but not strictly equal:

null == undefined   // true
null === undefined  // false

cards.question: What is NaN?
cards.answer:
NaN stands for "Not a Number" — yet it is a number: typeof NaN === "number". The better mental model: NaN is the special number value that represents an invalid numeric operation.

Whenever JavaScript is forced to produce a number but the operation makes no numeric sense, the result is NaN:

typeof NaN     // "number"
Number("foo")  // NaN — the string has no numeric meaning
0 / 0          // NaN — mathematically undefined
parseInt("x")  // NaN — no digits to parse

So the name describes where it came from (a failed numeric operation), not its type. Think of it as the number type's built-in error value.

cards.question: Why is NaN === NaN false?
cards.answer:
The IEEE 754 floating-point standard defines NaN as unequal to everything — including itself. JavaScript numbers follow IEEE 754, so JS inherits that rule.

NaN === NaN  // false
NaN == NaN   // false
NaN < 1      // false — <, <=, >, >=, ==, === with NaN are all false

The standard chose this on purpose: an invalid numeric result should propagate through calculations (NaN + 1 is NaN) and stay detectable at the end. If NaN === NaN were true, a bogus result could quietly compare equal to another bogus result and pass a check it should fail.

NaN is the only value in the language that is not equal to itself. That makes === useless for detecting it — use Number.isNaN(x) instead.

cards.question: How do you detect NaN reliably?
cards.answer:
Use Number.isNaN(x): it does no coercion and returns true only when x is actually NaN.

The trap is the older global isNaN. It first coerces its argument to a number, so any value that coerces to NaN reports true — even though it isn't NaN:

isNaN("foo")          // true   — WRONG: "foo" is coerced to NaN first
Number.isNaN("foo")   // false  — correct: a string is not NaN
Number.isNaN(NaN)     // true
Number.isNaN(0 / 0)   // true

Two other reliable checks exist:

Object.is(x, NaN)  // true if x is NaN
x !== x            // true if x is NaN — it is the only self-unequal value

The x !== x idiom predates ES2015 and still appears in older code and polyfills. Today the rule is simple: use Number.isNaN, never the global isNaN.

cards.question: What are Infinity and -Infinity?
cards.answer:
Infinity and -Infinity are special number values for results too big (or too negatively big) for a number to store. Their typeof is "number".

They appear from division by zero and from overflow — unlike many languages, JavaScript never throws on division by zero:

1 / 0                 // Infinity
-1 / 0                // -Infinity
Number.MAX_VALUE * 2  // Infinity — overflow
Math.pow(10, 1000)    // Infinity

Infinity compares greater than every other number, and most arithmetic keeps it infinite: Infinity + 1 is still Infinity. But operations with no sensible answer give NaN:

Infinity - Infinity   // NaN
Infinity / Infinity   // NaN
0 * Infinity          // NaN

To check that a value is a real, usable number, use Number.isFinite(x) — it returns false for Infinity, -Infinity, and NaN.

cards.question: What is -0 and why does it exist?
cards.answer:
JavaScript has two zeros: 0 and -0. They exist because JS numbers are IEEE 754 floats, and a float keeps a sign bit even when the value is zero.

-0 appears whenever a computation reaches zero "from the negative side" — for example -1 * 0 or negating 0. Most of the time it behaves exactly like 0: even === cannot tell them apart. Only Object.is and division reveal the difference.

-0 === 0          // true  — === cannot tell them apart
Object.is(-0, 0)  // false — Object.is can
-0 < 0            // false — it is not "less than zero"
1 / -0            // -Infinity
1 / 0             // Infinity

One quirk: Math.sign(-0) returns -0 itself, not -1 — it passes zeros through unchanged instead of reporting a sign.

cards.question: When does -0 actually matter?
cards.answer:
It matters when the sign carries meaning of its own: tracking direction, animation libraries, certain financial conventions. Most code can safely ignore it.

The classic example is direction. A velocity of -0 can mean "was heading left, now at zero speed" — which is different from 0, "fully stopped." A plain === 0 check erases that distinction; Object.is keeps it.

let velocity = -0;          // decelerated to zero while moving left

velocity === 0              // true  — looks fully stopped
Object.is(velocity, -0)     // true  — the leftward sign is still there
Object.is(velocity, 0)      // false

The rule is narrow: reach for Object.is only when you compare a value to zero and care which side of zero it came from. Everywhere else, treat -0 and 0 as the same number.

cards.question: Which built-in constructors should be called with new?
cards.answer:
Only Object, Array, Function, Date, RegExp, and Error. String/Number/Boolean must be called as plain functions, and for Symbol/BigInt new throws.

ConstructorUse new?
Object, Array, Function, Date, RegExp, ErrorYes — or prefer the literal form when one exists: {}, [], /.../
String, Number, BooleanNever — call as plain functions for explicit conversion: String(42), Number("3"), Boolean(0)
Symbol, BigIntNever — new actually throws. Call as functions: Symbol("id"), BigInt(42)

The middle row is the dangerous one: new String("hi") does not throw — it quietly creates a wrapper object instead of a primitive, which behaves subtly differently. Symbol and BigInt at least fail loudly:

String(42);       // "42"
Number("3");      // 3
Boolean(0);       // false
new Symbol("id"); // TypeError: Symbol is not a constructor

cards.question: Why does 'hi'.toUpperCase() work if 'hi' is a primitive?
cards.answer:
Because of auto-boxing: the engine temporarily wraps the primitive in the matching wrapper object, calls the method, and discards the wrapper.

"hi".toUpperCase(); // "HI" — engine briefly wraps "hi" in a String object

The value in your variable stays a plain primitive the whole time. The step-by-step boxing mechanics are covered in the Coercion topic.

cards.question: Why is new String('x') === 'x' false?
cards.answer:
Because new String("x") creates a wrapper object — a different type from the primitive string, and === across types is always false.

let a = "hi";              // primitive string
let b = new String("hi");  // String OBJECT (wrapper)

typeof a;  // "string"
typeof b;  // "object"
a === b;   // false — compared across types
a == b;    // true  — coercion strips the wrapper, then compares

== returns true because loose equality coerces the wrapper back to a primitive before comparing.

The rule: never construct primitive wrappers.

cards.question: Is everything in JavaScript an object?
cards.answer:
No — it's a myth. Primitives are not objects. They auto-box temporarily for method access, but the underlying value stays a primitive.

The proof is mutation: assigning a property to a primitive (s.foo = 1) silently does nothing — the assignment lands on a temporary wrapper that is discarded immediately, so s.foo reads back undefined. How that wrapper works is covered in the Coercion topic.

Correct mental model: primitives are not objects, but the language makes them look like objects sometimes via auto-boxing.

cards.question: What is bigint and why does number fail without it?
cards.answer:
bigint is the primitive for integers of arbitrary size. A regular number is a 64-bit float, exact for integers only up to Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER (253 − 1) — past that, distinct integers collapse into one value.

Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER;                 // 9007199254740991
9007199254740992 === 9007199254740993;   // true — precision is gone
9007199254740992n + 1n;                  // 9007199254740993n — exact

cards.question: How do you create a bigint, and what is its typeof?
cards.answer: Use the n suffix (42n) or call BigInt(42). Its typeof is "bigint".

cards.question: Can you mix bigint and number in arithmetic?
cards.answer:
No — JavaScript refuses to guess the precision and throws. Convert one side explicitly first:

1n + 2;            // TypeError: Cannot mix BigInt and other types, use explicit conversions
1n + BigInt(2);    // 3n
Number(10n) + 2;   // 12

cards.question: What are typical uses for bigint?
cards.answer: Database IDs, timestamps in nanoseconds, cryptography — anywhere integers can grow past 253.

cards.question: What is symbol and what is it for?
cards.answer:
A symbol is a primitive value that serves as a unique identifier. Every call to Symbol() produces a brand-new value, guaranteed not to equal any other.

You can pass a description — it is only a label for debugging. Two symbols with the same description are still different values:

let a = Symbol("id");
let b = Symbol("id");

a === b;          // false — each symbol is unique
typeof a;         // "symbol"
a.description;    // "id" — just the label

The main use is collision-free object property keys. A symbol key can never accidentally clash with a string key or with a symbol someone else created — even if the descriptions match:

let user = { name: "Ann" };
let id = Symbol("id");

user[id] = 123;   // cannot collide with any other key

This makes symbols ideal for attaching data to objects you don't own, such as objects coming from third-party code.

cards.question: What does Symbol.for(key) do?
cards.answer:
Usually all symbols are unique, even with the same name. Symbol.for(key) is the exception: it returns the same symbol for the same key from anywhere in the app via the global symbol registry, creating it on first request.

let id = Symbol.for("id");      // created in the registry
let idAgain = Symbol.for("id"); // read back — anywhere, even another file

id === idAgain; // true — same symbol, same key

Use it when a symbol must be shared by name — independent code agreeing on one property key without passing the symbol around. Use plain Symbol() for keys you control and pass by reference.

cards.question: What does Symbol.keyFor(sym) return, and when?
cards.answer:
The reverse lookup — it returns the registry key, and works only for global symbols. A regular symbol gives undefined (its description is all it has).

Symbol.keyFor(Symbol.for("id")); // "id"
Symbol.keyFor(Symbol("id"));     // undefined — not in the registry
Symbol("id").description;        // "id" — the label still readable

cards.question: What is coercion?
cards.answer:
Coercion is type conversion — JavaScript converting a value from one type to another.

Every conversion is governed by a well-defined spec algorithm. When code surprises you, the language is not being random — you just did not predict which algorithm ran.

"5" + 1;   // "51" — + saw a string, so it converted 1 to "1" and concatenated
"5" - 1;   // 4    — - only works on numbers, so it converted "5" to 5

Both lines followed exact rules; they just followed different rules. Learning coercion means learning which algorithm each operator runs.

cards.question: What are the two rules that decide every + expression?
cards.answer:
After ToPrimitive: if either operand is a string, + concatenates; otherwise both go through ToNumber. Every other arithmetic operator (-, *, /, %, **) always forces numbers.

"5" + 1;   // "51" — string operand → concat
"5" - 1;   // 4    — minus always forces ToNumber
1 + null;  // 1    — no string → ToNumber: null → 0

That single asymmetry — + being the only operator that can produce a string — explains the whole family. Don't memorize outputs; walk the algorithm and the answer is forced.

cards.question: What are the four abstract operations?
cards.answer:
The spec defines four conversion algorithms: ToPrimitive, ToString, ToNumber, and ToBoolean. Every coercion in the language eventually calls one of them.

OperationInputOutputTriggered by
ToPrimitive(input, hint)objectprimitivethe other three when given an object; +, == on objects
ToString(input)anystringString(x), template literals `${x}`, + when an operand is a string
ToNumber(input)anynumberNumber(x), +x, -x, *, /, %, **; <, > — unless both operands are strings, which compare character by character
ToBoolean(input)anybooleanif, while, &&, ||, !, ternary, Boolean(x)

They are abstract: you cannot call them directly. They run inside operators and built-ins. Knowing which operator triggers which operation is the whole game.

cards.question: ToString vs String() vs .toString() — what's the difference?
cards.answer:
Three layers. Capital ToString/ToNumber/ToPrimitive are spec-only algorithms — you never type them; the engine runs them. String(x)/Number(x) are the callable built-ins that trigger the matching algorithm. x.toString()/x.valueOf() are the instance methods the algorithm calls on objects.

You write:    String(obj)
                ↓ (built-in triggers the spec algorithm)
Engine runs:  ToString(obj)              ← spec algorithm
                ↓ (obj is an object, hands the job over)
Engine runs:  ToPrimitive(obj, "string") ← another algorithm
                ↓ (this one calls instance methods)
Engine calls: obj.toString()             ← actual method on the object

cards.question: When does ToPrimitive run, and what is a hint?
cards.answer:
ToPrimitive runs when the language needs a primitive but got an object. It takes a hint — "string", "number", or "default" — signalling which primitive the caller prefers.

HintSent whenExamples
"string"a string is clearly expected`${obj}`, String(obj)
"number"a number is clearly expectedNumber(obj), +obj, obj * 2, obj < 5
"default"the operator works on bothobj + x, obj == x

If the input is already a primitive, ToPrimitive returns it unchanged — the algorithm is only meaningful for objects.

cards.question: What does ToString return for each primitive?
cards.answer:
ToString knows the answer for every primitive directly; for objects it delegates to ToPrimitive(obj, "string").

InputResult
undefined"undefined"
null"null"
true / false"true" / "false"
42, 3.14"42", "3.14"
NaN, Infinity, -Infinity"NaN", "Infinity", "-Infinity"
very large/small numbersexponential: "1e+21", "1e-7"
42n (bigint)"42"
"hello""hello"
objectresult of ToPrimitive(obj, "string")

cards.question: What does String() return for arrays and plain objects?
cards.answer:
Array's toString is join(","); a plain object's toString always returns "[object Object]" regardless of contents.

String([]);                  // ""
String([1, 2, 3]);           // "1,2,3"
String([null, undefined]);   // ","          — render as empty
String([[1, 2], [3, 4]]);    // "1,2,3,4"    — nested flatten
String({});                  // "[object Object]"
String({ a: 1 });            // "[object Object]" — contents hidden

For a readable object representation, use JSON.stringify.

cards.question: What does ToNumber return for each value?
cards.answer:
For strings the whole string must parse; for objects it runs ToPrimitive(obj, "number") first.

InputResult
undefinedNaN
null0
true / false1 / 0
"" or whitespace-only " "0
"42"42
"42abc"NaN — whole string must parse, unlike parseInt
"0x10"16 (hex)
"0b10", "0o10"2, 8 (binary/octal)
"1e3"1000
Symbol(…)TypeError
objectToPrimitive(obj, "number"), then ToNumber

cards.question: Why is ToNumber(null) different from ToNumber(undefined)?
cards.answer:
An asymmetry worth memorizing: undefined gives NaN, but null gives 0 — a common source of off-by-zero bugs.

Number(undefined);  // NaN
Number(null);       // 0

cards.question: What does Number() return for arrays and objects?
cards.answer:
No special rules — they fall out by composition: ToPrimitive (→ a string) then string parsing.

Number([]);      // 0     — [].toString() is ""  → Number("") is 0
Number([42]);    // 42    — [42].toString() is "42"
Number([1, 2]);  // NaN   — "1,2" is not numeric
Number({});      // NaN   — "[object Object]" is not numeric

cards.question: What is the difference between Number() and parseInt()/parseFloat()?
cards.answer:
Number() is the strict whole-string converter; parseInt / parseFloat are forgiving prefix-parsers that read as much of the string as they can and stop.

Number("42abc");    // NaN  — whole string must parse
parseInt("42abc");  // 42   — parses the prefix, stops at the first non-numeric char
parseInt("abc42");  // NaN  — no numeric prefix

Number("");         // 0
parseInt("");       // NaN  — different defaults for empty input

parseInt("3.14");   // 3    — integer prefix only
parseFloat("3.14"); // 3.14

parseInt("10", 2);  // 2    — second arg is the radix

Always pass the radix explicitly — parseInt(x, 10) — to avoid the historical octal trap.

Different tools: Number for strict conversion, parseInt / parseFloat for pulling a number off the front of messy input.

cards.question: How does ToBoolean decide truthy or falsy?
cards.answer:
ToBoolean is not computed — the spec keeps a fixed list of falsy values. Anything on the list is false; everything else is true.

The full list — eight values (seven if you fold -0 into 0):

undefined   null   false   NaN   0   -0   0n   ""

There is no recursion or composition: the rule is simply "is this value on the list."

cards.question: What are the common truthy surprises in JS?
cards.answer:
Non-empty strings, all objects, and wrappers are truthy — even when their content looks falsy.

Boolean("0");                // true — non-empty string
Boolean("false");            // true — non-empty string
Boolean(" ");                // true — a single space
Boolean([]);                 // true — objects are always truthy
Boolean({});                 // true
Boolean(new Boolean(false)); // true — the wrapper OBJECT is truthy
Boolean(function(){});       // true
Boolean(Infinity);           // true

cards.question: How do you test for an empty array, since [] is truthy?
cards.answer: Check arr.length === 0 explicitly. [] is truthy not because of its contents but because no object is on the falsy list — Boolean coercion of any object always gives true.

cards.question: What happens when you call a method on a primitive?
cards.answer:
The engine implicitly coerces the primitive into its wrapper object, performs the member access, and immediately discards the wrapper. This is auto-boxing — implicit primitive→object coercion.

The wrapper lives for a single member access, not even a full statement. Each access gets a fresh wrapper, used once and thrown away.

let s = "hi";
s.length;          // 2    — boxed, .length read, wrapper discarded
s.toUpperCase();   // "HI" — a new wrapper, same pattern

cards.question: Why can't you store a property on a primitive?
cards.answer:
The assignment lands on a throwaway wrapper that is gone by the next access — there is no persistent object to hold the property.

let s = "hi";
s.foo = 1;   // assigned to a throwaway wrapper
s.foo;       // undefined — that wrapper no longer exists

cards.question: What is the difference between strict and sloppy mode when assigning a property to a primitive?
cards.answer:
Sloppy mode fails silently (making the bug hard to spot); strict mode throws.

"use strict";
let s = "hi";
s.foo = 1;   // TypeError: Cannot create property 'foo' on string 'hi'

cards.question: Does auto-boxing change the variable's type?
cards.answer: No. Through all member access s never stops being a primitive — typeof s stays "string". The wrapper object exists only inside individual accesses; your code never gets a reference to it.

cards.question: Which values can be boxed, and which throw?
cards.answer:
Five primitive types have wrapper objects and can be boxed: String, Number, Boolean, Symbol, BigInt. null and undefined have no wrappers — any member access on them throws TypeError.

Member accessWrapperResult
"hi".toUpperCase()String"HI"
(42).toFixed(2)Number"42.00"
true.toString()Boolean"true"
Symbol("id").descriptionSymbol"id"
(10n).toString()BigInt"10"
null.foo—TypeError
undefined.toString()—TypeError

The null / undefined gap is where the most familiar runtime error in JavaScript comes from — boxing has nothing to wrap, so member access fails immediately:

let user;
user.name;   // TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'name')

cards.question: What is the difference between explicit and implicit coercion?
cards.answer:
Both run the same four abstract operations underneath — the difference is whether the conversion is visible in the source.

// Explicit — the code says "convert":
String(42);   Number("3.14");   Boolean(0);
+x;           !!x;              // recognised idioms

// Implicit — a hidden side effect of an operator:
"x" + 1;      // + silently runs ToString on 1
if (user) {}  // ToBoolean(user) runs invisibly
`${n}`;       // template literal calls ToString
x == y;       // coerces when types differ

Rule of thumb: if a reader asks "wait, where is the conversion?" — it's implicit.

cards.question: How do symbols behave under coercion?
cards.answer:
Symbols are a coercion partial-citizen: explicit String(s) works, implicit string coercion throws, number coercion throws even explicitly, and boolean coercion is fine.

const s = Symbol("id");

String(s);   // "Symbol(id)" — explicit OK
`${s}`;      // TypeError — implicit ToString throws
"" + s;      // TypeError — same, implicit via +

Number(s);   // TypeError — even explicit
+s;          // TypeError

Boolean(s);  // true — symbols are truthy
if (s) { /* runs */ }

This is deliberate: a symbol leaking into a string or a number is almost always an accident, so the language refuses to do it silently. Only the explicit String(s) says "I really mean it".

Rule: with symbols, always coerce explicitly with String(). Anything implicit is likely a bug.

cards.question: What is the real difference between == and ===?
cards.answer:
Both operators check types. They differ in exactly one place: what happens when the types mismatch — === returns false, while == runs a coercion algorithm and compares again.

The popular framing "=== checks types, == doesn't" is wrong. == checks types too — its first step is "same type? then compare exactly like ===". The type check is what decides whether coercion fires at all.

1 === "1"   // false — types differ, stop
1 ==  "1"   // true  — types differ, coerce "1" → 1, compare again

So the real question behind choosing an operator is not "do I want type checking" — both do it. It is: do I want coercion to fire when types differ? === says no. == says yes, by the rules of the abstract equality algorithm — covered in the Abstract equality cards.

cards.question: How does === work exactly?
cards.answer:
=== is one rule with two steps: if the types differ, return false — done. If the types are the same, compare the values directly.

No coercion ever runs. A number is never equal to a string, and null is never equal to undefined:

1 === "1"            // false — number vs string
null === undefined   // false — different types
5 === 5              // true
"a" === "a"          // true

Two oddities sit inside step 2:

NaN === NaN is false. NaN is, by IEEE-754, not equal to anything — including itself. To detect NaN, use Number.isNaN(x), not a comparison.

+0 === -0 is true. Signed zero is collapsed: === treats the two zeros as the same value.

NaN === NaN   // false
+0 === -0     // true

These two corners are exactly where Object.is and SameValueZero diverge from === — covered in their own cards in this topic.

cards.question: How does === compare objects?
cards.answer:
For objects, "compare values" means reference equality: two operands are equal only if they are the same object in memory. Same shape is not enough.

({}) === ({})       // false — two different objects
[1, 2] === [1, 2]   // false — two different arrays

const a = {};
a === a             // true  — same reference
const b = a;
a === b             // true  — b points to the same object

This is not special behavior of === — an object's "value" simply is its reference. == compares objects the same way when both sides are objects.

Deep structural equality — "do these two objects have the same contents?" — has no operator in the language. You write it yourself or use a library like lodash.isEqual.

cards.question: What algorithm runs for a == b?
cards.answer:
If the types match, == behaves exactly like ===. If they differ, it coerces one side by a fixed rule and re-runs — checking the rules in strict priority order.

#CaseWhat happens
1Same typeBehave exactly like ===
2null / undefinedEqual to each other and to nothing else — no coercion
3Number vs stringToNumber the string, re-run
4Boolean on either sideToNumber the boolean — always, never ToString — re-run
5Object vs primitiveToPrimitive the object with default hint, re-run
6Anything elsefalse

cards.question: In a == b, does the boolean side coerce first?
cards.answer:
Yes — the boolean side moves first (step 4), regardless of what is on the other side.

"true" == true;   // false
// true   → 1      (boolean rule, step 4)
// "true" → NaN    (string rule, step 3)
// NaN == 1        → false

A boolean is only ever coerced to a number under == — never to a string.

cards.question: Walk through [] == false.
cards.answer:
[] == false is true. The boolean rule fires first, then the object rule, then the string rule — and both ends meet at the number 0.

[] == false
// 1. Boolean rule: false → 0           ⇒  [] == 0
// 2. Object rule: ToPrimitive([])
//    valueOf() returns the array itself (not a primitive)
//    → fall through to toString() → ""  ⇒  "" == 0
// 3. String rule: "" → 0                ⇒  0 == 0
// 4. Same type → true

The key insight: [] does not go directly to 0. It goes [] → "" → 0 — two coercion steps. The intermediate "" is what unifies the chain 0 == "" == [] == false: four different inputs collapsing through different paths to the number 0.

cards.question: Walk [1, 2] == "1,2" and [] == ![].
cards.answer:
Both are true. The first is a single object-rule step; the second is [] == false in disguise, because ! evaluates first.

[1, 2] == "1,2"
// Object rule: ToPrimitive([1, 2])
// valueOf() returns the array → fall through to toString() → "1,2"
// "1,2" == "1,2" — same type → true

Note [1, 2].toString() is "1,2" with no space. [1, 2] == "1, 2" is false — the strings differ at the same-type compare.

[] == ![]
// ! evaluates FIRST: [] is truthy (objects are not on
// the falsy list), so ![] is false
[] == false
// ...exactly the [] == false walk → true

The "WTF" framing here is misleading. Two separate, ordinary things happen: ! is a normal unary operator that runs first, and == does its normal coercion dance. Neither is unusual on its own.

cards.question: When is == fine to use, and when should you avoid it?
cards.answer:
== is fine in two cases: x == null (catches null and undefined, nothing else — idiomatic) and when both sides are the same type (mechanically identical to ===, pure style).

Avoid it only when either side could be true, false, 0, "", or [] — their coercion paths intersect at 0 and produce the surprises.

[] == false;   // true
"" == 0;       // true
0 == "0";      // true

Deeper principle: the danger isn't the operator — it's not knowing the types. With TypeScript the types are visible and the debate evaporates.

cards.question: How many equality semantics does JavaScript have?
cards.answer:
Four, not two: Abstract Equality (==), Strict Equality (===), SameValueZero, and SameValue. They differ in exactly two corners — how they treat NaN, and whether they distinguish +0 from -0.

SemanticUsed byNaN equals NaN?+0 equals -0?Coerces across types?
Abstract Equality==noyesyes
Strict Equality===noyesno
SameValueZeroincludes, Map, Setyesyesno
SameValueObject.is, internal spec opsyesnono

Knowing which semantic an API uses matters: the same lookup can fail under one and succeed under another — the SameValueZero card shows this with indexOf vs includes. Why NaN !== NaN and what -0 is are covered in the Types topic; the == coercion algorithm has its own cards in this topic.

cards.question: What is Object.is?
cards.answer:
Object.is(a, b) is === with two surgical changes: Object.is(NaN, NaN) is true, and Object.is(+0, -0) is false. The spec calls this semantic SameValue.

Object.is(NaN, NaN);     // true   — useful for NaN detection
Object.is(+0, -0);       // false  — signed zero is distinguished
Object.is({}, {});       // false  — still reference equality for objects

For objects nothing changes: two values are equal only if they are the same object in memory, exactly like ===.

cards.question: Why use Object.is at the leaves of a deep-equal?
cards.answer:
Because NaN === NaN is false, so a naive === base case reports { x: NaN } and { x: NaN } as unequal. Object.is is NaN-safe and fixes that.

function deepEqual(a, b) {
  if (Object.is(a, b)) return true;   // base case — NaN-safe
  // ...otherwise recurse into the keys of a and b...
}

deepEqual({ x: NaN }, { x: NaN });    // true — with ===, false

cards.question: What is SameValueZero, and where is it used?
cards.answer: SameValueZero is the equality of collection lookup — Array.prototype.includes, Map.prototype.has, Set.prototype.has. It is === with one change: NaN equals itself. Signed zero stays collapsed — +0 and -0 count as the same value, just like === and unlike Object.is.

cards.question: Why does includes(NaN) work but indexOf(NaN) doesn't?
cards.answer:
indexOf uses === (and NaN !== NaN), while includes uses SameValueZero (where NaN equals itself).

[1, NaN, 3].indexOf(NaN);    // -1     — indexOf uses ===
[1, NaN, 3].includes(NaN);   // true   — includes uses SameValueZero
new Set([NaN, NaN]).size;    // 1      — Set de-dupes with SameValueZero

Same array, two lookup methods, two answers.

cards.question: Why do collections need SameValueZero instead of == or ===?
cards.answer:
Collections need their equality to be a real equivalence relation — reflexive, symmetric, transitive — to guarantee no duplicate keys or elements. Neither operator qualifies:

OperatorFails because
==Not transitive — 0 == "", 0 == "0", but "" != "0"; a Set couldn't keep its uniqueness invariant
===Not reflexive for NaN (NaN === NaN is false); a Set couldn't tell if it already holds NaN

SameValueZero is the minimum fix that keeps both invariants — which is why new Set([NaN, NaN]) has size 1.

cards.question: How do null and undefined behave under ==?
cards.answer:
Step 2 of the == algorithm is a hard-coded special case: null and undefined are equal to each other and to nothing else. No coercion happens.

null == undefined;   // true  — the special case
null == 0;           // false
null == false;       // false
null == "";          // false
undefined == 0;      // false

This is the only special case among the commonly coerced types: number, string, boolean and object mismatches all get converted and re-compared, but here the result is hard-wired — the two nullish values match each other, and any other pairing is false.

So null never becomes 0, "", or false under ==, even though Number(null) is 0. The conversion rules from the Coercion topic simply do not apply to this pair.

Under === the special case disappears: null === undefined is false — different types.

cards.question: Why is null >= 0 true but null > 0 and null == 0 both false?
cards.answer:
Because == and the relational operators (<, >, <=, >=) follow different rules. Relational operators convert null to the number 0 and compare; == never coerces null — it only matches undefined.

null >= 0;   // true  — null → 0, then 0 >= 0
null > 0;    // false — null → 0, then 0 > 0
null == 0;   // false — special case: null only equals undefined

The trap is expecting the three results to agree. They come from two unrelated algorithms: comparison runs a numeric conversion, equality runs the hard-coded nullish rule from the previous card. So null is "greater than or equal to" 0 without being "equal to" it.

The practical rule: do not compare a value that might be null or undefined with <, >, <=, >=. Check for nullish first with x == null, then compare.

cards.question: Why is x == null the canonical nullish check?
cards.answer:
x == null is true exactly when x is null or undefined — and for nothing else. One short expression, two values caught, zero false positives.

if (user.address == null) {
  // catches both null and undefined
  // does NOT catch 0, "", false, NaN
}

It works because of the hard-coded special case: null and undefined match each other under == and nothing else. Other falsy values are not caught — 0 == null, "" == null, false == null, NaN == null are all false. That makes it strictly safer than a truthiness check like if (!x).

The strict equivalent is x === null || x === undefined — longer, identical in behavior. ESLint's eqeqeq rule has a null exception ("always", { "null": "ignore" }) for exactly this idiom.

This is the one case where senior reviewers expect == and would push back on ===. Don't expand it to the strict version unless a linter forces you to.

cards.question: Does JavaScript really run top to bottom?
cards.answer:
No — the engine makes two passes. Compile pass: parse the whole file and register every declaration (var, let, const, function, parameters) in its scope — no assignments run. Execute pass: run line by line; assignments happen and lookups resolve against the scopes from pass 1.

So where every variable lives is fixed at parse time, and that decision is permanent.

var a = 2;
// compile pass:  register `a` in this scope
// execute pass:  a = 2

This split is exactly why hoisting works — declarations are handled up front, assignments stay in place (see Hoisting & TDZ).

cards.question: What does it mean that JavaScript is lexically scoped?
cards.answer:
"Lexical" means relating to the source text: a function's outer scope is the scope it is physically written inside, decided at parse time. That bond is permanent and never changes based on who calls the function.

Lexical scope = nesting in the source text, not nesting in the call stack.

cards.question: Does the call site affect which outer variable a function sees?
cards.answer:
No. Lookup follows where the function is written, not where it is called — the call stack plays no part.

function foo() {
  console.log(a);   // which `a`?
}
function bar() {
  var a = 2;
  foo();            // foo is CALLED here, where a === 2
}
var a = 1;          // foo is WRITTEN here, next to a === 1
bar();              // logs 1

foo looks in its outer scope — the global scope, where it is written — so it finds a = 1. That bar called it is invisible.

To make foo log 2, move its declaration inside bar — change the writing, change the lookup.

cards.question: What is dynamic scope, and does JavaScript have it?
cards.answer:
Dynamic scope resolves variable names by following the call stack at runtime, so the same function could see different variables depending on who called it. JavaScript deliberately does not have it.

Bash does (so do some older Lisps). The same shape that logs 1 in JavaScript prints 2 in Bash, because foo looks up a in its caller:

# Bash — dynamic scope
foo() { echo "$a"; }
bar() { local a=2; foo; }
a=1
bar    # prints 2 — lookup followed the call stack

In JavaScript, lookup follows source nesting only: foo written at global scope sees the global a, no matter where it's called from.

The one JS mechanism that is call-site-dependent is this — which is exactly why people confuse this with scope. See the this & Binding topic.

cards.question: How does a variable lookup travel?
cards.answer:
Scopes nest into a chain. A lookup starts in the current scope and bubbles outward — inner, enclosing, …, global — stopping at the first match.

const tier = "global";

function outer() {
  const tier = "outer";
  function inner() {
    console.log(tier);   // "outer"
  }
  inner();
}
outer();

inner has no tier of its own, so the lookup moves one scope out, finds tier = "outer", and stops. The global tier is never reached — a nearer match hides a farther one. That hiding is shadowing, covered in its own subtopic.

If the lookup reaches the global scope and still finds nothing, the chain is exhausted and a read fails with ReferenceError: x is not defined. The exact failure rules — and how assignment behaves differently — are in LHS vs RHS.

cards.question: What sits at the top of the scope chain?
cards.answer:
The global scope — it is the last link in the chain. Every lookup that finds no nearer match ends there.

If the name is found in the global scope, the lookup succeeds. If even the global scope has no match, the chain is exhausted — and what happens next depends on whether you were reading or assigning (see LHS vs RHS).

const top = "global";

function f() {
  console.log(top);     // found at the top of the chain
  console.log(nope);    // chain exhausted → ReferenceError
}
f();

What actually lives in the global scope — the global object, window properties, Node's module scope — is covered in the Global Scope & var topic.

cards.question: What are LHS and RHS references?
cards.answer:
Every variable reference is one of two kinds: an LHS reference is a target — the variable is being assigned to (x = 5); an RHS reference is a source — its value is being retrieved (console.log(x)).

let x;
x = 5;            // LHS — value goes INTO x (target)
console.log(x);   // RHS — value is read OUT of x (5)

The names come from the sides of =, but only loosely — console.log(x) has no = and is still RHS. The real test: is a value going into the variable, or coming out?

cards.question: Is a function parameter an LHS or RHS reference?
cards.answer:
A parameter is an LHS — the argument is assigned into it when the function is called.

function square(n) {  // n is LHS — argument assigned into n
  return n * n;       // n here is RHS — its value is read
}
square(4);            // returns 16

The LHS/RHS distinction decides what happens when a scope-chain lookup finds nothing: an error, or an accidental global.

cards.question: What happens when a variable lookup fails?
cards.answer:
The kind of reference decides the failure.

ReferenceSloppy modeStrict mode
RHS (reading undeclared)ReferenceErrorReferenceError
LHS (assigning undeclared)silently creates a globalReferenceError
function f() {
  leak = 10;        // LHS, never declared → creates a GLOBAL (sloppy)
}
f();
console.log(leak);  // 10 — leaked out of the function

That sloppy-mode accidental-global footgun is why a forgotten let leaks a global. Strict mode closes it — a failed LHS throws instead — and ES modules and class bodies are strict by default.

cards.question: What is hoisting, mechanically?
cards.answer: Not the engine moving lines to the top. It is the visible consequence of the compile pass: every declaration is registered in its scope before any code runs, so the names already exist when execution starts.

cards.question: Why does reading a var before its assignment give undefined, not an error?
cards.answer:
A var is created and initialized to undefined at the start of its scope. The assignment stays where you wrote it and runs only when execution reaches that line — so declarations hoist, assignments do not.

console.log(score); // undefined — declared, not yet assigned
var score = 42;
console.log(score); // 42

cards.question: Can you call a function declaration before the line it appears on?
cards.answer:
Yes — function declarations hoist complete: the name and the full body are registered together, so the function is callable before its line.

greet(); // "hi" — the whole function is ready before this line

function greet() {
  console.log("hi");
}

cards.question: What is the temporal dead zone (TDZ)?
cards.answer:
The stretch from the start of a scope to the let/const declaration line, during which the variable exists but is uninitialized — any access throws ReferenceError.

console.log(user); // ReferenceError: Cannot access 'user' before initialization
let user = "Ann";

The error reads "cannot access before initialization" (not "not defined") — the variable exists, just in its dead zone. It's temporal: about execution time, not text position. Code above may mention it, as long as it runs after the declaration:

function read() { return token; }  // mentions token before its line — fine
let token = "abc";
read(); // "abc" — by call time the declaration has run

cards.question: Does the TDZ block reaching an outer variable of the same name?
cards.answer:
Yes. The inner variable is registered for its whole scope, so its TDZ shadows the outer one even on lines above the declaration.

let x = 1;
function func() {
  console.log(x); // ReferenceError — the inner x exists here, but is in its TDZ
  let x = 2;
}
func();

cards.question: Why does the language have a TDZ at all?
cards.answer:
With var, use-before-declaration silently produced undefined and the bug surfaced far away. The TDZ turns it into a loud error at the exact line.

It also makes const coherent: a const read before its line can never have a value — initializing to undefined then assigning later would mean a constant with two observable values.

cards.question: What hoists to where, and in what state?
cards.answer:
Each declaration form hoists to a different scope and arrives in a different state.

DeclarationHoists toState at scope startEarly access
var xnearest function/global — blocks ignoredundefinedundefined
let x / const xits blockuninitialized (TDZ)ReferenceError
function f(){}its scope, wholethe function itselfworks — callable
var f = function(){}only the variable fundefinedcalling: TypeError

cards.question: Does var respect block scope like let/const?
cards.answer:
No — var ignores blocks entirely; an if or for body does not contain it, the nearest function does. let/const stay inside the block.

function run() {
  if (true) {
    var v = 1;
    let l = 2;
  }
  console.log(v); // 1 — var ignored the block, lives in the function
  console.log(l); // ReferenceError: l is not defined
}
run();

cards.question: What is shadowing?
cards.answer:
When an inner scope declares a variable with the same name as an outer one, the inner declaration shadows the outer: inside that scope, the name refers to the inner variable only.

let answer = "outer";
function show() {
  let answer = "inner";   // shadows the outer `answer`
  console.log(answer);    // "inner"
}
show();                   // "inner"
console.log(answer);      // "outer" — outer still exists, untouched

It falls out of how lookup works — the search walks outward and stops at the first match, so the outer variable is untouched, just unreachable from inside.

cards.question: Do function parameters shadow outer variables?
cards.answer:
Yes — parameters are declarations too, so they shadow the same way.

let name = "global";
function greet(name) {    // the parameter shadows the outer `name`
  console.log(name);
}
greet("param");           // "param"

cards.question: Why is shadowing legal but redeclaring let in the same scope isn't?
cards.answer:
A scope boundary makes it a different variable. Shadowing across any boundary is legal — even let shadowing an outer var — but redeclaring with let in the same scope is a SyntaxError.

var count = 1;
{
  let count = 2;          // legal: different scope
  console.log(count);     // 2
}
console.log(count);       // 1

cards.question: Can you ever reach a shadowed variable?
cards.answer:
Normally no. There is no syntax for "the x one scope up" — lookup always stops at the nearest match.

let x = "outer";

function f() {
  let x = "inner";
  console.log(x);   // "inner" — no way to name the outer x from here
}
f();

The one narrow escape hatch — a shadowed global var reachable as window.x in browsers — is covered in the Global Scope & var topic.

In practice, needing the shadowed variable is a sign to rename: shadowing makes the same name mean different things a few lines apart. It's enough of a readability hazard that many teams ban it with the no-shadow ESLint rule.

cards.question: What is an execution context?
cards.answer:
When a function (or the top-level script) starts running, the engine creates a "box" for that run — holding everything the code needs:

  • the scope's local variables (its environment record),
  • the value of this,
  • a link to where the function was written ([[Environment]] — the scope chain follows this).

Every call gets a fresh box; when the call returns, the box is gone (popped off the call stack).

cards.question: How many kinds of execution context are there, and is one created per function or per call?
cards.answer:
Three kinds: one global context for top-level code, a new context for every function call, and a context for eval. It's per call, not per function — three calls make three contexts, each with its own fresh locals.

function count() {
  var n = 0;        // a fresh `n` in this call's context
  n++;
  return n;
}
count(); // 1
count(); // 1 — new call, new context, new `n`

cards.question: What are the two phases of an execution context?
cards.answer:
PhaseWhat happens
CreationThe environment record is set up: every declaration in the scope is registered, and this is determined.
ExecutionThe code runs line by line; assignments happen here.

The creation phase is the per-call counterpart of the compile pass — names exist before the first line runs, which is why hoisting works.

console.log(x);   // undefined — `x` registered in the creation phase
var x = 5;        // the assignment waits for the execution phase

cards.question: What is the relationship between the call stack and execution contexts?
cards.answer: The call stack is the stack of execution contexts: the global context sits at the bottom, every call pushes a new context on top, every return pops one off. The context on top is the one currently running.

cards.question: How do execution contexts relate to scope, and what is [[Environment]]?
cards.answer:
Each context holds an environment record — where that scope's variables live — and the scope chain is a chain of these records.

The chain's shape is fixed lexically: every function carries a hidden [[Environment]] reference to where it was written, set once at creation. So lookup follows the source text (own record → outer → … → global), never the call stack.

function foo() { console.log(a); }
function bar() {
  var a = 2;
  foo();   // bar's context is on the stack — but not in foo's chain
}
var a = 1;
bar();     // 1

Contexts and their records are created and destroyed with calls; the lexical structure they plug into is fixed at parse time and never changes.

cards.question: What is the difference between a function declaration and a function expression?
cards.answer:
A function declaration is a standalone statement that starts with the function keyword and must have a name. A function expression is a function created inside an expression — assigned to a variable, passed as an argument, returned from another function — and its name is optional.

// Function Declaration — a statement of its own
function sum(a, b) {
  return a + b;
}

// Function Expression — the function is a value, assigned to a variable
let mul = function(a, b) {
  return a * b;
};

The syntax of the function itself is the same — what differs is where it sits. A declaration stands on its own; an expression appears where a value is expected, such as the right side of =. How the parser uses position to tell them apart, and why that matters for calling a function in place, is covered in the IIFE subtopic.

cards.question: What does it mean that a function is a value?
cards.answer:
However it's created, a function is a value, like a number or a string — it can be assigned, copied, and printed. The name without parentheses is a reference to the function; only parentheses call it.

function sayHi() {
  console.log("Hello");
}

let func = sayHi;       // no parentheses: copies the function itself
func();                 // Hello — same function, second name

let result = sayHi();   // parentheses: runs sayHi, stores its result — undefined here

console.log(String(sayHi)); // "function sayHi() {...}" — the source code

After let func = sayHi there is one function with two names — both variables point at the same value. And because a function is a value, converting it to a string shows its source code.

The gotcha is the parentheses: sayHi hands over the function, sayHi() hands over whatever it returns. Passing sayHi() where a function is expected is a classic mistake.

cards.question: What is a callback function?
cards.answer:
A callback is a function passed as an argument to another function, expected to be "called back" later when the right moment comes. It works because a function is a value representing an action — handing one over hands over the action, and the receiver decides if and when to run it.

function ask(question, answer, yes, no) {
  console.log(question);
  if (answer) yes();
  else no();
}

function showOk() { console.log("You agreed."); }
function showCancel() { console.log("You canceled."); }

// showOk and showCancel are callbacks
ask("Do you agree?", true, showOk, showCancel);
// Do you agree?
// You agreed.

cards.question: Why does the receiving function not need to know what the callback does?
cards.answer:
The caller supplies the actions as values; the receiver only picks which one to invoke. ask doesn't know what agreeing or canceling should mean — it just calls one of the functions back.

In a browser, the answer would come from confirm(question) asking a real user, while the yes/no handlers stay entirely up to the caller.

cards.question: Do callbacks need to be named functions?
cards.answer:
No — a function expression can be written inline right inside the call, created on the spot and unreachable outside it.

ask(
  "Do you agree?", false,
  function() { console.log("You agreed."); },
  function() { console.log("You canceled."); }
);
// Do you agree?
// You canceled.

This anonymous inline form is the everyday shape of callbacks: array methods like [1, 2, 3].map(n => n * 2), timers, and event handlers all take them.

cards.question: When is a function declaration created vs a function expression?
cards.answer:
A declaration is created during the engine's compile pass, so it's usable anywhere in its scope — even above its line. An expression is created only when execution reaches it.

sayHi("John");   // "Hello, John" — already created

function sayHi(name) {
  console.log(`Hello, ${name}`);
}

cards.question: What happens if you call a function expression before its line?
cards.answer:
The function doesn't exist yet. With var, the variable exists but holds undefined — calling it throws TypeError.

sayBye("John");   // TypeError: sayBye is not a function

var sayBye = function(name) {
  console.log(`Bye, ${name}`);
};

With let/const the early call throws ReferenceError instead — the variable isn't initialized yet (see Hoisting & TDZ).

cards.question: Is a function declaration inside a block visible outside it?
cards.answer:
In strict mode, no — a declaration inside a block is visible only inside that block.

"use strict";

if (true) {
  function hi() { console.log("hi"); }
  hi();   // works — visible inside the block
}

hi();   // ReferenceError: hi is not defined

Outside strict mode it leaks into the enclosing function scope — a legacy quirk, don't rely on it. To use a function outside its block, assign an expression to a variable declared outside the block.

cards.question: Why does a function expression end with a semicolon but a declaration doesn't?
cards.answer:
An expression assignment is an ordinary statement, like let x = 5;, so it ends with ;. A declaration is not an assignment — no semicolon needed.

The semicolon terminates the assignment statement, not the function syntax itself.

cards.question: Should you default to function declarations or expressions?
cards.answer:
Default to declarations. Reach for an expression only when the function is genuinely a value: created conditionally, passed inline, or stored in a data structure.

Declarations win on readability — function calcRent(...) is easier to spot than let calcRent = function(...) — and they're callable anywhere in their scope, so you can put main logic at the top of a file and helpers below.

cards.question: When does a function expression beat a declaration?
cards.answer:
When creation depends on a condition — a declaration can't do this (in strict mode it stays trapped inside the branch's block).

let age = 16;

let welcome = (age < 18)
  ? function() { console.log("Hello!"); }
  : function() { console.log("Greetings!"); };

welcome();   // "Hello!"

The other natural homes for expressions: a callback passed straight into another function, and a function stored as an object property or array element. For short inline callbacks an arrow is usual — [1, 2, 3].map(n => n * 2).

cards.question: What is a named function expression (NFE)?
cards.answer: A function expression with its own internal name: let sayHi = function func(who) {...}. The name func is visible only inside the function body, and there it always refers to the current function.

cards.question: Why use the internal NFE name instead of the outer variable to self-call?
cards.answer:
The internal name is bound to the function itself, so it survives even if the outer variable is reassigned or nulled.

let sayHi = function func(who) {
  if (who) {
    console.log(`Hello, ${who}`);
  } else {
    func("Guest"); // always the current function
  }
};

let welcome = sayHi;
sayHi = null;

welcome(); // Hello, Guest

Had the body called sayHi("Guest"), it would fail here — by the time it runs, the outer sayHi is null: TypeError: sayHi is not a function.

cards.question: Is the NFE internal name visible outside the function?
cards.answer:
No — outside the body the name does not exist; func() there is a ReferenceError.

This internal-only name is a feature of function expressions. For a function declaration, the name is just a regular variable in the enclosing scope.

cards.question: What are the name and length properties of a function?
cards.answer: Every function has two built-in properties: name — the function's name as a string — and length — the number of declared parameters.

cards.question: How does an anonymous function expression get a name?
cards.answer:
The engine takes a "contextual name" from the assignment context — the variable or property it's assigned to. An NFE's own name wins over context.

let f = function() {};
