console.log(f.name); // "f" — from the variable

let user = { sayBye: function() {} };
console.log(user.sayBye.name); // "sayBye" — from the property

let greet = function inner() {};
console.log(greet.name); // "inner" — the NFE name wins

This shows up in stack traces and debuggers. When there's no context to take a name from — e.g. a function created directly inside an array literal — name is an empty string.

cards.question: Does function.length count rest parameters?
cards.answer:
No — length counts only declared parameters; rest parameters are not counted.

function f1(a) {}
function many(a, b, ...more) {}

console.log(f1.length); // 1
console.log(many.length); // 2

cards.question: Can a function carry custom properties?
cards.answer:
Yes. A function is an object, so you can attach your own properties — sayHi.counter = 0 — and they live on the function like properties on any object.

function sayHi() {
  console.log("Hi");
  sayHi.counter++;   // state stored on the function itself
}
sayHi.counter = 0;

sayHi();
sayHi();

console.log(`Called ${sayHi.counter} times`); // Called 2 times

cards.question: Is a function property the same as a variable inside the function?
cards.answer: No — sayHi.counter and a let counter in the body are unrelated: one is a property on the object, the other a variable in the scope.

cards.question: Function property vs closure for storing state — which to pick?
cards.answer:
The difference is access: a variable trapped in a closure is private, while a property is reachable from outside — any code can read or change sayHi.counter.

Pick a property when external access is wanted, a closure when the state must stay hidden.

cards.question: What are arrow functions?
cards.answer:
An arrow function is a shorter syntax for a function expression: (args) => expression creates a function that evaluates the expression and implicitly returns its result.

The syntax adapts to the parameter count and the body:

let sum = (a, b) => a + b;   // expression body: returns a + b implicitly
let double = n => n * 2;     // one parameter: parens optional
let sayHi = () => "Hi";      // zero parameters: parens required

let pow = (a, b) => {        // braces: statement body
  let result = a ** b;
  return result;             // explicit return needed
};

Arrows are always expressions — there is no arrow declaration. One is created when execution reaches it, and you use it through a variable or pass it inline. That makes them a natural fit for short callbacks:

[1, 2, 3].map(n => n * 2);   // [2, 4, 6]

cards.question: What can arrow functions not do?
cards.answer:
Four things: an arrow function has no own this, no arguments object, cannot be called with new, and has no own super.

No own this. An arrow uses the this of the scope it is written in — the full story is in the this & Binding topic. Because of this, arrows are usually wrong as object methods: the method's this will not be the object it is called on.

No arguments object. Use a rest parameter instead:

const max = (...args) => Math.max(...args);
max(1, 5, 3);   // 5

Not a constructor. An arrow has no prototype property and throws when called with new:

const f = () => {};
f.prototype;    // undefined — a regular function would have one
new f();        // TypeError: f is not a constructor

No own super. Like this, it is taken from the enclosing scope.

cards.question: What is an IIFE and why does it need parentheses?
cards.answer: An IIFE (Immediately Invoked Function Expression) is a function expression created and called in one go. The wrapping parentheses force the engine to read it as an expression — at statement position the function keyword would start a declaration instead.

cards.question: Why can't you just write function(){}() to invoke immediately?
cards.answer:
A Function Declaration must have a name and can't be called in place, so both bare attempts are syntax errors.

function() { ... }();     // SyntaxError: a declaration needs a name
function go() { ... }();   // SyntaxError: the trailing () parses on its own

cards.question: What forms can put a function in expression position for an IIFE?
cards.answer:
Anything that forces expression position works — parens are the convention, but a leading unary operator or the arrow form do too.

(function() { console.log("a"); })();   // a — parens around the function
(function() { console.log("b"); }());   // b — parens around the whole call
!function() { console.log("c"); }();    // c — ! starts an expression
+function() { console.log("d"); }();    // d — so does unary +
(() => { console.log("e"); })();        // e — arrow form

This is the same statement-vs-expression parsing rule that makes {} + [] behave oddly (see Coercion).

cards.question: What were IIFEs originally for?
cards.answer:
Pre-ES6 scope isolation: var has no block scope, so wrapping code in a function was the only way to keep variables private and — in browsers — off the global object. This trick is the seed of the module pattern.

(function() {
  var message = "hidden";
  console.log(message);   // "hidden"
})();
console.log(message);     // ReferenceError: message is not defined

cards.question: What replaces IIFEs for scope isolation today?
cards.answer:
let/const blocks and ES modules replace almost all of it — a plain block isolates just as well, and every ES module gets its own scope for free.

{
  let message = "hidden";
}
// message is not visible here

cards.question: What legitimate uses do IIFEs still have?
cards.answer:
Two. One-shot initialization — run statements right where a const is assigned:

const squares = (() => {
  const map = new Map();
  for (let i = 1; i <= 3; i++) map.set(i, i * i);
  return map;
})();
squares.get(3); // 9

And the async IIFE — the way to use await in a non-module script, since top-level await works only in modules:

(async () => {
  const data = await Promise.resolve("loaded");
  console.log(data); // "loaded"
})();

cards.question: What creates a block scope?
cards.answer:
Any code block { ... } creates a block scope: if/else bodies, loop bodies, try/catch, even a standalone block. let, const, and class declarations are confined to the nearest block; var ignores blocks entirely.

if (true) {
  let secret = 1;
  var leak = 2;
}

leak;   // 2 — var ignored the block and attached to the function (or global) scope
secret; // ReferenceError: secret is not defined

var respects only function boundaries — a block is invisible to it. That behavior, and the pre-let IIFE workaround, are covered in The old var in the Global Scope & var topic.

A standalone block is a legitimate tool: wrap a few lines in bare braces to keep temporaries from leaking into the surrounding scope.

{
  let total = 2 + 3;
  console.log(total); // 5
}
// total is gone — no leftover name out here

cards.question: How does for (let ...) scope each iteration?
cards.answer:
for (let i = ...) creates a new binding of i for every iteration — each turn of the loop gets its own copy of the variable. for (var i = ...) creates a single binding shared by all iterations.

let fns = [];
for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) fns.push(() => i);
fns.map(f => f()); // [0, 1, 2] — each function holds its own i

for (var j = 0; j < 3; j++) setTimeout(() => console.log(j)); // 3, 3, 3 — one shared j

Between iterations the engine copies the latest value into the fresh binding, so i++ still counts normally — the loop works as if there were one variable, but each iteration's functions see a private snapshot.

The header let belongs to the loop itself: after the loop, i is gone (ReferenceError), while var j survives with its final value 3.

Why the var version prints 3, 3, 3 — functions capturing the variable, not the value — is covered in the Closure topic.

cards.question: What is the global object?
cards.answer: The object whose properties are available everywhere without any import: built-ins like Array and JSON, plus environment-specific members like document and innerHeight in a browser, where it's named window.

cards.question: In a browser classic script, which top-level declarations become properties of window?
cards.answer:
var and function declarations become properties of window. let/const are still global in scope but live in a separate record, not on window.

// browser, classic <script> (not a module)
var test = 5;
let user = "John";

alert(window.test); // 5 — var became a window property
alert(window.user); // undefined — let did not
alert(user);        // "John" — global in scope, just not on window

cards.question: Do top-level declarations in an ES module reach the global object?
cards.answer: No. In an ES module, top-level var and function declarations stay module-scoped and never reach the global object. The var-becomes-a-property behavior matters mainly for old non-module scripts.

cards.question: In Node.js (CommonJS), is top-level var a property of global?
cards.answer:
No — it's module-scoped. Node wraps every file in a function, so its "top level" is really a function body.

// app.cjs
var x = 5;
console.log(global.x); // undefined — x belongs to this module

cards.question: When is it legitimate to use the global object?
cards.answer: Sparingly — keep globals rare. The legitimate uses are feature detection with polyfills, making an intentional cross-script value explicit, and safely reading a global that may not exist. For everything else, prefer modules and explicit imports.

cards.question: How do you use the global object for feature detection and polyfills?
cards.answer:
Built-in APIs live on the global object, so checking it tells you whether the environment supports a feature — and assigning to it fills the gap.

if (!globalThis.myApi) {
  globalThis.myApi = { ready: true };
}

console.log(myApi.ready); // true — global-object properties are readable by bare name

cards.question: How do you make a global value intentional rather than accidental?
cards.answer:
Write it as an explicit property of the global object instead of a bare variable — it makes the intent visible: global on purpose.

window.currentUser = { name: "John" };

// any other script:
alert(currentUser.name); // John

cards.question: How do you reach a global variable that a local variable shadows?
cards.answer:
Use property access: a bare name finds the nearest declaration, but window.x asks the global object directly, skipping every local.

var greeting = "global hi"; // becomes window.greeting

function show() {
  let greeting = "local hi";
  alert(greeting);        // local hi — nearest declaration
  alert(window.greeting); // global hi — straight to window
}
show();

cards.question: How do you safely read a global that may not exist?
cards.answer:
Go through the global object: a missing property is just undefined, while a bare read of an undeclared name throws. (typeof analytics is the other safe read.)

alert(globalThis.analytics); // undefined — no error
alert(analytics);            // ReferenceError: analytics is not defined

cards.question: What is globalThis?
cards.answer:
globalThis is the standardized name for the global object, added in ES2020. It is the same name in every JavaScript environment.

Historically each environment named its global object differently, so cross-environment code had to guess which one existed:

EnvironmentGlobal object name
Browserwindow
Web workerself
Node.jsglobal
Everywhere (ES2020)globalThis

All the names point at the same object in their environment — globalThis is just the portable alias:

globalThis.answer = 42;

console.log(globalThis.answer);     // 42
console.log(globalThis === global); // true (in Node)
console.log(globalThis === window); // true (in a browser)

Use globalThis when code must run across environments — a library loaded in browsers, workers, and Node. In code that only ever runs in one environment, the local name (window in browser scripts) is still fine.

cards.question: How is var scoped?
cards.answer:
var has no block scope: a var is either function-scoped or global. Blocks like if, for and while do not contain it.

Declare a var inside a block and it leaks into the enclosing function — or becomes global if there is no function around it:

if (true) {
  var test = true;
}
console.log(test); // true — var ignored the block

With let test the last line would throw ReferenceError: test is not defined, because let respects the block.

Loops are blocks too, so the counter survives them:

for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {}
console.log(i); // 3

The only wall a var cannot cross is a function. Declared anywhere inside a function — even deep inside nested blocks — it is visible everywhere in that function, but not outside it.

cards.question: How does var hoist within its function?
cards.answer:
The var declaration is processed at the start of its function and begins as undefined; the assignment stays where it's written. So reading it before the assignment line gives undefined, not an error.

function sayHi() {
  console.log(phrase); // undefined — declared, not yet assigned
  var phrase = "Hello";
  console.log(phrase); // Hello
}
sayHi();

cards.question: What happens when you redeclare a var with the same name?
cards.answer:
It's silently ignored — the second var acts as a plain assignment, no error.

var user = "Pete";
var user = "John"; // no error
console.log(user); // John

With let, the second declaration throws SyntaxError: Identifier 'user' has already been declared.

cards.question: Why are var's hoisting and redeclaration behaviors considered bug-prone?
cards.answer: Both hide bugs: a read placed too early quietly gives undefined, and a duplicate declaration quietly overwrites an existing variable. let/const turn both into immediate errors you see right away.

cards.question: Why does var exist, and should you use it?
cards.answer:
var is the original declaration from the first versions of JavaScript; let and const replaced it in 2015 (ES6). It survives only for backward compatibility with old code.

Its lack of block scope shaped old code style. Since the only thing that contains a var is a function, programmers wrapped code in a function and called it immediately — the IIFE. The pattern and its mechanics are covered in the IIFE subtopic of the Function Expressions topic; with block-scoped let/const, a plain { } block does the same job.

The modern rule:

const limit = 10;   // default
let count = 0;      // only when reassigned
count++;
// var — only when maintaining legacy code

In new code there is no case where var is the better choice.

cards.question: What is a closure?
cards.answer:
A closure is a function that remembers its outer variables and can access them — it carries its birthplace scope with it.

It isn't a special type of function you turn on — it happens automatically whenever a function uses a variable from outside itself.

It reads those variables from where it was created, not from where it's called.

cards.question: Are all JavaScript functions closures?
cards.answer: Yes — all functions are naturally closures, with one exception: functions made with new Function.

cards.question: How does a closure keep a value between calls
cards.answer:
The returned inner function is the closure — it holds a reference to the outer variable, so the variable stays alive and each call reads and updates the same one.

function makeCounter() {
  let count = 0;
  return function () { return count++; };  // ← this inner function is the closure
}

const a = makeCounter();
a(); // 0  — same count, remembered
a(); // 1  — and updated on the next call

cards.question: If you make two counters, do they share the same count?
cards.answer:
No — each call to makeCounter() creates a fresh, independent count.

const a = makeCounter();
const b = makeCounter();

a(); // 0
a(); // 1   — a has its own count
b(); // 0   — b starts fresh, untouched by a

cards.question: Why does a closure keep working after the outer function returns?
cards.answer:
When a function returns, its stack frame is removed and its variables are normally garbage-collected — nothing references them. A closure is the exception: the returned inner function keeps a hidden link to those variables, so they stay in memory and stay usable.

function makeCounter() {
  let count = 0;          // closure uses it  → kept alive
  let label = "scratch";  // closure ignores it → collected after return
  return function () { return count++; };  // references count only
}

const a = makeCounter();  // makeCounter returned → stack frame gone
                          // label: nothing references it → garbage-collected
                          // count: closure still holds it → survives
a(); // 0
a(); // 1

cards.question: What is a Lexical Environment?
cards.answer:
A Lexical Environment is the internal "box" that holds the variables of one scope — each function call gets its own.

Every function records a hidden link, called [[Environment]], to the box where it was created. Every function uses these boxes to look up variables — a closure is special only because it keeps an outer box alive after the outer function has returned.

  function                       Lexical Environment (box)
  ┌─────────────────┐            ┌──────────────┐
  │ [[Environment]] ─┼──────────►│  count: 0    │
  └─────────────────┘            └──────────────┘

cards.question: Does a closure copy the outer variables?
cards.answer: No — it keeps its [[Environment]] link and reads the variable through it at call time; nothing is copied.

cards.question: Why can't a function made with new Function see outer variables?
cards.answer:
Because its [[Environment]] is set to the global Lexical Environment, not to the place where it was created. It only sees global variables.

This is the one exception to “all functions are closures”. A regular function records its birthplace in [[Environment]]; a function built with new Function records the global environment instead:

function getFunc() {
  let value = "test";
  let func = new Function('return value;');
  return func;
}

getFunc()(); // ReferenceError: value is not defined

The restriction is deliberate. new Function builds a function from a string, often at runtime, when the surrounding code is long gone — and minifiers (tools like Terser that shrink code by renaming variables) rename local variables, so a string referring to them by name would break anyway. If such a function needs outside data, pass it in as arguments.

cards.question: Does a closure capture the value or the variable?
cards.answer:
The variable (its binding), not a value. The closure reads the variable's current value every time it's called, so a change made after creation is visible.

let name = "John";
function greet() { return "Hi, " + name; }

name = "Pete";   // changed after greet was created
greet();         // "Hi, Pete" — not "John"

cards.question: How do you make a closure freeze a snapshot of a value?
cards.answer:
Copy the value into its own variable at creation time — then the closure captures that copy instead of the changing one.

let x = 1;
const frozen = x;          // copy the current value
const read = () => frozen; // closure tracks frozen, not x

x = 99;                    // x changes, frozen doesn't
read(); // 1  — snapshot frozen

cards.question: Why does each call to the outer function produce independent closure state?
cards.answer:
Because a function's Lexical Environment (its box) is created fresh on each call — not at definition. Every call's inner functions close over a brand-new variable, so their state is independent.

function makeCounter() { let count = 0; return () => count++; }

makeCounter(); // new box { count: 0 }
makeCounter(); // another new box { count: 0 } — unrelated

cards.question: When is a closed-over variable garbage collected?
cards.answer:
A closed-over variable stays alive as long as a reachable closure still references it. It becomes collectable when you drop the last reference to that closure.

let counter = makeCounter(); // count kept alive by counter
counter = null;              // last reference gone → count can be collected

cards.question: How can closures cause memory leaks?
cards.answer:
A long-lived closure keeps its whole environment alive — including big objects it captured but never uses. If it's parked somewhere persistent (event listener, cache, setInterval), that memory is never freed.

function makeHandler() {
  const bigData = new Array(1_000_000).fill('x');
  return function onClick() {
    return 'clicked'; // never touches bigData
  };
}
let handler = makeHandler(); // bigData retained while handler is reachable

cards.question: How do you fix a closure memory leak?
cards.answer:
Remove the reference that keeps the closure alive.

el.removeEventListener('click', handler); // remove listeners
clearInterval(id);                        // clear timers
handler = null;                           // null references
// now the closure and its environment can be collected

cards.question: Don't engines drop unused captured variables?
cards.answer: V8 retains only the variables a closure actually references, so an obviously-unused capture may be dropped — but it's an optimization, not a guarantee. Don't rely on it across engines (and in V8 those variables become unavailable while debugging).

cards.question: What are closures actually used for?
cards.answer:
They let a function remember variables from where it was created, powering: data privacy, function factories, partial application/currying, stateful callbacks, and memoization.

The common thread: an inner function keeps a live reference to its outer scope, so that data survives after the outer function returns.

cards.question: What is a function factory and how does it use closures?
cards.answer:
A function that builds and returns other functions pre-loaded with some values. Each returned function closes over its own captured value, so factories never interfere.

function makeAdder(x) {
  return function (y) {
    return x + y; // x from outer scope
  };
}
const add5 = makeAdder(5);
add5(2);  // 7
add5(10); // 15
makeAdder(100)(1); // 101

cards.question: How do closures give you private state?
cards.answer:
Variables declared in an outer function are unreachable from outside — only the functions it returns can read or write them, making them effectively private. This was the pre-class way to get private fields, and is the basis of the module pattern.

function makeCounter() {
  let count = 0; // private
  return {
    increment() { count++; },
    decrement() { count--; },
    value() { return count; },
  };
}
const counter = makeCounter();
counter.increment();
counter.increment();
counter.value(); // 2
counter.count;   // undefined — not a property

cards.question: How do closures enable memoization?
cards.answer:
A wrapper function closes over a cache and returns stored results instead of recomputing them.

function memoize(fn) {
  const cache = {};                 // lives in the closure
  return (n) => (n in cache ? cache[n] : (cache[n] = fn(n)));
}

cards.question: Why does a for loop with var and setTimeout print the same number?
cards.answer:
var has one function-scoped binding shared by every iteration, and the deferred callbacks all run after the loop ends — by then that single i holds its final value, 3. The closures captured the variable, not a per-iteration snapshot.

for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
  setTimeout(() => console.log(i));
}
// 3
// 3
// 3

Not specific to setTimeout — any deferred call (promise, event) behaves the same.

cards.question: How do you fix the var-in-loop pitfall?
cards.answer:
Give each iteration its own variable to close over. Three ways — reach for let unless stuck in old code:

// 1. let — a fresh binding per iteration
for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) setTimeout(() => console.log(i)); // 0 1 2

// 2. IIFE — pass i in as a new local each iteration
for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) ((j) => setTimeout(() => console.log(j)))(i); // 0 1 2

// 3. factory — return a closure over the value
function logger(v) { return () => console.log(v); }
for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) setTimeout(logger(i)); // 0 1 2

cards.question: What problem do modules solve?
cards.answer:
Modules let you split a growing codebase into separate files that share functionality explicitly, instead of one shared global scope.

A module is just a file. One script is one module. Without modules, every script shares one global scope: any file can overwrite another's variables, and nothing in the code says which file depends on which. Modules fix both — each file gets its own scope, and its dependencies are written out explicitly at the top.

The language had no modules at first; scripts were small. As applications grew, the community invented its own systems — AMD (require.js), CommonJS (Node.js), UMD (compatible with both). Language-level modules arrived in 2015 and are now supported by all major browsers and Node.js; the older systems survive only in legacy code.

cards.question: How did the module pattern fake modules with closures?
cards.answer:
Before language modules, an IIFE played the role of a module: variables declared inside it were private, and whatever it returned became the public interface.

This is the private-state trick from the Closure topic applied to code organization. The returned methods close over the hidden variables; nothing else can reach them:

const counter = (function () {
  let count = 0; // private

  return {
    increment() { count++; },
    value() { return count; },
  };
})();

counter.increment();
counter.value(); // 1
counter.count;   // undefined — no way in

One IIFE per "module" kept variables out of the global scope and exposed at most a single global name. The pattern is obsolete — export/import give the same privacy at the language level — but it is the direct ancestor of today's module scope, and you'll still meet it in pre-ES6 code.

cards.question: What is CommonJS and how do require and module.exports work?
cards.answer:
CommonJS is the module system built for Node.js: every file is a module, module.exports is the value it exposes, and require() loads another module and returns that value.

// math.js
function add(a, b) { return a + b; }
module.exports = { add };

// main.js
const { add } = require('./math.js');
add(2, 3); // 5

Node gives each file its own scope by wrapping it in a function behind the scenes:

(function (exports, require, module, __filename, __dirname) {
  // your file runs here
});

exports is just a local alias for module.exports — the same object. Attaching properties works (exports.add = ...), but assigning a whole new value (exports = {...}) only rebinds the alias and exports nothing. To replace the export entirely, assign to module.exports.

CommonJS is not part of the JavaScript language — it predates ES modules. Node.js still runs it everywhere, and most existing Node code uses it.

cards.question: What does require() do when it loads a module?
cards.answer: require() loads and runs the file synchronously, caches the result, and hands every importer the same module.exports value.

cards.question: Why is require() synchronous, and where can it be called?
cards.answer: require reads the file from disk and runs it before the next line executes — fine for a server where files are local. Because it's just a runtime call, it can appear anywhere, even inside an if.

cards.question: How many times does a CommonJS module's code run?
cards.answer:
Once — on the first require. Every later require of the same path returns the cached module.exports, so all importers share one instance and its state.

// counter.js
let count = 0;
module.exports = { inc: () => ++count };

// anywhere
require('./counter.js') === require('./counter.js'); // true — same object

cards.question: Does a destructured CommonJS import follow later changes to the module?
cards.answer: No. require hands you the module.exports value as of require time. Destructure a primitive out of it and you hold a copy — if the module changes its internal variable later, your copy doesn't follow. (ES modules differ here — see ES Modules.)

cards.question: What does the inner require() return in a CommonJS cycle?
cards.answer: The same module.exports object the first module is still filling — incomplete at that moment, but not a copy. Properties the module adds later are visible through the reference.

cards.question: Why can't Node re-run a module during a circular require?
cards.answer:
When a.js requires b.js which requires a.js back, a.js is mid-run and already in the cache — so b.js receives a reference to the half-finished exports object.

// a.js
exports.loaded = false;
const b = require("./b");  // b runs now, sees {loaded: false}
exports.loaded = true;     // same object — b's reference sees this

// b.js
const a = require("./a");
a.loaded; // false — a.js hasn't finished yet
setTimeout(() => console.log(a.loaded)); // true — the mutation is visible

cards.question: When does a value freeze (stop updating) during a CommonJS cycle?
cards.answer:
Two cases:

CaseWhy it freezes
const { loaded } = require("./a")destructuring copies the current value, which never follows
module.exports = {...}first module reassigns; requirer still holds the old object

cards.question: What's the fix for a circular require()?
cards.answer: Treat it as a design smell: usually one module is doing two jobs. Extracting the shared piece into a third module dissolves the loop. (ESM resolves cycles with live bindings — see ES Modules.)

cards.question: What is an ES module and what makes a file one?
cards.answer:
An ES module is a file that uses the language's own export/import. The browser opts in with <script type="module">; Node.js with the .mjs extension or "type": "module" in package.json.

<script type="module">
  import { sayHi } from './sayHi.js';
  sayHi('John');
</script>

With type="module" the browser automatically fetches and evaluates the imported module (and its imports, recursively), then runs the script.

In the browser, import must get a relative or absolute URL — './sayHi.js', not 'sayHi'. Modules without any path are called bare modules and aren't allowed in browser import; Node.js and bundlers resolve them through node_modules.

Two browser details worth knowing: external module scripts with the same src run only once, and modules fetched from another origin require CORS headers. Old browsers ignore type="module"; a nomodule script provides the fallback.

cards.question: What's the difference between named and default exports?
cards.answer:
A module can have many named exports (imported in curly braces by exact name) and at most one default export (imported without braces under any name you choose).

NamedDefault
Exportexport class User {}export default class User {}
Importimport { User } from ...import User from ...

cards.question: What are the two equivalent ways to write named exports?
cards.answer:
Put export before a declaration, or declare first and export a list separately:

export const YEAR = 2015;
export function sayHi(user) { /*...*/ }

// or declare first, export apart:
function sayBye(user) { /*...*/ }
export { sayBye };

cards.question: How do you rename or grab everything when importing named exports?
cards.answer:
Import by exact name, rename with as, or grab everything as a namespace object:

import { sayHi, sayBye as bye } from './say.js';
import * as say from './say.js'; // say.sayHi(...)

cards.question: Can a default-exported entity be anonymous?
cards.answer:
Yes — there's one per file, for modules that are "one thing", and the entity may be anonymous because the importer names it.

// user.js
export default class User { /*...*/ }

// main.js — no braces, any name
import User from './user.js';

cards.question: How do you import a default and named exports in one statement?
cards.answer:
Default first, then the braces:

import User, { sayHi } from './user.js';

cards.question: Is default really a special kind of export?
cards.answer: No — default is just a name. export { sayHi as default } equals export default sayHi, and import * as obj exposes it as obj.default.

cards.question: Why do many teams prefer named exports over default?
cards.answer: Named exports force every importer to use the right name; with default each importer picks its own, so names drift across a team.

cards.question: What is re-export and how does export ... from work?
cards.answer:
export ... from imports a name and immediately exports it again — the standard way to build a package entry point that hides internal files.

Instead of making users import from deep paths, one index.js gathers the public API:

// auth/index.js
export { login, logout } from './helpers.js';
export { default as User } from './user.js';

Re-exported names aren't usable in the current file — the syntax only passes them through, without creating a local binding.

The default export needs explicit handling: export * from './user.js' re-exports only the named exports and ignores the default. To pass the default along, write export { default as User } from './user.js' — or export { default } from './user.js' to keep it as the default.

cards.question: Do ES modules need "use strict"?
cards.answer: No — module code is always in strict mode, no opt-in. Assigning to an undeclared variable throws.

cards.question: Are a module's top-level variables visible to other scripts?
cards.answer: No. Top-level variables and functions live in the module's own scope and aren't seen elsewhere. Sharing goes through import/export, not globals.

cards.question: How many times is an ES module evaluated, and what do importers share?
cards.answer:
Code runs on the first import; every importer then gets the same exports. Mutate an exported object in one place and all others see it — enabling setup/config patterns.

// admin.js
export let admin = { name: "John" };

// 1.js
import { admin } from './admin.js';
admin.name = "Pete";

// 2.js
import { admin } from './admin.js';
admin.name; // "Pete" — one shared object

cards.question: Can you use await at the top level of a module?
cards.answer: Yes — only modules may use await outside a function. Importers wait until the awaited work finishes before running.

cards.question: How are module scripts loaded in the browser by default?
cards.answer: Like defer: they download in parallel, wait until the HTML document is fully ready, and keep their relative order. The async attribute also works on inline module scripts — they run as soon as their imports are ready.

cards.question: What is top-level this in an ES module?
cards.answer: undefined — where a non-module script would see the global object.

cards.question: Is an ESM import a copy or a live view of the export?
cards.answer:
A live, read-only view of the module's own variable — it doesn't copy, so updates are visible.

// counter.mjs
export let count = 0;
export function inc() { count++; }

// main.mjs
import { count, inc } from './counter.mjs';
inc();
count; // 1 — the import sees the update

cards.question: What does the CommonJS equivalent of a live binding do instead?
cards.answer: const { count, inc } = require('./counter.js') leaves count at 0 forever — a primitive copied out of the exports object at require time.

cards.question: Can you reassign an imported binding in ESM?
cards.answer: No — the binding is read-only on the importer's side. Assigning to count in the importing module is an error.

cards.question: Why is static import "static" and what does that enable?
cards.answer: Unlike require (an ordinary runtime call), import is rigid by design — so the whole module graph is known before execution: missing names fail early, and bundlers can tree-shake unused exports. (Run-time loading via import() — see Dynamic import.)

cards.question: Are circular ESM imports an error?
cards.answer: No. The engine builds the whole module graph first, then runs the bodies, and imports are live bindings — so the cycle usually resolves itself, as long as imported values aren't used at the top level too early.

cards.question: What happens when you read a not-yet-initialized import at the top level in a cycle?
cards.answer:
It behaves exactly like the underlying declaration:

DeclarationTop-level read in cycle
let / conststill in TDZ → ReferenceError
functionhoisted → already callable
varreads undefined
// a.mjs: export let loaded = ...; export function check() {...}

// b.mjs — runs while a.mjs is still initializing
import { loaded, check } from "./a.mjs";
loaded;   // ReferenceError — let binding still in TDZ
check();  // works — function declarations are hoisted

export function report() {
  return loaded; // fine — called after init, binding is live
}

cards.question: What are the rules of thumb for ESM circular imports?
cards.answer: Don't use imported values at the top level in a cycle — defer the use into functions; and treat a cycle as a design smell, extracting the shared piece into a third module to dissolve the loop. (CommonJS resolves cycles differently — see CommonJS.)

cards.question: What is import() and what does it return?
cards.answer:
import(modulePath) loads a module on demand and returns a promise that resolves to the module object containing all its exports.

let { hi, bye } = await import('./say.js');
hi();
bye();

cards.question: Why does static import exist alongside dynamic import()?
cards.answer: Static import is deliberately rigid — the path must be a primitive string (no function calls) and it can't sit inside if or a function. That rigidity makes the module graph analyzable. import() is the escape hatch for genuine run-time loading.

cards.question: How do you get a default export from import()?
cards.answer:
It shows up as the default property of the module object:

let { default: say } = await import('./say.js');
say();

cards.question: Is import() a function call, and does it need type="module"?
cards.answer: No to both. Dynamic imports work in regular scripts (no type="module" needed), and despite the parentheses import() is special syntax (like super()), not a function — you can't copy it to a variable or use call/apply.

cards.question: What are typical uses for dynamic import()?
cards.answer: Loading a heavy feature only when the user asks for it, picking a module by condition (a polyfill, a locale file), or splitting a bundle so the first paint stays light.

cards.question: What is import.meta?
cards.answer:
import.meta is an object with information about the current module. Its main field is import.meta.url — the URL of the module file (or of the page, for an inline script).

The common use is resolving a file relative to the module itself, not to the page that loaded it:

const iconURL = new URL('./icon.png', import.meta.url);

In Node.js ES modules there is no __dirname or __filename — those belong to the CommonJS wrapper. import.meta.url replaces them, and recent Node versions add import.meta.dirname and import.meta.filename directly.

Unlike this or globals, import.meta is per-module: every module sees its own metadata, which is exactly what makes it the right home for "where am I" information.

cards.question: What is an object and how do you create one?
cards.answer:
An object stores a keyed collection of data: properties, each a string key paired with a value of any type. The usual way to create one is the object literal — curly braces with key: value pairs.

let user = {
  name: "John",
  age: 30
};

(new Object() creates the same empty object as {}; the literal is what everyone writes.)

Read and write properties with the dot, add new ones by plain assignment, remove with delete:

user.name;          // "John"
user.isAdmin = true; // add
delete user.age;     // remove

Property keys are strings or symbols. A number used as a key becomes a string: { 0: "test" } is the same as { "0": "test" }. Reserved words are fine as keys — { for: 1, let: 2, return: 3 } works, because keys aren't variables.

cards.question: When do you need square brackets instead of the dot?
cards.answer:
The dot works only for keys that are valid identifiers. Square brackets take any string — including multiword keys and, more importantly, keys computed at runtime.

let user = {
  "likes birds": true
};

user["likes birds"]; // true — user.likes birds is a syntax error

The real power is dynamic access — the key can come from a variable or any expression:

let key = "name";
user[key]; // reads user.name
user.key;  // reads the literal property "key" — not what you want

cards.question: How do you check whether a property exists?
cards.answer:
Use the in operator: "key" in obj returns true if the object has the property.

Comparing with undefined seems to work, but it lies when the property exists and stores undefined:

let obj = { test: undefined };
obj.test === undefined; // true — looks absent, but isn't
"test" in obj;          // true — the property is there

The left side of in must be a string (or a variable holding one): let key = "test"; key in obj.

Note that in also finds inherited properties. To test own properties only, use Object.hasOwn(obj, key) — ownership is covered with enumerability below.

cards.question: What are constructor functions and what does new do?
cards.answer:
A constructor function is a regular function meant to be called with new. new F() creates an empty object, makes this point to it, runs the function body, and returns that object.

function User(name) {
  // this = {};  (implicitly)
  this.name = name;
  this.isAdmin = false;
  // return this;  (implicitly)
}

let user = new User("Jack");
user.name; // "Jack"

By convention constructors start with a capital letter and are only called with new. The point is reuse: one function, many similar objects.

The gotcha is return inside a constructor. Returning an object replaces this; returning a primitive is ignored:

function BigUser() {
  this.name = "John";
  return { name: "Godzilla" }; // this object is returned
}
new BigUser().name; // "Godzilla"

function SmallUser() {
  this.name = "John";
  return 5; // ignored — this is returned
}
new SmallUser().name; // "John"

cards.question: What are computed properties and the value shorthand?
cards.answer:
Computed properties let an object literal take its key from an expression: [expr]: value. The value shorthand lets you write name instead of name: name when the variable and the key share a name.

Computed key. Square brackets inside the literal evaluate the expression and use the result as the key:

let fruit = "apple";

let bag = {
  [fruit]: 5,                 // key from a variable -> { apple: 5 }
  [fruit + 'Computers']: 10,  // any expression works -> appleComputers
};

It's the literal-syntax twin of bag[fruit] = 5 — same brackets, same meaning, just done at creation time.

Value shorthand. Building an object from existing variables is so common that repeating the name is unnecessary:

function makeUser(name, age) {
  return { name, age }; // same as { name: name, age: age }
}

Shorthand and normal pairs mix freely in one literal.

cards.question: What is the method shorthand?
cards.answer:
A function stored as a property is called a method. The shorthand lets you write sayHi() { ... } in a literal instead of sayHi: function() { ... }.

let user = {
  sayHi() {        // same as sayHi: function() {
    console.log("Hello");
  }
};

user.sayHi(); // Hello

Inside a method, this refers to the object before the dot — how this works is the this & Binding topic.

The two forms are interchangeable in everyday code; the shorthand has minor extra abilities tied to inheritance (super), covered with classes.

cards.question: What is optional chaining?
cards.answer:
?. stops the evaluation if the value before it is undefined or null, and returns undefined instead of throwing.

The problem it solves — reading a nested property when an intermediate object may not exist:

let user = {};
user.address.street;  // TypeError — user.address is undefined
user.address?.street; // undefined — no error

Before ?. this took repetitive guards: user.address && user.address.street or a ternary. The chain reads naturally now.

Three forms, one rule each:

FormMeaning
obj?.propproperty, if obj exists
obj?.[key]bracket access, if obj exists
obj.method?.()call, if the method exists
 
let admin = { sayHi() { console.log("Hi"); } };
let guest = {};

admin.sayHi?.(); // Hi
guest.sayHi?.(); // nothing — no error

cards.question: What does ?. short-circuit, and does it skip side effects?
cards.answer:
When ?. hits null/undefined it stops and returns undefined — everything to its right is skipped, including side effects like function calls and argument evaluation.

let user = null;
let x = 0;
user?.sayHi(x++); // sayHi isn't called...
x;                // 0 — ...and x++ never ran

cards.question: Can you assign to a property through ?.
cards.answer:
No. ?. produces a value, not an assignment target — so it can't be on the left of =. It works for reading and deleting only.

user?.name = "John"; // SyntaxError
delete user?.name;   // fine — deletes if user exists

cards.question: Does ?. protect against an undeclared variable?
cards.answer: No. user?.address still throws ReferenceError if user was never declared. ?. only guards against null/undefined values, not missing variables.

cards.question: When should you NOT use ?.
cards.answer:
Don't overuse it — put ?. only where absence is a legitimate state. If user must exist by your logic, write user.address?.street, not user?.address?.street.

Otherwise a real bug (no user) is silenced into undefined and surfaces somewhere far away, much harder to debug.

cards.question: What does a variable actually hold for an object, and what does assignment copy?
cards.answer:
A variable never holds the object itself — it holds a reference (the object's address in memory). Assignment copies that reference, so two variables point at one object. Primitives, by contrast, are copied as whole values.

let user = { name: "John" };
let admin = user;   // copies the reference, not the object

admin.name = "Pete";
user.name; // "Pete" — there is only one object

cards.question: When are two objects equal with == or ===?
cards.answer:
Only when both sides reference the same object. Two separately created objects are never equal, even with identical contents.

let a = {};
let b = a;
a === b; // true — same object

let c = {}, d = {};
c === d; // false — two different objects

cards.question: Why can a const object still be mutated?
cards.answer:
const freezes the reference, not the object. You can change the object's contents; you just can't reassign the variable to a new object.

const user = { name: "John" };
user.name = "Pete"; // fine — the reference didn't change
user = {};          // Error — reassigning the reference

cards.question: How do you make a shallow copy of an object, and what does it miss?
cards.answer:
Object.assign({}, obj) or spread { ...obj } duplicates top-level properties. It's shallow: nested objects are copied as references, so they stay shared.

let user = { name: "John", sizes: { height: 182, width: 50 } };
let clone = Object.assign({}, user); // or { ...user }

clone.sizes === user.sizes; // true — same nested object
user.sizes.width = 60;
clone.sizes.width;          // 60 — the "copy" followed

cards.question: How do you make a true deep copy of an object?
cards.answer:
structuredClone(obj) duplicates the whole structure — nested objects and arrays included, and even circular references.

let clone = structuredClone(user);
clone.sizes === user.sizes; // false — truly separate

let obj = {};
obj.me = obj;              // circular
structuredClone(obj).me;   // works, points at the clone

cards.question: What can't structuredClone copy?
cards.answer: It can't copy functions or DOM nodes — it throws. For those cases reach for a library implementation like lodash's cloneDeep.

cards.question: What three flags does every data property have, and what is a descriptor?
cards.answer:
Beyond its value, each data property has writable (can the value change), enumerable (does it show in loops), and configurable (can it be deleted or have its flags changed). The value plus those flags is its descriptor.

Properties created the normal way get all three flags true.

cards.question: How do you read and change a property's flags?
cards.answer:
Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptor(obj, key) reads them; Object.defineProperty(obj, key, descriptor) sets them.

let user = { name: "John" };

Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptor(user, "name");
// { value: "John", writable: true, enumerable: true, configurable: true }

Object.defineProperty(user, "name", { writable: false });

cards.question: What flags do you get when defineProperty CREATES a new property?
cards.answer:
The trap: every flag you don't supply defaults to false — the opposite of normal property creation.

let obj = {};
Object.defineProperty(obj, "hidden", { value: 42 });
// writable: false, enumerable: false, configurable: false

cards.question: How do you clone an object including its flags?
cards.answer:
Pair Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptors(obj) with Object.defineProperties. A plain Object.assign copy can't do this — it copies values only and resets flags to defaults.

let clone = Object.defineProperties({}, Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptors(obj));

cards.question: What does writable: false do, and what happens if you assign anyway?
cards.answer:
It makes the value read-only. Assignment is silently ignored in sloppy mode and throws TypeError in strict mode.

Object.defineProperty(user, "name", { writable: false });
user.name = "Pete"; // TypeError in strict mode

cards.question: What does enumerable: false do?
cards.answer:
The property still works fully — it just stops appearing in for..in and Object.keys. (Same reason built-ins like toString don't clutter your loops.)

Object.defineProperty(user, "secret", { value: 1, enumerable: false });
Object.keys(user); // no "secret"
user.secret;       // 1 — still readable

cards.question: What does configurable: false do, and can it be undone?
cards.answer:
It's a one-way door: the property can no longer be deleted or have its flags changed, and configurable: false itself can't be reversed — not even by defineProperty. Math.PI is the classic example (non-writable, non-enumerable, non-configurable).

Object.defineProperty(user, "name", { configurable: false });
delete user.name;                                    // ignored / throws in strict
Object.defineProperty(user, "name", { enumerable: false }); // TypeError

cards.question: Is there any change allowed on a non-configurable property?
cards.answer: One exception: you may still flip writable from true to false — tightening only. No other flag change is permitted.

cards.question: What's the difference between Object.keys and for..in?
cards.answer:
Object.keys lists the object's own enumerable string keys. for..in lists those too, plus enumerable keys inherited from the prototype chain.

let animal = { eats: true };
let rabbit = Object.create(animal);
rabbit.jumps = true;

Object.keys(rabbit);            // ["jumps"] — own only
for (let key in rabbit) { ... } // jumps, eats — walks the chain

cards.question: How do you test whether a key is the object's own?
cards.answer: Use Object.hasOwn(obj, key) (or the older obj.hasOwnProperty(key)). The mechanics of inheritance belong to the Prototypes topic.

cards.question: In what order do for..in and Object.keys list keys?
cards.answer:
Integer-like keys first, sorted ascending; all other keys after, in creation order. An integer property is a string that converts to an integer and back unchanged — "49" qualifies, "+49" and "1.2" don't.

let codes = { "49": "Germany", "41": "Switzerland", "1": "USA" };
Object.keys(codes); // ["1", "41", "49"] — sorted, not creation order

cards.question: How do you keep numeric-looking keys in creation order?
cards.answer: Make them non-integer so they skip the ascending sort — e.g. prefix each with "+". They then list in creation order like normal string keys.

cards.question: What are getter and setter properties?
cards.answer:
Accessor properties are functions that run on property read and write, but from the outside look like ordinary properties. get runs when the property is read, set when it is assigned.

let user = {
  name: "John",
  surname: "Smith",

  get fullName() {
    return `${this.name} ${this.surname}`;
  },

  set fullName(value) {
    [this.name, this.surname] = value.split(" ");
  }
};

user.fullName;                // "John Smith" — no parentheses
user.fullName = "Alice Cooper";
user.name;                    // "Alice"

That's the point: callers read and assign fullName like a data property; the functions run invisibly.

In defineProperty terms, an accessor descriptor has get and set in place of value and writable (plus the usual enumerable and configurable). A property is either a data property or an accessor — supplying both get and value in one descriptor is an error.

cards.question: What are the two main practical uses of getters and setters?
cards.answer: 1) Wrapping a real property to validate or control access, and 2) keeping old code working when a property's implementation changes.

cards.question: How do you use a setter to validate before storing a value?
cards.answer:
Store the real value in an underscore-prefixed backing property (convention for "internal, don't touch") and let the setter guard it.

let user = {
  get name() {
    return this._name;
  },
  set name(value) {
    if (value.length < 4) {
      console.log("Name is too short, need at least 4 characters");
      return;
    }
    this._name = value;
  }
};

user.name = "Pete"; // stored
user.name = "";     // rejected

cards.question: How does a getter keep old code working after changing a property's implementation?
cards.answer:
Replace the removed property with a getter that computes it on the fly. Old code reading user.age keeps working even though you now store birthday.

function User(name, birthday) {
  this.name = name;
  this.birthday = birthday;

  Object.defineProperty(this, "age", {
    get() {
      let todayYear = new Date().getFullYear();
      return todayYear - this.birthday.getFullYear();
    }
  });
}

let john = new User("John", new Date(1992, 6, 1));
john.age; // calculated from birthday — old code unchanged

cards.question: How do you lock down a whole object?
cards.answer:
Three levels, each stricter: Object.preventExtensions forbids adding properties; Object.seal also forbids removing them; Object.freeze additionally makes every value read-only.

In flag terms: seal sets configurable: false on all existing properties; freeze sets configurable: false, writable: false.

adddeletechange value
preventExtensions✗✓✓
seal✗✗✓
freeze✗✗✗
 
const config = Object.freeze({ retries: 3 });
config.retries = 5;  // ignored (TypeError in strict mode)
config.delay = 100;  // ignored
config.retries;      // 3

Each has a test: Object.isExtensible(obj), Object.isSealed(obj), Object.isFrozen(obj).

Freezing is shallow: a nested object stays mutable — Object.freeze({ a: { b: 1 } }) still allows obj.a.b = 2. Deep-freezing means walking the structure and freezing each level.

cards.question: What determines the value of this?
cards.answer:
this is not lexical. It is set by how a function is called (the call-site), not by where the function is written.

The same function gets a different this depending on the call; JavaScript decides at runtime.

cards.question: What are the four binding rules for this?
cards.answer:
JS checks the call-site against four rules; the highest that applies wins.

RuleCall-sitethis becomes
newnew Foo()a brand-new object
explicitfoo.call(o) / .apply / .bindthe object you pass
implicitobj.foo()the object left of the dot
defaultfoo()undefined (strict) / global (sloppy)

cards.question: What is the precedence order of the four binding rules?
cards.answer: Highest first: new > explicit > implicit > default. The highest rule that applies to the call-site wins.

cards.question: How does one function behave when called four different ways?
cards.answer:
function whoAmI() { return this; }
const obj = { name: "obj", whoAmI };

obj.whoAmI() === obj            // true   (implicit: left of the dot)
whoAmI.call({ name: "x" })      // {name:"x"}  (explicit)
new whoAmI()                    // a fresh object (new)
whoAmI()                        // undefined / global (default)

cards.question: What is default binding?
cards.answer:
A plain call foo() with no object, no call, no new. this is the global object in sloppy mode and undefined in strict mode. It applies when nothing else does.

// sloppy script
function show() { return this; }
show() === globalThis     // true
"use strict";
function show() { return this; }
show()                    // undefined

cards.question: Why assume strict mode for default binding?
cards.answer: Code inside ES modules and class bodies always runs strict, so this is undefined there with default binding. Reading this.name in that case throws.

cards.question: What is implicit binding?
cards.answer:
A method call obj.foo() through an object: this is whatever sits immediately left of the dot at the call-site.

const obj = { name: "obj", greet() { return this.name; } };
obj.greet()               // "obj"

cards.question: In a chain a.b.c(), what is this?
cards.answer:
Only the last link counts. For a.b.c(), this is b — not a.

const a = { b: { name: "b", c() { return this.name; } } };
a.b.c()                   // "b"

cards.question: What is explicit binding?
cards.answer:
Using call, apply, or bind to force this to an object you choose. call/apply invoke immediately; bind returns a new function with this locked in.

function greet() { return this.name; }
const user = { name: "John" };

greet.call(user)          // "John"
greet.apply(user)         // "John"
const bound = greet.bind(user);
bound()                   // "John"

cards.question: Can a later call() override an existing bind()?
cards.answer:
No. A bind is a hard binding — permanent. Explicit beats implicit, but a later call cannot override an existing bind.

bound.call({ name: "Other" })   // "John" — bind wins, call ignored

cards.question: What is new binding?
cards.answer:
Calling with new creates a fresh empty object and sets it as this. Assigning to this populates that object, which is returned.

function Person(name) { this.name = name; }
const p = new Person("Alice");
p.name                    // "Alice"

cards.question: What does new return if the constructor returns a value?
cards.answer:
If the constructor returns an object, that object replaces the fresh this. Returning a primitive (or nothing) is ignored and this is returned.

function A() { this.x = 1; return { x: 99 }; }
new A().x                 // 99 — returned object wins

function B() { this.x = 1; return 5; }
new B().x                 // 1  — primitive ignored

cards.question: Does new override a hard bind?
cards.answer:
Yes — new sits at the top of precedence. The bound this is thrown away and the new object is used instead.

function P(name) { this.name = name; }
const BP = P.bind({ name: "preset" });
const made = new BP("FromNew");
made.name                 // "FromNew" — new won, "preset" ignored
made instanceof P         // true

cards.question: How do you lose this?
cards.answer:
By detaching a method from its object. obj.method() binds this to obj, but obj.method alone is a plain function value with no link back to obj — the dot at call time is what binds this.

'use strict';
const user = {
  firstName: "John",
  sayHi() { console.log(`Hello, ${this.firstName}`); }
};

user.sayHi();          // Hello, John   (this = user)

const f = user.sayHi;  // just a function value, no object
f();                   // TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined

cards.question: What this does a detached method get when called standalone?
cards.answer:
The default binding: this is undefined in strict mode (so this.firstName throws), or the global object in sloppy mode (so this.firstName is silently undefined).

// sloppy mode
const f = user.sayHi;
f();  // this = global object, this.firstName = undefined

cards.question: Why does passing a method as a callback lose this?
cards.answer:
A callback detaches the method the same way. setTimeout(user.sayHi, 1000) hands over the function separately from user, so when it runs there is no dot and no binding.

setTimeout(user.sayHi, 1000);
// later: called with no object -> this lost, firstName undefined

cards.question: What is this in an addEventListener handler passed as obj.method?
cards.answer: addEventListener("click", obj.method) stores the bare function, so this is no longer your object. An event listener actually sets this to the element — still not your object.

cards.question: What are the three ways to preserve this when detaching a method?
cards.answer: 1. bind it. 2. Wrap it in an arrow that calls the method. 3. Define it as a class-field arrow.

cards.question: How does bind preserve this?
cards.answer:
obj.method.bind(obj) returns a new function whose this is permanently fixed. It can be called alone or passed anywhere and the binding holds.

const user = {
  firstName: "John",
  sayHi() { console.log(`Hello, ${this.firstName}!`); }
};

const bound = user.sayHi.bind(user);
bound();                  // Hello, John!
setTimeout(bound, 1000);  // Hello, John!

cards.question: How does an arrow wrapper preserve this, and how does it differ from bind?
cards.answer:
Wrap the call: () => obj.method(). The arrow has no own this; inside it a normal obj.method() call lets the dot bind at call time.

setTimeout(() => user.sayHi(), 1000);  // Hello, John!

Difference: the arrow re-reads user when it runs, so reassigning user changes which method runs. bind locks the object in now.

cards.question: How does a class-field arrow preserve this?
cards.answer:
Defining a method as an arrow in a class field binds this to the instance at construction, so it survives detaching.

class Button {
  text = "Click";
  handleClick = () => {
    console.log("clicked:", this.text);
  };
}
const b = new Button();
const detached = b.handleClick;
detached();                    // clicked: Click
setTimeout(b.handleClick, 0);  // clicked: Click

cards.question: What do call and apply do, and how do they differ?
cards.answer:
Both invoke a function immediately with a this you choose, overriding the normal call-site rule. They differ only in argument shape: call takes them one by one, apply as an array (mnemonic: A for Array).

function greet(greeting, punct) {
  return greeting + ", " + this.name + punct;
}
const user = { name: "John" };

greet.call(user, "Hi", "!");      // "Hi, John!"  — args one by one
greet.apply(user, ["Hi", "!"]);   // "Hi, John!"  — args as an array

cards.question: How are call and apply interchangeable with spread?
cards.answer: With spread the two are equivalent: fn.call(ctx, ...args) equals fn.apply(ctx, args).

cards.question: What does bind do?
cards.answer:
bind returns a new function permanently locked to a given this; it does not call now. Calling that function always runs the original with the fixed this.

function greet(greeting) {
  return greeting + ", " + this.name;
}
const user = { name: "John" };

const bound = greet.bind(user);
bound("Hi");   // "Hi, John" — this is baked in

cards.question: Is a bound function the same object as the original?
cards.answer: No — the bound function is a separate function object. greet.bind(user) is not greet, and its this is hard-fixed.

cards.question: How does bind do partial application?
cards.answer:
Extra arguments after the context are pre-filled as the leading arguments, producing a more specific function from a general one.

function mul(a, b) { return a * b; }
const double = mul.bind(null, 2); // a fixed to 2; this unused, so null
double(3); // 6
double(5); // 10

cards.question: How does this work in an arrow function?
cards.answer:
An arrow has no own this. Reading this takes the value from the enclosing lexical scope, fixed where the arrow is written.

The lookup is like an ordinary variable lookup: not found in the arrow, the search continues outward to the nearest enclosing function (or top-level scope).

cards.question: Can call/apply/bind change an arrow's this?
cards.answer:
No. The binding is lexical and decided at write-time, so call, apply, and bind are silently ignored.

const outer = {
  x: 10,
  getArrow() { return () => this.x; }
};

const arrow = outer.getArrow();
arrow();              // 10  (this === outer)
arrow.call({ x: 999 }); // 10  (call is ignored)

cards.question: When is an arrow the right vs wrong tool for this?
cards.answer: Right for a callback written inside a method — it inherits the method's this automatically, replacing the old const self = this / .bind(this) workaround. Wrong as a standalone object method, where you want this to be the object, not the surrounding scope.

cards.question: When does an arrow fix this inside a method?
cards.answer:
A callback in setTimeout, an array iterator, or an event handler normally loses the method's this. An arrow keeps it, inheriting this from the method it's written in.

const group = {
  title: "Our Group",
  students: ["John", "Pete", "Alice"],
  showList() {
    this.students.forEach(s => console.log(this.title + ": " + s));
  }
};
group.showList(); // "Our Group: John" ...  (this === group)

cards.question: What this does a regular function callback get in forEach or setTimeout?
cards.answer:
It does not inherit the method's this. forEach without a thisArg calls it with this = undefined (global in sloppy mode); browser setTimeout always passes this = window explicitly, so even a strict-mode function gets window.

Strict mode only blocks coercion of an undefined this; it cannot refuse a this handed over directly.

cards.question: Why does an arrow break an object method defined directly as an arrow?
cards.answer:
Then this is the scope where the object literal sits, not the object. So it reads the wrong value or throws.

const obj = {
  x: 5,
  getXArrow: () => this.x,      // this = enclosing scope, NOT obj
  getXMethod() { return this.x; } // this = obj
};

obj.getXMethod(); // 5
obj.getXArrow();  // undefined  (or throws)

cards.question: What is the arrow method's this in different environments?
cards.answer:
It depends on the surrounding scope, and never sees obj.

Environmenttop-level thisobj.getXArrow()
browser <script>windowundefined
CommonJS modulemodule.exportsundefined
ES moduleundefinedthrows TypeError

cards.question: Why does obj.method() keep this but a detached call loses it?
cards.answer: Because the dot doesn't return the function — it returns an internal Reference Type that remembers the base object, and the () right after uses that base as this. Read the function out of the reference first and the link is gone.

cards.question: What is the Reference Type and what does it hold?
cards.answer: When you write user.hi, the dot evaluates to a Reference Type — a triple (base, name, strict), here (user, "hi", true). It's not the function value; it carries which object the property came from.

cards.question: Which operation reads the Reference Type to set this?
cards.answer:
The call () is the only operation that reads a Reference Type: it takes the base and uses it as this. Bracket access works the same — the mechanism is property access, not the dot specifically.

user["hi"](); // "John" — brackets produce a reference too, base = user

cards.question: Which operations throw away the base object of obj.method?
cards.answer:
Almost any operation other than the call reads the plain function value out of the reference: assignment, passing as an argument, a ternary, a ||.

let user = {
  name: "John",
  hi() { return this.name; },
  bye() { return "bye " + this.name; },
};

user.hi(); // "John" — () reads the reference, base = user

let cond = true;
(cond ? user.hi : user.bye)();
// TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'name')

cards.question: Which operations keep vs discard the Reference Type?
cards.answer:
this survives only when () is applied directly to obj.method (or a parenthesized form). The instant another operation consumes it, it unwraps to the bare function.

OperationReference kept?
obj.m()yes — call reads it directly
obj['m']()yes — bracket access produces a reference
(obj.m)()yes — parens don't change order
let f = obj.mno — assignment
run(obj.m)no — passed as argument
return obj.mno — returned out
(cond ? obj.m : obj.n)()no — ternary
(obj.m || obj.n)()no — logical operator

cards.question: Do plain parentheses discard the Reference Type?
cards.answer:
No. In (obj.m)() the dot still runs first and the parens just wrap the result — no extra operation touches the value, so the reference passes through to the call.

let obj = {
  name: "Obj",
  go() { return "go " + this.name; },
  stop() { return "stop " + this.name; },
};

(obj.go)();              // "go Obj"  — reference survives
(obj.go || obj.stop)();  // TypeError — || reads obj.go to its
                         // plain value, so the base is gone

cards.question: What is the syntactic test for whether this binds to obj?
cards.answer: Is () applied directly to obj.method (or a parenthesized form of it)? If yes, this binds to obj. If the function value first flows through a variable, argument, return, or operator, the base is lost.

cards.question: What is prototypal inheritance?
cards.answer:
Every object has a hidden link, [[Prototype]], to another object called its prototype. When you read a property the object lacks, the engine follows that link and looks on the prototype, then up the chain, until it finds the property or reaches null.

Objects inherit from objects directly — no classes underneath. Methods live on the prototype and are shared; each object keeps its own data.

cards.question: How does setting __proto__ in an object literal work?
cards.answer:
The __proto__: key in a literal sets the [[Prototype]] link at creation. Here rabbit has no own walk or eats, so the engine finds them on animal.

let animal = {
  eats: true,
  walk() { return "Animal walk"; }
};

let rabbit = {
  jumps: true,
  __proto__: animal   // rabbit's prototype is animal
};

rabbit.walk();  // "Animal walk"  -- found on animal
rabbit.eats;    // true           -- found on animal
rabbit.jumps;   // true           -- own property

Object.getPrototypeOf(rabbit) === animal;  // true

cards.question: What values can a [[Prototype]] be?
cards.answer:
Only an object or null. Assigning anything else via __proto__ is silently ignored; Object.setPrototypeOf(obj, 5) throws a TypeError.

You cannot form a cycle (the engine throws), and an object has exactly one prototype — no multiple inheritance.

cards.question: How do reads and writes differ with the prototype?
cards.answer: Reads walk up the prototype chain; writes and delete act on the own object only. The prototype is used for reading, never written through.

cards.question: What happens when you assign to an inherited property (shadowing)?
cards.answer:
It creates an own property that shadows the prototype's; the prototype stays untouched and other objects sharing it are unaffected. Deleting the own copy re-exposes the inherited one.

let animal = { walk() { return "Animal walk"; } };
let rabbit = { __proto__: animal };

rabbit.walk = function() { return "Rabbit bounce"; };  // own property

rabbit.walk();  // "Rabbit bounce"  -- own shadows prototype
animal.walk();  // "Animal walk"    -- prototype untouched
rabbit.hasOwnProperty("walk");  // true

delete rabbit.walk;             // removes the OWN copy only
rabbit.walk();  // "Animal walk" -- inherited again

cards.question: What happens when you assign to a name that has an inherited setter?
cards.answer:
The assignment runs the inherited setter instead of creating an own data property. The setter writes to the object before the dot (this), not the prototype, so no own data prop is created.

let user = {
  name: "John",
  set fullName(value) { [this.name, this.surname] = value.split(" "); },
  get fullName() { return `${this.name} ${this.surname}`; }
};
let admin = { __proto__: user };

admin.fullName = "Alice Cooper";   // inherited setter runs, on admin
admin.fullName;                    // "Alice Cooper"
admin.hasOwnProperty("fullName");  // false -- no own data prop
admin.name;                        // "Alice" (own)
user.name;                         // "John"  -- prototype untouched

cards.question: What is the difference between __proto__ and prototype?
cards.answer:
__proto__prototype
Lives onevery objectfunctions only
What it isthe [[Prototype]] link lookups fall through toobject that becomes the __proto__ of instances made with new

The connection: new Foo() gives an object whose __proto__ is Foo.prototype.

cards.question: Do plain objects have a .prototype property?
cards.answer:
No — prototype belongs to functions (constructors). Plain objects have __proto__ but no .prototype.

function Foo() {}
let inst = new Foo();
inst.__proto__ === Foo.prototype;     // true
typeof Foo.prototype;                 // "object"
({}).prototype;                       // undefined
({}).__proto__ === Object.prototype;  // true

cards.question: What does new Foo() do step by step?
cards.answer: new Foo(): creates an empty object, sets its __proto__ to Foo.prototype, runs Foo with this bound to that object, and returns it.

cards.question: Why are methods on Foo.prototype shared across instances?
cards.answer:
Every instance gets the same Foo.prototype as its __proto__, so a method placed there is one function in memory, not a copy per object.

function Rabbit(name) { this.name = name; }
Rabbit.prototype.sayHi = function() { return "hi " + this.name; };

let a = new Rabbit("White");
let b = new Rabbit("Black");

a.__proto__ === Rabbit.prototype; // true
a.sayHi();                        // "hi White"
b.sayHi();                        // "hi Black"
a.sayHi === b.sayHi;              // true — same shared function

cards.question: When does new read Foo.prototype — does reassigning it affect existing instances?
cards.answer:
Foo.prototype is read only at the moment new runs. Reassigning it later leaves existing instances on their old prototype; only objects created afterward get the new one.

function Rabbit() {}
let old = new Rabbit();
Rabbit.prototype = { jumps: true }; // replace after old exists
let fresh = new Rabbit();

old.jumps;    // undefined — old keeps its original prototype
fresh.jumps;  // true      — created after the swap

cards.question: What is the constructor property?
cards.answer:
By default a function's prototype is an object with one property, constructor, pointing back at the function. Instances reach it via the chain, so obj.constructor tells you which function built the object — by convention.

function Rabbit() {}
Rabbit.prototype.constructor === Rabbit; // true (the default)
let r = new Rabbit();
r.constructor === Rabbit;                // true (via the prototype)

cards.question: How can the constructor property break, and how do you keep it?
cards.answer:
Replacing prototype wholesale drops the default constructor, so instances report the wrong one. Keep it by adding to the existing prototype, or restore it by hand.

function Rabbit() {}
Rabbit.prototype = { jumps: true };      // wholesale replace
new Rabbit().constructor === Rabbit;     // false — now points to Object

Rabbit.prototype = { jumps: true, constructor: Rabbit }; // restore
new Rabbit().constructor === Rabbit;     // true

JS never checks or uses this value, so constructor is only a convention — not a reliable answer to "what built this object".

cards.question: What is the prototype chain?
cards.answer:
The series of [[Prototype]] links from an object up through its prototypes, ending at Object.prototype and finally null. Reading a property walks this chain until a match is found, or reaches null and returns undefined.

function Foo() {}
Foo.prototype.greet = function () { return "hi"; };
const obj = new Foo();

Object.getPrototypeOf(obj) === Foo.prototype;              // true
Object.getPrototypeOf(Foo.prototype) === Object.prototype; // true
Object.getPrototypeOf(Object.prototype);                   // null

obj.greet();          // "hi"        -- found on Foo.prototype
typeof obj.toString;  // "function"  -- found on Object.prototype
obj.nope;             // undefined   -- walked to null, not found

cards.question: Where do shared methods like toString and hasOwnProperty live?
cards.answer: On Object.prototype, which sits at the top of the chain. That's why almost every object has them. The chain for obj is obj -> Foo.prototype -> Object.prototype -> null; greet sits on Foo.prototype, toString on Object.prototype.

cards.question: When an inherited method runs, what is this bound to?
cards.answer:
this is the object you called it on, not the prototype that holds the method. That is how shared methods operate on per-object state.

const animal = { walk() { return this.name + " walks"; } };
const rabbit = Object.create(animal);
rabbit.name = "Rabbit";
rabbit.walk();   // "Rabbit walks"  -- this === rabbit

cards.question: How do you check whether a property is own vs inherited?
cards.answer:
Object.hasOwn(obj, key) — or the older obj.hasOwnProperty(key) — is true only when the key sits on the object itself. A plain read returns the first match anywhere on the chain, and in also searches the whole chain.

const proto = { kind: "animal" };
const dog = Object.create(proto);
dog.name = "Rex";

dog.kind;                     // "animal"  -- inherited read
"kind" in dog;                // true      -- `in` walks the chain
Object.hasOwn(dog, "kind");   // false
Object.hasOwn(dog, "name");   // true
dog.hasOwnProperty("name");   // true      -- same check, older form

cards.question: Why prefer Object.hasOwn over hasOwnProperty?
cards.answer:
hasOwnProperty is itself inherited from Object.prototype, so it is missing on prototype-less objects and can be shadowed by an own key of the same name. Object.hasOwn (ES2022) is static and works on anything.

const dict = Object.create(null);
dict.x = 1;

// dict.hasOwnProperty("x");  // TypeError -- nothing inherited
Object.hasOwn(dict, "x");     // true

cards.question: What does prototype chain lookup cost in performance?
cards.answer:
Lookup walks the chain top-down and stops at the first match, but engines cache where a property was found (inline caches), so inherited reads in hot code cost about the same as own reads.

Chain depth mostly matters on first, uncached lookups — keep chains shallow for clarity, not micro-speed. What truly hurts is changing a prototype after creation.

cards.question: What are native prototypes?
cards.answer:
The built-in objects that hold the methods for each type. arr.map lives on Array.prototype, "x".toUpperCase on String.prototype, (5).toFixed on Number.prototype. The value stores only data; methods sit one level up on its prototype.

let arr = [1, 2, 3];
arr.__proto__ === Array.prototype;        // true
typeof Array.prototype.map;               // "function"
arr.map === Array.prototype.map;          // true — same function

"x".toUpperCase === String.prototype.toUpperCase; // true
(5).toFixed === Number.prototype.toFixed;         // true

cards.question: Where does every native prototype chain end?
cards.answer:
At Object.prototype, whose own __proto__ is null. That's why every value has toString and hasOwnProperty. An array's chain is arr -> Array.prototype -> Object.prototype -> null.

let arr = [1, 2, 3];
arr.__proto__.__proto__ === Object.prototype; // true
arr.__proto__.__proto__.__proto__;            // null
Object.prototype.__proto__;                   // null — top
