Array.prototype.toString shadows Object.prototype.toString, so arrays stringify to a comma-joined list, not [object Object].

cards.question: How do primitives like "x" have methods if they aren't objects?
cards.answer: "x".toUpperCase() works because the engine reaches the method through a temporary wrapper object that exposes the matching prototype. null and undefined have no wrapper, so no methods.

cards.question: Can you reuse a native method on an unrelated object?
cards.answer:
Yes. Many built-in methods are generic — they never check the type of this, only its shape. Array.prototype.join just walks indices 0..length-1, so any object with numeric keys and a length (a function's arguments, a DOM NodeList) satisfies it, even though it isn't an array.

That generality is what makes the methods reusable. The pattern of actually reusing them on other objects — method borrowing — is covered in Advanced Functions.

cards.question: Should you modify native prototypes (monkey-patching)?
cards.answer:
No. Adding your own methods to Array.prototype, Object.prototype, etc. is a well-known anti-pattern: the prototypes are global and shared by everything, so your additions leak everywhere.

Array.prototype.last = function () {
  return this[this.length - 1];
};
[1, 2, 3].last(); // 3 — works, but now every array has it

cards.question: What problems does monkey-patching native prototypes cause?
cards.answer:
Collisions: prototypes are global, so a future language version or another library defining the same name silently overwrites yours.

Array.prototype.last = function () { return "lib A"; };
Array.prototype.last = function () { return "lib B"; }; // clobbers A
[1].last(); // "lib B"

Enumeration pollution: added methods are enumerable, so they show up in for...in over every object of that type.

let arr = [10, 20];
for (let key in arr) console.log(key); // "0", "1", "last"

cards.question: When is modifying a native prototype acceptable?
cards.answer:
The one historically accepted use is a polyfill: adding a standardized method an old engine lacks, guarded so it only runs when missing — filling a known gap, not inventing API.

if (!String.prototype.repeat) {
  String.prototype.repeat = function (n) {
    return new Array(n + 1).join(this);
  };
}
"La".repeat(3); // "LaLaLa"

For your own helpers, write a plain function — last(arr) — not a prototype method.

cards.question: How do you create an object with a chosen prototype?
cards.answer:
Object.create(proto) makes a new empty object whose [[Prototype]] is proto. It is the explicit, modern way to set up inheritance, replacing legacy __proto__.

let animal = {
  eats: true,
  walk() { return "walking"; }
};

let rabbit = Object.create(animal);
rabbit.eats;       // true   (inherited)
rabbit.walk();     // "walking"
Object.getPrototypeOf(rabbit) === animal; // true

cards.question: What is the second argument to Object.create?
cards.answer:
An optional property descriptors map (the { value, writable, ... } shape) that defines own properties on the new object.

let rabbit = Object.create(animal, {
  jumps: { value: true }
});
rabbit.jumps; // true

cards.question: How do you make a full clone that preserves prototype and descriptors?
cards.answer:
Feed Object.create the source's prototype and all its descriptors. Unlike spread or Object.assign, this keeps the prototype and exact descriptors (getters/setters, non-enumerable, non-writable) instead of flattening to plain data.

let clone = Object.create(
  Object.getPrototypeOf(obj),
  Object.getOwnPropertyDescriptors(obj)
);
// same prototype and every property exactly as on obj

cards.question: What does Object.create(null) give you?
cards.answer:
A prototype-less object, useful as a pure dictionary. It has no inherited methods like toString, and a key named __proto__ behaves as ordinary data instead of a magic accessor.

let dict = Object.create(null);
Object.getPrototypeOf(dict); // null
dict.toString;               // undefined
dict["__proto__"] = 123;
dict["__proto__"];           // 123  (just data, no accessor)

cards.question: What is the modern prototype API for reading and changing a prototype?
cards.answer:
Object.getPrototypeOf(obj) reads a prototype; Object.setPrototypeOf(obj, proto) changes it. With Object.create they are the standard API, replacing the legacy __proto__ getter/setter (which lives in spec Annex B for web compatibility).

let animal = { eats: true };
let rabbit = Object.create(animal);

Object.getPrototypeOf(rabbit) === animal; // true

Object.setPrototypeOf(rabbit, { hops: true });
rabbit.hops; // true
rabbit.eats; // undefined (prototype swapped)

cards.question: Why avoid Object.setPrototypeOf on a live object?
cards.answer: Engines optimize for a fixed prototype chain, so changing it after creation is a deopt that slows every later property access on that object. When you know the prototype up front, create the object with it via Object.create instead of mutating later.

cards.question: Why is "__proto__" a risky key on a plain object?
cards.answer: On a plain object, "__proto__" is not a normal key — it is an accessor inherited from Object.prototype. Assigning to it changes the object's prototype instead of storing data.

cards.question: What is prototype pollution and how does it happen?
cards.answer:
When user-supplied names are written straight into a plain object, the key "__proto__" hits the inherited setter: a non-object value is silently ignored (data lost); an object value replaces the prototype — prototype pollution, where injected keys appear as "inherited" properties.

let store = {};
let key = "__proto__";          // came from user input

store[key] = "my value";        // non-object: setter ignores it
store[key];                     // Object.prototype — data is gone

store[key] = { admin: true };   // object: prototype replaced
store.admin;                    // true — polluted, looks inherited

cards.question: How do you safely store untrusted keys like "__proto__"?
cards.answer:
Use a container where "__proto__" is just data: a prototype-less object (no inherited accessor) or a Map, whose keys never touch the chain.

let dict = Object.create(null);
dict["__proto__"] = "my value";
dict["__proto__"];              // "my value" — plain data

let map = new Map();
map.set("__proto__", "my value");
map.get("__proto__");           // "my value"

cards.question: What is a class in JavaScript?
cards.answer:
A template for creating objects: a constructor that initializes each instance, plus methods shared by all of them. new ClassName(...) creates the object and runs the constructor.

class User {
  constructor(name) {
    this.name = name;
  }
  sayHi() {
    console.log(this.name);
  }
}

let user = new User("John");
user.sayHi(); // John

cards.question: Under the hood, what does `class User {...}` actually create?
cards.answer:
A class is a kind of function. It does two things: creates a function named User from the constructor code, and puts the methods into User.prototype.

typeof User;          // "function"
User.prototype.sayHi; // the method

cards.question: How do class instances reach their methods?
cards.answer:
Through the prototype chain — methods live on User.prototype, which is the instance's [[Prototype]].

Object.getPrototypeOf(user) === User.prototype; // true

cards.question: Can classes use computed method names?
cards.answer:
Yes — classes support getters/setters and computed method names, both written to the prototype.

class Greeter {
  ['say' + 'Hi']() { console.log("Hello"); }
}
new Greeter().sayHi(); // Hello

cards.question: Is class just syntactic sugar over constructor functions?
cards.answer:
Mostly — but not entirely. A class is a function with three real differences: it can't be called without new, its methods are non-enumerable, and all its code runs in strict mode.

Must use new. A class function carries the internal flag [[IsClassConstructor]]: true, and calling it without new throws — a plain constructor function would just run:

class User { constructor() {} }
User(); // TypeError: Class constructor User cannot be invoked without 'new'

Non-enumerable methods. Class methods get enumerable: false, so for..in over an instance doesn't drag the methods in.

Always strict. Code inside the class construct is automatically in strict mode.

cards.question: Can a class be used as a value, like a function expression?
cards.answer:
Yes. Like functions, classes are values: you can assign them to variables, pass them around, return them — and write class expressions, anonymous or named.

let User = class {
  sayHi() { console.log("Hello"); }
};

new User().sayHi(); // Hello

A named class expression works like a named function expression: the name is visible only inside the class itself — handy for self-reference, invisible outside:

let User2 = class MyClass {
  sayHi() { console.log(MyClass); } // sees itself
};

new User2().sayHi(); // shows the class definition
// MyClass;          // ReferenceError — name not visible here

Since classes are values, a function can build them on demand:

function makeClass(phrase) {
  return class {
    sayHi() { console.log(phrase); }
  };
}

let User3 = makeClass("Hello");
new User3().sayHi(); // Hello

cards.question: What is a class field?
cards.answer:
name = value written directly in the class body — creates an own property on each instance, not on the prototype.

class User {
  name = "John"; // field: own per instance
  sayHi() {      // method: shared on prototype
    console.log(`Hello, ${this.name}!`);
  }
}

new User().name;     // "John"
User.prototype.name; // undefined

cards.question: When is a class field's value evaluated?
cards.answer: Per instance, at construction time. The value can be any expression, including function calls — each instance gets its own evaluation.

cards.question: How does an arrow-function class field fix lost `this` in callbacks?
cards.answer:
An arrow takes this from where it was created — the instance under construction — so it survives being passed as a callback.

class Button {
  constructor(value) { this.value = value; }
  click = () => {
    console.log(this.value);
  };
}

let button = new Button("hello");
setTimeout(button.click, 1000); // hello — this survived

cards.question: What is the trade-off of the bound-method (arrow field) pattern?
cards.answer: Each instance carries its own copy of the function instead of sharing one prototype method.

cards.question: What does `extends` do under the hood?
cards.answer:
class Rabbit extends Animal sets Rabbit.prototype.[[Prototype]] to Animal.prototype. Inheritance is just the prototype chain — a method missing on the instance and Rabbit.prototype is looked up on Animal.prototype.

class Animal {
  run(speed) { console.log(`runs ${speed}`); }
}
class Rabbit extends Animal {
  hide() { console.log("hides!"); }
}

let rabbit = new Rabbit();
rabbit.run(5); // found on Animal.prototype
rabbit.hide(); // found on Rabbit.prototype

cards.question: How do you override a parent method?
cards.answer: Define one with the same name in the child — lookup stops at the first match.

cards.question: How do you build on the parent method instead of replacing it?
cards.answer:
Call it with super inside the override.

class Rabbit extends Animal {
  stop() {
    super.stop(); // parent logic first
    this.hide();  // then extend it
  }
}

cards.question: Do arrow functions have their own `super`?
cards.answer: No — like this, they take super from the enclosing method, which is exactly what you want in a callback inside a method.

cards.question: What can follow `extends`?
cards.answer: Any expression — e.g. a function call that returns a class.

cards.question: Why must a derived constructor call `super()` before using `this`?
cards.answer:
In a derived class the parent constructor is the one that creates the object. Until super(...) runs there is no this yet — touching it throws.

class Rabbit extends Animal {
  constructor(name, earLength) {
    super(name);                // creates this
    this.earLength = earLength; // now safe
  }
}

cards.question: What's the internal difference between a base and a derived constructor?
cards.answer: A regular (base) constructor called with new creates an empty object as this. A derived one is marked [[ConstructorKind]]: "derived" and skips that step — it expects the base constructor to do it via super().

cards.question: What error do you get if you use `this` before `super()` in a derived constructor?
cards.answer: ReferenceError: this is not defined — whether you skip super() entirely or touch this above it.

cards.question: What happens if a derived class declares no constructor?
cards.answer:
The engine generates one that passes everything through — which is why simple subclasses work without writing a constructor.

constructor(...args) { super(...args); }

cards.question: Why does a parent constructor see its own field value, not the child's overriding field?
cards.answer:
Class fields initialize at different moments: base fields before the base constructor body, derived fields only after super() returns. So while the parent constructor runs, the child's fields don't exist yet.

class Animal {
  name = 'animal';
  constructor() { console.log(this.name); }
}
class Rabbit extends Animal {
  name = 'rabbit';
}
new Rabbit(); // animal — not rabbit

cards.question: What is the exact init sequence for `new Rabbit()` when both classes have a `name` field?
cards.answer: derived constructor starts → super() → base fields init (name='animal') → base body runs (logs animal) → returns → then Rabbit's fields init (name='rabbit').

cards.question: Do overridden methods have the same field-timing problem in constructors?
cards.answer:
No — a method lives on the prototype, so lookup finds the child's version immediately, even when called from the parent constructor.

class Animal2 {
  showName() { console.log('animal'); }
  constructor() { this.showName(); }
}
class Rabbit2 extends Animal2 {
  showName() { console.log('rabbit'); }
}
new Rabbit2(); // rabbit

cards.question: If a parent constructor needs a value the child customizes, how should you expose it?
cards.answer: Via a method (or constructor parameter), not a field — methods resolve to the child's version during construction, fields don't.

cards.question: How does `super` find the parent method?
cards.answer:
Through [[HomeObject]] — a hidden property on functions written with method syntax, remembering the object/class they were defined in. super.method() looks in [[HomeObject]].[[Prototype]], independent of this.

let rabbit = {
  __proto__: animal,
  eat() {        // [[HomeObject]] = rabbit
    super.eat(); // looks in rabbit's prototype -> animal
  }
};
rabbit.eat(); // Animal eats

cards.question: Why can't `super` just use `this.__proto__.method.call(this)`?
cards.answer: this always points at the original instance, so in a chain two levels deep the "parent" computed from this is the same object every time — the call loops forever. super needs an anchor fixed at definition time: [[HomeObject]].

cards.question: Why are methods using `super` not free to copy onto another object?
cards.answer: [[HomeObject]] is permanent — copy such a method elsewhere and its super still resolves against the original home, giving the wrong parent.

cards.question: Does `super` work inside a function-property method (`method: function(){}`)?
cards.answer: No — only method syntax method() {...} gets a [[HomeObject]]. A function property has none, so super inside it is an error.

cards.question: Can you extend built-in classes like Array or Error?
cards.answer:
Yes — class PowerArray extends Array works like normal inheritance, with a bonus: built-in methods like map and filter return objects of the subclass, not plain Array.

class PowerArray extends Array {
  isEmpty() {
    return this.length === 0;
  }
}

let arr = PowerArray.of(1, 2, 5, 10, 50);
let filtered = arr.filter(item => item >= 10);

filtered.isEmpty(); // false — filter returned a PowerArray

That happens because built-in methods use the object's constructor to create the result — so subclass methods keep working on derived arrays.

Extending Error follows the same pattern; the one extra step is setting name, since the parent only sets message:

class ValidationError extends Error {
  constructor(message) {
    super(message);              // parent sets this.message
    this.name = "ValidationError";
  }
}

throw new ValidationError("No field: age");

cards.question: What is Symbol.species for?
cards.answer:
A static getter Symbol.species tells built-in methods like map and filter which constructor to use for their results. Return Array from it, and a subclass's methods produce plain arrays again.

class PowerArray extends Array {
  isEmpty() {
    return this.length === 0;
  }

  static get [Symbol.species]() {
    return Array; // use plain Array for derived results
  }
}

let arr = PowerArray.of(1, 2, 5, 10, 50);
arr.isEmpty(); // false — arr itself is still a PowerArray

let filtered = arr.filter(item => item >= 10);
filtered.isEmpty; // undefined — filter built a plain Array

Use it when you want the custom behavior on objects you create directly, but don't want it to propagate through every derived result. Other built-ins, like Map and Set, respect Symbol.species the same way.

cards.question: Do built-in classes inherit static methods from each other?
cards.answer:
No. Built-ins inherit instance methods through their prototypes, but the classes themselves are not linked — so Array doesn't get Object's statics. That's why Object.keys exists but Array.keys-style statics must be defined on each class, like Array.isArray.

// instance side: linked
Array.prototype.__proto__ === Object.prototype; // true
// so arrays get hasOwnProperty, toString, ...

// static side: NOT linked
Array.__proto__ === Object; // false
// so Array doesn't inherit Object.keys, Object.entries, ...

This is the one place built-ins differ from extends. class Rabbit extends Animal builds two chains — Rabbit.__proto__ === Animal for statics and Rabbit.prototype.__proto__ === Animal.prototype for instance methods. Built-ins only have the second one.

cards.question: What are static methods and properties?
cards.answer:
Members that belong to the class itself, not instances: User.staticMethod(), not user.staticMethod(). Inside a static method, this is the class. They hold logic about the class as a whole.

class Article {
  static compare(a, b) { return a.date - b.date; }
  static publisher = "Ilya Kantor";
}
articles.sort(Article.compare);
Article.publisher; // "Ilya Kantor"

cards.question: In a static factory method, what does `this` refer to?
cards.answer:
The class itself, so new this(...) builds an instance of it.

class Article {
  static createTodays() {
    return new this("Today's digest", new Date()); // this == Article
  }
}
let article = Article.createTodays();

cards.question: Are static members inherited?
cards.answer:
Yes. extends builds two prototype chains — one between the classes (statics), one between their prototypes (instance methods).

class Animal { static planet = "Earth"; }
class Rabbit extends Animal {}

Rabbit.planet; // "Earth"
Rabbit.__proto__ === Animal;                     // statics chain
Rabbit.prototype.__proto__ === Animal.prototype; // methods chain

cards.question: What are the two levels of hiding class members?
cards.answer: An underscore prefix (_water) marks a member protected by convention only; #water is a private field the language enforces — readable only inside the class that declared it.

cards.question: What does a `_name` underscore prefix actually enforce?
cards.answer:
Nothing — it's convention. It says "internal, use the accessors," but outside code can still touch it. Subclasses can use it too, which is the point of "protected".

class CoffeeMachine {
  _waterAmount = 0;
  set waterAmount(value) {
    if (value < 0) value = 0;
    this._waterAmount = value;
  }
  get waterAmount() { return this._waterAmount; }
}

cards.question: How do you make a property read-only from outside?
cards.answer: Provide a getter without a setter.

cards.question: How strictly are `#` private fields enforced?
cards.answer:
By the engine. Access from outside the class — or from a subclass — is a syntax error, with no dynamic back door (this['#name'] doesn't work).

class CoffeeMachine2 {
  #waterLimit = 200;
  fill(amount) {
    if (amount > this.#waterLimit) amount = this.#waterLimit;
  }
}
new CoffeeMachine2().#waterLimit; // SyntaxError

cards.question: Why encapsulate class internals at all?
cards.answer: Users can't break internal state, you can refactor internals freely, and the public surface stays small and documented.

cards.question: What are private methods?
cards.answer:
Private methods extend the # rule from fields to behavior: #method() is callable only from inside the class that declared it.

Same enforcement as private fields: invisible outside, unavailable to subclasses, and no dynamic back door — this['#method'] doesn't work. Use them for internal steps that shouldn't be part of the API. Private getters/setters and static #members work too:

class CoffeeMachine {
  #waterAmount = 0;

  #checkWater(value) {       // private method
    if (value < 0) throw new Error("Negative water");
    return value;
  }

  fill(amount) {
    this.#waterAmount = this.#checkWater(amount);
  }
}

new CoffeeMachine().#checkWater; // SyntaxError

The gotcha: privacy is per-class. Even a subclass can't touch the parent's # members — if subclasses need access, use the _protected convention instead.

cards.question: What is a static initialization block?
cards.answer:
A static {} block runs once, when the class is defined — for static setup too complex for a single static x = value initializer.

A static property handles one value; a static block handles initialization that needs statements — try/catch, loops, deriving several values together. this inside is the class:

class Config {
  static mode;
  static retries;

  static {
    let env = globalThis.process?.env ?? {};
    this.mode = env.MODE ?? "dev";
    this.retries = this.mode === "dev" ? 0 : 3;
  }
}

Config.mode; // computed once, at class definition

A class may have several static blocks; they run in order, top to bottom, exactly once. This is ES2022 — check your target environment.

cards.question: How does `instanceof` work?
cards.answer:
obj instanceof Class walks the object's prototype chain asking at each step: is this Class.prototype? One match → true. So it respects inheritance.

class Animal {}
class Rabbit extends Animal {}
let rabbit = new Rabbit();

rabbit instanceof Rabbit; // true — first link
rabbit instanceof Animal; // true — second link

cards.question: Does `instanceof` check the constructor function?
cards.answer: No — only Class.prototype matters. The same test can be written as Class.prototype.isPrototypeOf(obj).

cards.question: What breaks `instanceof` for existing objects?
cards.answer:
Reassigning the prototype — existing instances still point at the old one, so they stop matching.

function Rabbit2() {}
let r = new Rabbit2();
Rabbit2.prototype = {};
r instanceof Rabbit2; // false

cards.question: How can a class customize what instanceof returns?
cards.answer:
By defining a static Symbol.hasInstance method. If the class has one, obj instanceof Class calls it instead of walking the prototype chain.

class Animal {
  static [Symbol.hasInstance](obj) {
    if (obj.canEat) return true;
  }
}

let obj = { canEat: true };
obj instanceof Animal; // true — duck-typing check, no chain involved

This turns instanceof into whatever check you want — here, "does it have canEat?" rather than "was it created by Animal?". Most classes don't define it, so the default chain walk applies.

cards.question: What does Object.prototype.toString tell you, and how does Symbol.toStringTag change it?
cards.answer:
Object.prototype.toString.call(x) returns [object Type] for anything — primitives, built-ins, even null — a "typeof on steroids". Symbol.toStringTag lets your own objects pick the name in that string.

let s = Object.prototype.toString;
s.call([]);   // [object Array]
s.call(123);  // [object Number]
s.call(null); // [object Null]

let user = { [Symbol.toStringTag]: "User" };
s.call(user); // [object User]

Picking the tool:

works forreturns
typeofprimitivesstring
{}.toStringprimitives, built-ins, tagged objectsstring
instanceofobjects, with inheritanceboolean

So when you need a type as a string and typeof is too coarse for objects, {}.toString is the answer; when you need a hierarchy-aware boolean, use instanceof.

cards.question: What is a mixin and why use one?
cards.answer: A class can extend only one class. A mixin is an object of methods that other classes copy in — added behavior without joining the inheritance chain.

cards.question: How do you apply a mixin to a class?
cards.answer:
One line — copy its methods into the prototype with Object.assign.

let sayHiMixin = {
  sayHi() { console.log(`Hello ${this.name}`); },
  sayBye() { console.log(`Bye ${this.name}`); }
};
Object.assign(User.prototype, sayHiMixin);
new User("Dude").sayHi(); // Hello Dude

cards.question: Does using a mixin use up the single `extends` slot?
cards.answer: No — a mixin isn't inheritance, just copied methods, so class User extends Person plus a mixin works fine.

cards.question: Can a mixin method use `super`?
cards.answer: Yes — a mixin can have its own internal inheritance. The method's [[HomeObject]] points at the mixin, so super resolves through the mixin's prototype, not the consuming class's.

cards.question: What is the event-mixin pattern?
cards.answer:
A classic mixin giving any class on/off/trigger.

Object.assign(Menu.prototype, eventMixin);
let menu = new Menu();
menu.on("select", value => console.log(`Selected: ${value}`));
menu.choose("123"); // fires trigger("select", "123")

cards.question: What is the risk of mixins?
cards.answer: A mixin method can silently overwrite an existing class method with the same name — name carefully. Reach for mixins when several unrelated classes need the same capability and inheritance can't express it.

cards.question: How does JavaScript store numbers?
cards.answer:
Regular numbers are stored in 64-bit IEEE-754 format — "double precision" floats. There is no separate integer type: 5 and 5.5 are the same kind of value.

The 64 bits split into 1 sign bit, 11 bits for the exponent (where the decimal point sits), and 52 bits for the digits themselves. Those 52 bits set the limits: integers are exact only up to 2**53 - 1, and most fractions are stored as close approximations.

typeof 5;   // "number"
typeof 5.5; // "number" — one type for everything

Two consequences worth flagging now: the sign has its own bit, so there are two zeros (0 and -0, equal in almost every operation), and integers beyond 2**53 - 1 need BigInt — both covered later.

cards.question: Why are there two zeros, 0 and -0?
cards.answer:
Because the sign lives in its own bit, zero exists in two signed forms: 0 and -0. They behave identically in almost every operation — === says they're equal.

-0 === 0;        // true
String(-0);      // "0"

Object.is(0, -0); // false — the one detector that tells them apart
1 / -0;           // -Infinity — the sign survives division

-0 appears when a negative result rounds or shrinks to zero: -1 * 0, Math.round(-0.4), -1e-323 / 10.

In practice you can ignore it — only Object.is and division-by-zero ever expose the sign.

cards.question: How do you write big and small numbers readably?
cards.answer:
Append e with the zero count: 1e9 is 1 with 9 zeros, 1e-6 is 1 divided by a million. Underscores between digits are ignored — pure visual grouping.

let billion = 1e9;        // 1000000000
let ms = 1e-6;            // 0.000001
let same = 1_000_000_000; // === 1e9

1.23e6; // 1230000 — the part before e can have a fraction

e multiplies by 10 to the given power: 1e3 === 1 * 1000, 1e-3 === 1 / 1000.

Underscores exist only in literals — conversions don't understand them: Number("1_000") is NaN.

cards.question: What are hex, binary, and octal literals?
cards.answer:
The prefixes 0x, 0b, and 0o write a number in base 16, 2, or 8 directly in code.

0xff;       // 255 — hexadecimal (case doesn't matter: 0xFF)
0b11111111; // 255 — binary
0o377;      // 255 — octal

Hex is the one you'll actually meet — colors, character codes. These are just notations for writing a value: the stored number is a plain number and prints in decimal — alert(0xff) shows 255.

For any other base there's no literal — use parseInt(str, base) to read and num.toString(base) to write (both covered later).

cards.question: Why is 0.1 + 0.2 not equal to 0.3?
cards.answer:
Because 0.1 and 0.2 have no exact binary form — in base 2 they are endless repeating fractions, like 1/3 in decimal. The engine stores the nearest representable value, and the two tiny errors add up to something visibly off.

0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3; // false
0.1 + 0.2;        // 0.30000000000000004

(0.1).toFixed(20); // "0.10000000000000000555" — the stored value isn't exactly 0.1

This isn't a JavaScript bug — every language using IEEE-754 floats (Java, C, Python, Ruby) gives the same result. Binary simply can't store decimal tenths exactly, the same way decimal can't store 1/3.

cards.question: How do you handle precision loss in practice?
cards.answer:
Round explicitly at the moment you need a clean value — toFixed for display — and for money, work in integer units like cents so fractions never appear.

let sum = 0.1 + 0.2;
sum.toFixed(2);  // "0.30" — string, ready to show
+sum.toFixed(2); // 0.3 — unary plus turns it back into a number

// money: 10 + 20 cents, not 0.1 + 0.2 dollars
(10 + 20) / 100; // 0.3 exactly

The multiply-round-divide trick ((0.1 * 10 + 0.2 * 10) / 10) reduces the error but doesn't eliminate it — the scaled values are still floats. The reliable fixes are rounding the final result or staying in integers.

cards.question: What is a safe integer?
cards.answer:
An integer that fits exactly in the 52 digit bits — anything up to Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER = 2**53 - 1 = 9007199254740991. Beyond it, not every integer exists: values silently snap to the nearest representable one.

9999999999999999; // 10000000000000000 — off by one, no error

Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER + 1; // 9007199254740992
Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER + 2; // 9007199254740992 — same! the gaps began

"Safe" means every integer in the range has its own unique representation, so arithmetic and === behave as expected. Past the limit, adjacent integers share one stored value and equality checks lie.

Number.isSafeInteger(x) tests for it. The real-world hit: 64-bit database IDs arriving in JSON — parse them as strings or BigInt, never as numbers.

cards.question: How do you compare two floats for equality?
cards.answer:
Not with === — check that the difference is smaller than a tolerance. Number.EPSILON is the standard one: the gap between 1 and the next representable number.

0.1 + 0.2 === 0.3;                          // false
Math.abs(0.1 + 0.2 - 0.3) < Number.EPSILON; // true

Number.EPSILON is about 2.22e-16 — any difference below it is float noise, not a real difference.

It's calibrated for numbers around 1. For larger values the rounding errors grow too, so scale the tolerance — or simply round both sides to the precision you actually care about.

cards.question: How can a primitive number have methods at all?
cards.answer:
The engine creates a temporary wrapper object Number, runs the method on it, and throws the wrapper away. How this boxing works in full — and why you never write new Number(...) yourself — is covered in Coercion.

One number-specific catch: on a literal, the first dot reads as a decimal point. Use two dots or parentheses:

123..toFixed(2);  // works — the second dot is the method call
(123).toFixed(2); // same, clearer

cards.question: How do you round to a whole number?
cards.answer:
Four Math methods: Math.floor rounds down, Math.ceil up, Math.round to the nearest integer, Math.trunc just cuts the fraction off.

3.13.6-1.1-1.6
Math.floor33-2-2
Math.ceil44-1-1
Math.round34-1-2
Math.trunc33-1-1

For positive numbers floor and trunc agree. They split on negatives: floor goes toward minus infinity, trunc toward zero — Math.floor(-1.1) is -2, Math.trunc(-1.1) is -1.

cards.question: How do you round to n decimal places?
cards.answer:
Two ways: multiply-round-divide keeps a number; toFixed(n) rounds to n digits after the point and returns a string.

let num = 1.23456;

Math.round(num * 100) / 100; // 1.23 — number
num.toFixed(2);              // "1.23" — string!
+num.toFixed(2);             // 1.23 — back to a number

toFixed rounds to the nearest value (like Math.round) and pads with zeros: (12.34).toFixed(5) → "12.34000" — handy for prices.

The trap: it rounds the stored value, not what you typed. (6.35).toFixed(1) gives "6.3", because 6.35 is stored as slightly less than 6.35.

cards.question: What does num.toString(base) do?
cards.answer:
Writes the number as a string in any base from 2 to 36 — digits first, then letters.

(255).toString(16);    // "ff" — hex: colors, character codes
(255).toString(2);     // "11111111" — binary: bitwise debugging
(123456).toString(36); // "2n9c" — base 36: shortest form, url shorteners

Without an argument it's plain decimal — the usual number-to-string conversion.

The reverse direction is parseInt(str, base): parseInt("ff", 16) → 255 (covered in Number gotchas).

cards.question: What do Math.random, Math.max/min, Math.pow, Math.abs do?
cards.answer:
Math.random(), Math.max/Math.min, Math.pow, and Math.abs handle most day-to-day math.

Math.random();       // random in [0, 1) — 1 excluded
Math.max(3, 5, -10); // 5 — any number of arguments
Math.min(1, 2);      // 1
Math.pow(2, 10);     // 1024 — same as 2 ** 10
Math.abs(-5);        // 5

The classic recipe — a random integer from min to max inclusive:

Math.floor(Math.random() * (max - min + 1)) + min;

For an array, spread it: Math.max(...arr) — these methods take separate arguments, not arrays.

cards.question: What are Infinity and NaN?
cards.answer:
Special values that belong to the number type. Infinity is greater than any number; NaN means a failed math operation. Math in JavaScript is "safe" — it never throws, it returns these instead.

1 / 0;   // Infinity
-1 / 0;  // -Infinity
"a" / 2; // NaN
NaN + 1; // NaN — sticky: NaN poisons every operation it touches

One exception to the stickiness: NaN ** 0 is 1.

And the famous property — NaN is the only value not equal to itself:

NaN === NaN; // false

cards.question: What's the difference between isNaN and Number.isNaN?
cards.answer:
The global isNaN(x) converts x to a number first, so any unparseable string counts as "NaN". Number.isNaN(x) converts nothing — it's true only when x already is the value NaN. Same split for isFinite vs Number.isFinite.

isNaN("str");        // true  — "str" coerces to NaN
Number.isNaN("str"); // false — it's a string, not NaN

isFinite("15");        // true  — coerces to 15
Number.isFinite("15"); // false — not a number type

The strict Number.* versions are the modern defaults — the coercing globals answer a vaguer question and surprise you with strings. The global isFinite(value) still earns its keep as a validity check for string input: true means "converts to a regular number, not NaN/Infinity".

A third detector worth knowing: Object.is(NaN, NaN) is true — and it also tells 0 from -0.

cards.question: When do you use parseInt and parseFloat instead of Number()?
cards.answer:
When the string has units stuck to it. Number("100px") is NaN — strict conversion fails on any junk. The parse functions read a number from the start of the string and stop at the first character that doesn't fit.

parseInt("100px");    // 100
parseFloat("12.5em"); // 12.5

parseInt("12.3");     // 12 — integer only
parseFloat("12.3.4"); // 12.3 — stops at the second dot
parseInt("a123");     // NaN — nothing readable at the start

parseInt takes a radix as a second argument — useful for reading other bases without a prefix: parseInt("ff", 16) → 255, parseInt("2n9c", 36) → 123456.

cards.question: What is BigInt and when do you need it?
cards.answer:
A separate numeric type for integers of any length — no 2**53 ceiling. Create one with the n suffix or BigInt().

const big = 1234567890123456789012345678901234567890n;
const fromString = BigInt("9007199254740993"); // beyond safe — exact as bigint
const fromNumber = BigInt(10);                 // 10n

typeof 10n; // "bigint"

Use it where regular numbers silently lose digits: 64-bit database IDs, high-precision timestamps, cryptography. Elsewhere stick to regular numbers — they're simpler and faster.

cards.question: What are the rules of BigInt arithmetic?
cards.answer:
Operators work as usual between bigints — with three rules: no mixing with regular numbers, division drops the fraction, and unary plus throws.

1n + 2n; // 3n

1n + 2;         // TypeError: Cannot mix BigInt and other types
1n + BigInt(2); // 3n — convert explicitly
Number(1n) + 2; // 3

5n / 2n; // 2n — integer division, rounds toward zero

+10n;        // TypeError
Number(10n); // 10 — the conversion to use

The no-mixing rule is deliberate: silently converting either side would lose precision somewhere, so you decide which side converts. Careful converting down: Number() on a bigint over 2**53 loses digits.

cards.question: How do BigInts behave in comparisons, booleans, and JSON?
cards.answer:
Comparisons cross the type line fine; == equates them with numbers but === doesn't; 0n is falsy; and JSON.stringify throws on them.

2n > 1;   // true — comparisons mix freely
1n == 1;  // true
1n === 1; // false — different types

if (0n) {}                  // never runs — 0n is falsy
JSON.stringify({ id: 1n }); // TypeError — convert to string first

The JSON gap closes the loop on big IDs: they cross the wire as strings, become bigints in code, and go back as strings.

BigInt can't be polyfilled — operators can't be overridden — so for old targets the JSBI library is the workaround.

cards.question: What kinds of quotes does JavaScript have, and how do special characters work?
cards.answer:
Three kinds of quotes — single 'text', double "text", backticks `text` — and special characters are written with a backslash escape, like \n for a newline.

Single and double quotes are identical; backticks add expression embedding and multiline (own subtopic).

Special characters start with a backslash: \n newline, \t tab, \\ a backslash itself, and \' / \" / \` to put a quote inside the same kind of quotes.

"Hello\nWorld";  // two lines
'I\'m here';     // escaped quote
"I'm here";     // or just use the other quote kind

The backslash is consumed by the engine: "\n" is one character, and "\\" has length 1. To show an actual backslash in output, double it in the source.

cards.question: How do you get a string's length and read single characters?
cards.answer:
str.length is a property — no parentheses. Characters are read with str[i] or str.at(i).

str[i] works for plain indexes but returns undefined for negatives; str.at(i) accepts negatives and counts from the end.

let str = "Hello";
str.length;  // 5 — property, not a method
str[0];      // "H"
str[-1];     // undefined
str.at(-1);  // "o"

To walk every character, for..of iterates the string character by character:

for (let ch of "Hello") { /* H, e, l, l, o */ }

Common typo from other languages: str.length() — that calls a number and throws. No parentheses.

cards.question: Why can't you change a character in a string, and what do toUpperCase/toLowerCase return?
cards.answer:
Strings are immutable — the content can never change. Every "modifying" method, like toUpperCase/toLowerCase, returns a new string; the original stays as it was.

let str = "Hi";
str[0] = "h";   // silently ignored (TypeError in strict mode)
str;            // "Hi" — unchanged

str = "h" + str.slice(1); // the way: build a new string, reassign

Case change is the everyday example of "returns new":

"Interface".toUpperCase(); // "INTERFACE"
"Interface".toLowerCase(); // "interface"

The trap: calling str.toUpperCase() and discarding the result does nothing — assign it. Chaining is fine because each step returns a fresh string: str.trim().toLowerCase().

cards.question: How do you search for a substring?
cards.answer:
str.includes(sub) answers yes/no, str.indexOf(sub) gives the position (or -1), startsWith/endsWith check the edges.

indexOf takes a second argument — the position to search from; lastIndexOf searches from the end.

The classic trap: "not found" is -1, but a match at position 0 is falsy — so never truthy-check indexOf:

let str = "Widget";
if (str.indexOf("Widget")) { ... }        // wrong — found at 0, falsy, skipped
if (str.indexOf("Widget") !== -1) { ... } // right
if (str.includes("Widget")) { ... }       // best — boolean by design

Use indexOf only when you need the position; for a plain "is it there" question, includes.

cards.question: How do you extract part of a string — slice, substring or substr?
cards.answer:
Default to slice(start, end): from start up to (not including) end, negatives count from the end. The other two exist for history.

methodselectsnegatives
slice(start, end)from start to end (end excluded)allowed — count from the end
substring(start, end)between the indexestreated as 0; swaps reversed args
substr(start, length)length chars from startstart only; legacy, avoid
 
"stringify".slice(0, 5); // "strin"
"stringify".slice(-4);   // "gify" — last 4 characters
"stringify".slice(2);    // "ringify" — to the end

The quiet difference: substring(6, 2) swaps the arguments and works; slice(6, 2) returns "". Sticking to slice keeps behavior predictable — it does everything the others do.

cards.question: How are strings compared in JavaScript?
cards.answer: Character by character, by numeric code in the internal encoding. Comparison walks both strings from the start; the first differing pair of characters decides.

cards.question: Why is "a" > "Z" true?
cards.answer:
Because lowercase letters have higher codes than uppercase: "Z" is 90, "a" is 97 — higher wins.

"a" > "Z"; // true — code 97 vs 90

cards.question: How does string comparison handle a shared prefix?
cards.answer:
When one string is a prefix of the other, the longer string is greater.

"apple" > "app"; // true — same prefix, longer wins

cards.question: Why does code-order comparison break for real languages?
cards.answer:
Accented letters sit past the plain alphabet in the encoding, so they sort after unrelated plain letters.

"Österreich" > "Zealand"; // true — Ö's code is beyond Z

cards.question: How do you get human, language-aware string ordering?
cards.answer:
Use localeCompare instead of >/<.

"Österreich".localeCompare("Zealand"); // negative — Ö sorts before Z, correctly

cards.question: What does localeCompare return, and how do you use it in sort?
cards.answer:
Returns negative / zero / positive — drop it straight into arr.sort().

arr.sort((a, b) => a.localeCompare(b));

cards.question: How do you do a case-insensitive string comparison?
cards.answer:
Lowercase both sides first.

a.toLowerCase() === b.toLowerCase();

cards.question: What do template literals give you over quoted strings?
cards.answer:
Backtick strings embed any expression directly in the text: ${expression} is evaluated and its result becomes part of the string.

let name = "John";
let age = 30;

`Hello, ${name}!`;                   // variable in place
`1 + 2 = ${1 + 2}.`;                 // any expression
`${age >= 18 ? "adult" : "minor"}`;  // even a ternary

Versus concatenation, the text reads in one piece — no + seams, no quote juggling:

"User " + name + " is " + age;   // before
`User ${name} is ${age}`;        // after

The embedded value is converted to a string the usual way (ToString — see Coercion), so an object lands as [object Object] unless it defines its own conversion.

cards.question: How do multiline and nested templates work?
cards.answer:
Inside backticks a line break is a real character — write multiline text as-is, no \n stitching.

let list = `Guests:
 * John
 * Pete
 * Mary`;

// same with quotes: "Guests:\n * John\n * Pete\n * Mary"

Both quote characters are free inside backticks — no escaping ' or ". And templates nest: an embedded expression can itself be a template:

`Result: ${ ok ? `yes, ${count}` : "no" }`

Watch the indentation: every space at the start of a continued line is part of the string. Indented source code means indented output.

cards.question: What is a tagged template?
cards.answer:
A function name placed before a template — tag`...` — makes the engine call that function with the literal chunks and the embedded values as separate arguments.

The tag receives the text pieces as an array, then each ${} value on its own:

function tag(strings, ...values) {
  // strings: ["Hello ", ", you are ", "!"]
  // values:  ["John", 30]
  return strings[0] + values[0].toUpperCase()
       + strings[1] + values[1] + strings[2];
}

let name = "John";
tag`Hello ${name}, you are ${30}!`; // "Hello JOHN, you are 30!"

The point: the tag sees the values before they merge into the string, so it can escape, transform, or route them. That's how HTML-escaping templates, SQL parameter binding, i18n libraries, and styled-components work — the syntax of a string, the safety of a function.

cards.question: What are raw strings and String.raw?
cards.answer:
Inside a tag function, strings.raw holds the chunks exactly as typed — stays two characters, backslashes untouched. The built-in String.raw tag returns the whole string in that raw form.

alert(`C:
ew	able`);           // broken: 
 became a newline, 	 a tab
alert(String.raw`C:
ew	able`); // C:
ew	able — kept as typed

Handy whenever backslashes are data, not escapes: Windows paths, regex sources written as strings, LaTeX.

So every tag gets two views of the text — strings (escapes processed) and strings.raw (as written) — and chooses which to build from.

cards.question: How does JavaScript store strings internally?
cards.answer:
As UTF-16: a string is a sequence of 16-bit code units. Each common character takes one unit; rare ones take two.

You can write a character by its code: \uXXXX for exactly 4 hex digits, \u{…} for any code point:

"\u00e9";    // "é"
"\u{1F60D}"; // "😍" — code point too big for 4 digits

charCodeAt(i) reads one 16-bit unit at position i. For everyday Latin/Cyrillic text one unit = one character, so length and indexes match what you see — the mismatch starts with emoji and rare symbols (next card).

cards.question: Why does '😂'.length equal 2?
cards.answer:
Unicode has more characters than 16 bits can hold, so emoji and rare symbols are stored as a surrogate pair — two code units. length counts units, not characters.

'😂'.length; // 2 — one symbol, two code units
'𝒳'[0];      // half a pair — garbage, not a character
'𝒳'.charCodeAt(0).toString(16); // "d835" — first unit only

The pair-aware tools: codePointAt reads the whole character, String.fromCodePoint builds one, and for..of / spread iterate by real characters:

'𝒳'.codePointAt(0).toString(16); // "1d4b3" — the full character
String.fromCodePoint(0x1d4b3);   // '𝒳'

[...'Hi😂'].length;              // 3 — spread walks characters
for (let ch of '😂') { ... }     // one iteration, whole emoji

Rule of thumb: any code that slices or measures user text by raw index can cut a pair in half. Iterate with for..of or spread into an array first.

cards.question: Why can two identical-looking strings compare as not equal?
cards.answer:
The same visible glyph can be composed differently — one precomposed character, or a base letter plus combining marks. Different code units, so == says false.

let s1 = 'S\u0307\u0323'; // Ṩ — S + dot above + dot below
let s2 = 'S\u0323\u0307'; // Ṩ — same look, marks in other order

s1 == s2;                         // false
s1.normalize() == s2.normalize(); // true

normalize() brings every variant to one canonical form. Run it before comparing, deduplicating, or hashing user-entered text — otherwise "the same" name can exist twice in your database.

cards.question: How do you replace all occurrences of a substring?
cards.answer:
str.replaceAll(search, replacement). Plain replace with a string replaces only the first match — the classic surprise.

"a-b-c".replace("-", "+");    // "a+b-c" — first only!
"a-b-c".replaceAll("-", "+"); // "a+b+c"

Before ES2021 the workarounds were a /g regex or split("-").join("+") — replaceAll retires both.

One rule: if you pass replaceAll a regex instead of a string, it must have the g flag, or it throws.

cards.question: How do padStart, padEnd and repeat work?
cards.answer:
padStart/padEnd pad the string with another string until it reaches a target length — at the beginning or the end; repeat(n) returns the string repeated n times.

"5".padStart(2, "0");  // "05"   — clock digits
"7".padStart(4, "0");  // "0007" — invoice numbers
"abc".padEnd(6, ".");  // "abc..." — aligned columns

The pad string repeats and is cut to fit exactly; if the string is already long enough, it comes back unchanged. Default pad is a space.

For plain repetition there's str.repeat(n):

"ab".repeat(3); // "ababab"
"-".repeat(20); // a divider line

cards.question: How do you trim whitespace from one side only?
cards.answer:
trimStart() removes leading whitespace, trimEnd() trailing; trim() does both.

"  hi  ".trimStart(); // "hi  "
"  hi  ".trimEnd();   // "  hi"
"  hi  ".trim();      // "hi"

trimLeft/trimRight are the old non-standard aliases — use the Start/End names. And like every string method, these return a new string; the original is untouched.

cards.question: What is a regular expression and how do you create one?
cards.answer:
A regular expression is a search pattern plus optional flags. There are two ways to create one: the literal /pattern/flags and the constructor new RegExp("pattern", "flags").

let re1 = /ab+c/i;                  // literal — pattern known at write time
let re2 = new RegExp("ab+c", "i");  // constructor — pattern built at runtime

The literal uses slashes as delimiters, the way quotes delimit strings — and like a string literal, it's fully static: you can't insert variables into it. When the pattern depends on data, build it as a string:

let tag = prompt("What tag?", "h2");
let re = new RegExp(`<${tag}>`);   // /<h2>/ from user input

cards.question: What do the regexp flags do?
cards.answer:
Flags change how the pattern searches. Six core flags — i, g, m, s, u, y — plus two newer ones: d and v.

flageffect
icase-insensitive — A and a are the same
gfind all matches, not just the first
mmultiline — ^ and $ work per line
s"dotall" — lets . match newlines
ufull Unicode support — surrogate pairs, \p{...}
ysticky — match at an exact position only
d"hasIndices" — match results also carry start/end positions of each group in .indices
v"unicodeSets" — an upgraded u: set operations inside [...] and properties of strings
 
"We will, we will".match(/we/gi); // ["We", "we"] — all matches, any case
"We will, we will".match(/we/i);  // ["We"] + index/input — first only

g is the one you'll reach for most — without it, methods stop at the first match.

cards.question: Which characters need escaping — and why does new RegExp need double backslashes?
cards.answer:
These have special meaning and need a backslash to be matched literally: [ ] { } ( ) \ ^ $ . | ? * +. In a regex literal, the slash / too — it's the delimiter.

/\d\.\d/.test("5.1");  // true  — \. is a literal dot
/\d.\d/.test("5A1");   // true too — unescaped . matches anything

"function g()".match(/g\(\)/); // "g()"
"/usr/bin".match(/\/usr/);     // slash escaped in a literal

With new RegExp there's a second layer: string quotes consume backslashes first. In the string "\d", the quotes see an unknown escape and strip the backslash — the constructor receives just "d". So every regex backslash must be doubled:

new RegExp("\d\.\d").test("5.1");   // false — pattern became d.d
new RegExp("\\d\\.\\d").test("5.1"); // true — \\ survives as \

Rule: literal syntax → single escaping; constructor syntax → double escaping.

cards.question: What are the main character classes?
cards.answer:
A character class is shorthand for "any character from a certain set". The three core ones: \d — a digit, \s — a space character (space, tab \t, newline \n…), \w — a "wordly" character: a Latin letter, digit, or underscore.

"+7(903)-123-45-67".match(/\d/);   // "7" — first digit
"+7(903)-123-45-67".match(/\d/g);  // all digits
"+7(903)-123-45-67".replace(/\D/g, ""); // "79031234567"

Each class has an uppercase inverse: \D — any non-digit, \S — any non-space, \W — any non-wordly character.

Classes mix freely with regular characters — \d\d:\d\d matches a time like 09:30. Two things to keep in mind:

"1 - 5".match(/\d-\d/);   // null — the spaces aren't in the pattern
"1 - 5".match(/\d - \d/); // works: in a regexp all characters matter, spaces too

And \w means Latin letters only — Cyrillic or hieroglyphs don't count as wordly.

cards.question: What does the dot match?
cards.answer:
The dot . is "any character except a newline". It's a class like \d — just the broadest one.

"CSS4".match(/CS.4/);  // "CSS4"
"CS-4".match(/CS.4/);  // "CS-4"
"CS 4".match(/CS.4/);  // "CS 4" — space is a character too

It matches "any character", not "absence of a character" — there must be something there:

"CS4".match(/CS.4/); // null — no character between S and 4

The newline exception trips people up with multiline text. Two fixes — the s flag makes the dot truly match anything, or the older idiom [\s\S] ("a space or not a space" — anything):

"A\nB".match(/A.B/);        // null
"A\nB".match(/A.B/s);       // "A\nB"
"A\nB".match(/A[\s\S]B/);   // "A\nB" — works without the flag

cards.question: How do sets and ranges work?
cards.answer:
Square brackets [...] match one character from those listed. [eao] means "e, a, or o"; a dash makes a range: [a-z], [0-5].

"Mop top".match(/[tm]op/gi);  // ["Mop", "top"]
"Voila".match(/V[oi]la/);     // null — a set is exactly one character

"Exception 0xAF".match(/x[0-9A-F][0-9A-F]/g); // ["xAF"] — ranges combine

Character classes work inside sets too — [\d\s] is "a digit or a space". In fact \d is just shorthand for [0-9], and \w for [a-zA-Z0-9_].

A caret first in the brackets excludes: [^...] matches any character except the listed ones:

"alice15@gmail.com".match(/[^\d\sA-Z]/gi); // ["@", "."]

Inside brackets most special characters lose their powers — [.,+()] needs no escaping. Only watch the dash (put it first or last so it can't form a range), a caret anywhere but the start, and ], which always needs escaping to be literal.

cards.question: What does the u flag do for Unicode characters?
cards.answer: The u flag turns on full Unicode handling: 4-byte characters (emoji, rare symbols) are treated as one character instead of two surrogate halves.

cards.question: Why does a set like /[𝒳𝒴]/ misbehave without the u flag?
cards.answer:
Without u, a surrogate-pair character is seen as two broken halves, so sets and ranges over it misbehave or throw. The u flag makes it work:

"𝒳".match(/[𝒳𝒴]/);   // wrong / error — pair split in half
"𝒳".match(/[𝒳𝒴]/u);  // "𝒳"

cards.question: What does \p{...} match, and what does it need?
cards.answer:
\p{...} matches characters by their Unicode property — far beyond Latin-only \w. The gotcha: it silently does nothing without the u flag.

"число: 123".match(/\p{L}/gu);     // all letters, any language
"x05 xAF".match(/x\p{Hex_Digit}\p{Hex_Digit}/gu); // ["x05", "xAF"]
"Prices: $2, €1".match(/\p{Sc}\d/gu); // ["$2", "€1"] — currency symbols
"Hello 你好".match(/\p{sc=Han}/gu);   // ["你", "好"] — by script

cards.question: What are the main \p{...} Unicode categories?
cards.answer:
CodeCategory
Lletters
Nnumbers
Ppunctuation
Ssymbols
Zseparators
Mcombining marks

cards.question: What quantifiers exist?
cards.answer:
A quantifier says how many times the thing before it may repeat. The explicit form is {...}; the three shorthands +, *, ? cover the common cases.

quantifiermeanssame as
{5}exactly 5—
{3,5}3 to 5—
{3,}3 or more—
+one or more{1,}
*zero or more{0,}
?zero or one (optional){0,1}
 
"I'm 12345 years old".match(/\d{5}/);    // "12345"
"+7(903)-123-45-67".match(/\d+/g);       // ["7", "903", "123", "45", "67"]
"color colour".match(/colou?r/g);        // ["color", "colour"]
"100 10 1".match(/\d0*/g);               // ["100", "10", "1"]

Quantifiers apply to one character, a class, or a parenthesized group. The general tradeoff: the more precise the regexp, the longer and more complex it gets — /\d+/ happily matches the 12 in #12; full precision needs anchors or boundaries around it.

cards.question: How do greedy and lazy quantifiers differ?
cards.answer:
Quantifiers are greedy by default: grab as many characters as possible, then backtrack — give them back one by one — until the rest fits. A ? after the quantifier makes it lazy: take as few as possible, expanding only when the rest can't match.

let str = 'a "witch" and her "broom" is one';

str.match(/".+"/g);  // ['"witch" and her "broom"'] — greedy ran to last quote, backtracked
str.match(/".+?"/g); // ['"witch"', '"broom"'] — lazy stops at first closing quote

cards.question: What's often better than greedy or lazy for matching between delimiters?
cards.answer:
An excluding set states the intent directly and doesn't backtrack at all:

str.match(/"[^"]+"/g); // ['"witch"', '"broom"'] — "anything but a quote" inside

cards.question: Why isn't lazy .*? a universal fix?
cards.answer:
Lazy .*? still expands across a delimiter if that's what makes the rest match — so it can glue together pieces of two different tags:

// matching 
//  // can span across a quote into another tag
// // reliable — can't leave the attribute

cards.question: What do ^ and $ do?
cards.answer:
The caret ^ matches at the beginning of the text, the dollar $ at the end. They are tests of position, not characters — they have zero width.

/^Mary/.test("Mary had a little lamb"); // true — starts with Mary
/snow$/.test("its fleece was white as snow"); // true — ends with snow

Together, ^...$ means "the whole string must match the pattern" — the standard shape for validation:

/^\d\d:\d\d$/.test("12:34");     // true
/^\d\d:\d\d$/.test("12:345");    // false — extra character
/^\d\d:\d\d$/.test("ok 12:34");  // false — extra prefix

Because anchors match positions rather than characters, the empty string passes /^$/ — the engine finds the start and is immediately at the end.

cards.question: What does the m flag change about anchors?
cards.answer:
With the m flag, ^ and $ match at the start and end of every line, not just the whole text.

let str = `1st place: Winnie
2nd place: Piglet
3rd place: Eeyore`;

str.match(/^\d/g);   // ["1"] — one match, start of text only
str.match(/^\d/gm);  // ["1", "2", "3"] — start of each line

"Start of a line" means "immediately after a line break \n" — the anchor tests the position around the newline without consuming it. That's the difference from matching \n directly, which grabs the actual character into the result:

let scores = `Winnie: 1
Piglet: 2
Eeyore: 3`;

scores.match(/\d$/gm); // ["1", "2", "3"] — positions before each break + text end
scores.match(/\d\n/g); // ["1\n", "2\n"] — real characters; no \n after the last line

Use anchors to test positions; use \n when you actually want the newline in the match.

cards.question: What is a word boundary \b?
cards.answer:
\b is a position test, like ^ and $: it matches where a wordly character (\w) meets a non-wordly one. It's how you match a whole word, not a fragment of a longer one.

Three positions qualify: the start of the string if it begins with \w, between a \w and a non-\w character, and the end of the string if it ends with \w.

"Hello, Java!".match(/\bJava\b/);       // "Java"
"Hello, JavaScript!".match(/\bJava\b/); // null — no boundary after "Java"

Works for numbers too — \b\d\d\b finds standalone two-digit numbers:

"1 23 456 78".match(/\b\d\d\b/g); // ["23", "78"]
"12,34,56".match(/\b\d\d\b/g);    // ["12", "34", "56"] — commas are boundaries

The limitation is inherited from \w: it only knows Latin letters, digits, and underscore — \b gives wrong results on Cyrillic or hieroglyphs.

cards.question: What are lookahead and lookbehind?
cards.answer:
Lookarounds match something only if it's (not) followed or preceded by something else — without including that context in the match. Four forms:

patternmeaning
X(?=Y)X if followed by Y
X(?!Y)X if not followed by Y
(?<=Y)XX if preceded by Y
(?<!Y)XX if not preceded by Y
 
let str = "1 turkey costs 30€";

str.match(/\d+(?=€)/);    // "30" — the € is checked but not captured
str.match(/(?<=\$)\d+/);  // price after a dollar sign
"2 turkeys cost 60€".match(/\d+\b(?!€)/g); // ["2"] — quantity, not price

Like anchors, lookarounds are zero-width: the contents of the parentheses is a test, not part of the result. If you do want the context, add a capturing group inside it:

str.match(/\d+(?=(€|kr))/); // captures "30" and "€" separately

cards.question: How do capturing groups work?
cards.answer:
Parentheses (...) do two jobs: they let a quantifier apply to a whole chunk, and they capture that chunk as a separate item in the match result.

"Gogogo now!".match(/(go)+/i); // "Gogogo" — quantifier on the group

let m = "<h1>".match(/<(.*?)>/);
m[0]; // "<h1>" — full match
m[1]; // "h1"     — what group 1 captured

Groups are numbered left to right by their opening paren — nesting doesn't change the rule. An optional group that didn't participate still holds its slot, as undefined:

let r = "ac".match(/a(z)?(c)?/);
r[1]; // undefined — (z)? matched nothing
r[2]; // "c"

Counting parens gets fragile, so two refinements:

let dateRe = /(?<year>[0-9]{4})-(?<month>[0-9]{2})/;
"2026-06".match(dateRe).groups.year; // "2026" — named group

/(?:go)+ (\w+)/i  // (?:...) groups without capturing — no slot in the result

cards.question: What are backreferences \1 and \k<name>?
cards.answer:
A backreference reuses what a group already captured, inside the pattern itself: \1 means "the exact text group 1 matched", \k<name> the same for a named group.

The classic case — quoted strings where the closing quote must match the opening one. Without a backreference, mixed quotes slip through:

let str = `He said: "She's the one!".`;

str.match(/['"](.*?)['"]/);      // `"She'` — wrong: opened with ", closed with '
str.match(/(['"])(.*?)\1/);      // `"She's the one!"` — \1 = same quote as opened
str.match(/(?<quote>['"])(.*?)\k<quote>/); // same, readable

Two gotchas: a non-capturing group (?:...) isn't memorized, so it can't be referenced. And the syntax depends on where you are — \1 in the pattern, $1 in the replacement string.

cards.question: What does alternation | match?
cards.answer:
The pipe | is OR. Unlike square brackets, which choose between single characters, alternation chooses between whole expressions.

"Java or JavaScript?".match(/Java|PHP|C\+\+/g); // ["Java", "Java"]

cards.question: What is the scope of | without parentheses?
cards.answer:
Without parentheses, | splits the entire pattern. Parentheses bound it:

/I love HTML|CSS/   // matches "I love HTML" or just "CSS"
/I love (HTML|CSS)/ // matches "I love HTML" or "I love CSS"

cards.question: In what order are | alternatives tried?
cards.answer:
Left to right — the first that fits wins. So put longer variants first, or factor them:

"JavaScript".match(/Java|JavaScript/); // "Java" — never reaches the longer one
"JavaScript".match(/JavaScript|Java/); // "JavaScript"
/Java(Script)?/                        // same idea, factored

cards.question: How do you validate an hour 00–23 with a regexp?
cards.answer:
No single range covers 00–23, so use alternation for the two cases:

/^([01]\d|2[0-3]):[0-5]\d$/.test("23:59"); // true
/^([01]\d|2[0-3]):[0-5]\d$/.test("25:99"); // false

cards.question: How do match and matchAll work?
cards.answer:
str.match(re) has two modes: without g it returns the first match as an array — full match, then capturing groups, plus index and input. With g it returns all matches as plain strings — and drops the groups.

let m = "12-34".match(/(\d+)-(\d+)/);
m[0], m[1], m[2]; // "12-34", "12", "34"  + m.index

"12-34, 56-78".match(/(\d+)-(\d+)/g); // ["12-34", "56-78"] — no groups

The trap: no matches returns null, not an empty array — .length on it throws. Guard with || []:

let results = str.match(re) || [];

str.matchAll(re) fixes both problems: it requires g, returns an iterable of full match-arrays — every one with its groups and index — and yields an empty iterable instead of null:

for (let m of "12-34, 56-78".matchAll(/(\d+)-(\d+)/g)) {
  m[1], m[2]; // "12","34" then "56","78"
}
// or: [...str.matchAll(re)]

Rule of thumb: all matches with groups → matchAll; just the strings → match with g.

cards.question: How does replace work with regexps?
cards.answer:
str.replace(re, replacement) replaces matches with a string or with whatever a function returns. With a regexp it replaces the first match — add g for all.

"12-34-56".replace(/-/, ":");  // "12:34-56"
"12-34-56".replace(/-/g, ":"); // "12:34:56"

The replacement string understands special tokens:

tokeninserts
$&the whole match
$1, $2…contents of group N
$<name>contents of a named group
$` / $'text before / after the match
$$a literal dollar
 
"John Smith".replace(/(\w+) (\w+)/, "$2, $1"); // "Smith, John"

For logic, pass a function — it gets the match, the groups, and the position, and its return value is inserted:

"html and css".replace(/html|css/g, s => s.toUpperCase()); // "HTML and CSS"

replaceAll exists mainly for plain strings (replaces all occurrences without a regexp); with a regexp it demands the g flag and behaves like replace.

cards.question: What do test, search, and split do?
cards.answer:
Three simple ways to use a regexp: re.test(str) answers "is there a match" with a boolean, str.search(re) gives the position of the first match, and str.split(re) splits the string by the pattern.

methodreturns
re.test(str)true / false
str.search(re)index of the first match, -1 if none
str.split(re, limit)array of pieces between matches
 
/love/.test("I love JavaScript");   // true
"I love JavaScript".search(/love/); // 2
"12, 34, 56".split(/,\s*/);         // ["12", "34", "56"]

search only finds the first match and ignores g — there is no way to continue from a given position with it.

cards.question: How does exec work, and why are regexps with g stateful?
cards.answer:
re.exec(str) returns one full match-array — the match, its groups, index and input. With the g flag, each call returns the next match, because the regexp stores its search position in its own lastIndex property.

let re = /(\d+)-(\d+)/g, m;
while (m = re.exec("12-34, 56-78")) {
  m[1], m[2], m.index; // groups + position, one match per call
}

That makes a regexp with g (or y) stateful — the object itself remembers where it stopped. The state isn't limited to exec: test uses the same machinery. Reuse one regexp across strings, or call it twice, and the search continues from lastIndex:

let r = /java/g;
r.test("java");  // true  — lastIndex is now 4
r.test("java");  // false — searched from position 4!

Fix: don't share global regexps between searches, or reset re.lastIndex = 0 first.

cards.question: What does the sticky y flag do?
cards.answer:
The y flag makes the regexp match exactly at lastIndex — not "starting from it, searching onward" like g does.

let str = 'let varName = "value"';
let re = /\w+/y;

re.lastIndex = 4;
re.exec(str); // "varName" — a word starts exactly at 4

re.lastIndex = 3;
re.exec(str); // null — position 3 is a space; g would have kept searching

That's the tool for parsers and lexers — "read the token at this position": you advance through the text, and a failed match means "no such token here", not somewhere later.

It's also faster: on a long text with no match nearby, g scans to the end while y checks one position and stops.

cards.question: What is catastrophic backtracking?
cards.answer:
A simple-looking regexp hangs the engine: a failing match forces it to try an exponential number of ways to split the text between nested quantifiers.

let re = /^(\w+\s?)*$/; // "words separated by optional spaces"

re.test("A good string"); // true, instant
re.test("An input string that takes a long time or even makes this regexp hang!");
// hangs — the ! can't match, so the engine backtracks forever

cards.question: Why does (\w+\s?)* blow up on a non-matching string?
cards.answer: Ambiguity: \w+ inside (...)* means one word can be split between repetitions many ways — (input), (inpu)(t), (inp)(ut)… A word of n letters splits 2n-1 ways; across words this multiplies into millions. On success the first try fits; on failure the engine tries them all.

cards.question: How do you fix catastrophic backtracking by rewriting?
cards.answer:
Remove the ambiguity so there's only one way to consume each word:

/^(\w+\s)*\w*$/ // word + mandatory space, repeated; then one last word

cards.question: How do you forbid backtracking with a lookahead in JS?
cards.answer:
JS has no possessive quantifiers, but a capture inside a lookahead emulates one — the lookahead grabs the whole word as a test, and the backreference consumes it whole, never giving letters back:

/^((?=(\w+))\2\s?)*$/ // \2 = the full word the lookahead saw — no re-splitting

cards.question: What pattern shape is the red flag for ReDoS / catastrophic backtracking?
cards.answer: Nested quantifiers over overlapping patterns: (\w+\s?)*, (.*)*, (a+)+. This is how ReDoS attacks work — feeding a crafted non-matching string to such a pattern.

cards.question: What is an array and how do you add or remove elements at the ends?
cards.answer:
An array is an ordered collection, indexed from zero. Create it with the literal []; read and write by index; at(-1) reads from the end.

let fruits = ["Apple", "Orange", "Plum"];
fruits[0];      // "Apple"
fruits.at(-1);  // "Plum" — fruits[-1] would be undefined
fruits[3] = "Lemon"; // arrays grow on assignment

Four methods work at the ends — and their speed differs sharply:

fruits.push("Pear");    // add to the end       — fast
fruits.pop();           // remove from the end  — fast
fruits.unshift("Kiwi"); // add to the start     — slow
fruits.shift();         // remove from the start — slow

push/pop only touch the last slot. shift/unshift must renumber every element — index 1 becomes 0, 2 becomes 1, and so on — which makes them 10–100× slower on big arrays. Queue-like code that shifts in a hot loop pays for it.

cards.question: What is an array under the hood, and what's special about length?
cards.answer:
An array is a regular object whose keys happen to be numbers, plus engine optimizations that assume you use it as an ordered collection. Break that assumption and the optimizations switch off.

let arr = [];
arr[9999] = 5;       // huge gap — engine falls back to slow dictionary mode
arr.age = 25;        // legal (it's an object!) but kills array optimizations

length isn't "the count of elements" — it's the greatest numeric index plus one, kept up to date automatically:

let a = [];
a[123] = "x";
a.length; // 124

And it's writable. Decreasing it truncates the array, and the cut is irreversible:

let b = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
b.length = 2;  // [1, 2]
b.length = 5;  // [1, 2, empty × 3] — the values are gone
b.length = 0;  // the simplest way to clear an array

cards.question: How should you loop over an array — and how not to?
cards.answer:
Use for..of when you need the values, a classic for when you need the index. Never for..in.

let fruits = ["Apple", "Orange", "Plum"];

for (let fruit of fruits) { ... }          // values, clean
for (let i = 0; i < fruits.length; i++) { ... } // index + value

for..in is built for plain objects: it walks all enumerable keys — including non-numeric ones an array-like or a careless library may have added — and it's 10–100× slower than the array-aware loops.

cards.question: Why doesn't == work for comparing arrays?
cards.answer:
Arrays are objects, so == compares references — two arrays are equal only if they are literally the same array.

[] == [];        // false — different objects
[0] == [0];      // false

let a = [1], b = a;
a == b;          // true — same reference

To compare contents, compare lengths and then the elements one by one in a loop.

Don't lean on coercion instead: when an array meets a primitive in +, it converts to a string by joining its elements with commas:

[1, 2] + 1; // "1,21" — the string "1,2" plus "1"

cards.question: Which array methods mutate in place vs return a new array?
cards.answer:
Half the array API changes the array in place; the other half leaves it alone and returns a new one. Knowing which side a method is on prevents "where did my data go" bugs.

mutate in placereturn a new array
push, pop, shift, unshiftslice, concat
splicemap, filter, flat, flatMap
sort, reversetoSorted, toReversed, toSpliced, with
fill, copyWithinArray.from(arr), [...arr]

cards.question: Do sort and reverse return a copy of the array?
cards.answer:
No — sort and reverse mutate and return the same array. The return value looks like a copy but isn't.

let arr = [3, 1, 2];
let sorted = arr.sort();
sorted === arr; // true — same object, original reordered too

cards.question: What do fill and copyWithin do?
cards.answer:
Both write in place. fill(value, start, end) overwrites a range with one value; copyWithin(target, start, end) copies part of the array over another part of itself.

[1, 2, 3, 4].fill(0, 1, 3);       // [1, 0, 0, 4]
[1, 2, 3, 4, 5].copyWithin(0, 3); // [4, 5, 3, 4, 5] — copied [4, 5] to the start

cards.question: How do splice, slice, and concat differ at a glance?
cards.answer: splice edits the array in place — delete, insert, replace anywhere. slice copies a portion into a new array. concat builds a new array from this one plus more items.

cards.question: How does splice(start, deleteCount, ...items) work?
cards.answer:
The swiss army knife: removes deleteCount elements at start, inserts items in their place, and returns what it removed. Negative start counts from the end.

let arr = ["I", "study", "JavaScript", "right", "now"];
arr.splice(0, 2);              // removed ["I", "study"]
arr.splice(1, 0, "and", "TS"); // deleteCount 0 — pure insertion
arr.splice(-1, 1, "today");    // negative index — from the end

cards.question: How does slice(start, end) work?
cards.answer:
Copies from start up to (not including) end into a new array; negatives allowed. arr.slice() with no arguments is the idiomatic full copy.

["t", "e", "s", "t"].slice(1, 3); // ["e", "s"]
["t", "e", "s", "t"].slice(-2);   // ["s", "t"]

cards.question: What does concat(...items) do?
cards.answer:
Builds a new array, appending values and spreading array arguments one level deep.

[1, 2].concat([3, 4], 5); // [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

cards.question: How does sort actually work?
cards.answer:
By default sort() converts elements to strings and compares them lexicographically — which mangles numbers. Pass a comparison function for any real ordering.

[1, 2, 15].sort();            // [1, 15, 2] — "15" < "2" as strings!
[1, 2, 15].sort((a, b) => a - b); // [1, 2, 15]

The comparator's contract: return a negative number if a goes first, positive if b goes first, zero if order doesn't matter. For numbers, a - b ascending and b - a descending fall out of that contract for free.

For human-language strings, delegate to localeCompare — it already returns the right sign and handles accents and non-Latin letters:

["Österreich", "Andorra", "Vietnam"].sort((a, b) => a.localeCompare(b));
// ["Andorra", "Österreich", "Vietnam"]

And remember from the mutation card: sort reorders the array itself and returns that same array — toSorted is the copy-returning variant.

cards.question: Which array search method should I reach for?
cards.answer: For primitives: includes for yes/no, indexOf for the position. For objects or conditions: find returns the element, findIndex the position, filter everything that fits.

cards.question: How do indexOf and includes differ on NaN and false?
cards.answer:
indexOf uses strict ===, so it can't find NaN (and false ≠ 0). includes handles NaN correctly.

[1, 0, false].indexOf(0);      // 1 — strict ===, so false ≠ 0
[1, 0, false].includes(false); // true

[NaN].indexOf(NaN);   // -1 — the one place === lets you down
[NaN].includes(NaN);  // true

cards.question: What does lastIndexOf do?
cards.answer: The same search as indexOf, but scanning from the end of the array toward the start.

cards.question: How do find, findIndex, findLastIndex, and filter behave with a callback?
cards.answer:
All take a predicate. find/findIndex/findLastIndex stop at the first match; filter returns every match (maybe empty).

let users = [{id:1,name:"John"},{id:2,name:"Pete"},{id:3,name:"Mary"}];

users.find(u => u.id == 2);       // {id:2,name:"Pete"} — or undefined
users.findIndex(u => u.id == 2);  // 1 — or -1
users.findLastIndex(u => u.name == "Mary"); // searches right to left
users.filter(u => u.id < 3);      // array of all matches

cards.question: What's the difference between map and forEach?
cards.answer:
map transforms: it calls the function for every element and collects the results into a new array. forEach just runs the function per element and returns undefined — it's a loop, not a transformation.

["Bilbo", "Gandalf", "Nazgul"].map(item => item.length); // [5, 7, 6]

["Bilbo", "Gandalf"].forEach((item, index) => {
  alert(`${item} is at index ${index}`);
});

cards.question: What arguments does an iteration callback receive, and what is thisArg?
cards.answer: Callbacks get (item, index, array). Most iteration methods also accept an optional thisArg after the function — it becomes this inside the callback; arrow functions made it rarely needed.

cards.question: Why can't you use the return value of forEach?
cards.answer:
forEach always returns undefined — chaining off it or expecting a result fails. Need a resulting array → map; need a side effect (logging, DOM) → forEach. Using map purely for side effects allocates an array nobody reads.

let result = arr.forEach(x => x * 2); // undefined — always
let doubled = arr.map(x => x * 2);    // the actual transform

cards.question: What do some, every, flat, and flatMap do?
cards.answer:
some asks "does at least one element pass?", every asks "do they all pass?" — both return a boolean and stop as soon as the answer is known. flat unnests arrays; flatMap is map-then-flatten in one step.

let users = [{age: 16}, {age: 25}];

users.some(u => u.age < 18);  // true  — found one, stopped
users.every(u => u.age < 18); // false
[1, [2, [3]]].flat();    // [1, 2, [3]] — one level by default
[1, [2, [3]]].flat(Infinity); // [1, 2, 3] — all the way down

["it was cold", "very cold"].flatMap(s => s.split(" "));
// ["it", "was", "cold", "very", "cold"] — map would give nested arrays

cards.question: How does reduce work?
cards.answer:
reduce boils an array down to a single value via an accumulator: each call gets the previous result and the current element, and whatever it returns becomes the accumulator for the next call.

let sum = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].reduce((acc, item) => acc + item, 0); // 15
// acc: 0 → 1 → 3 → 6 → 10 → 15

cards.question: Why should you always pass reduce an initial value?
cards.answer:
