The second argument is the initial accumulator. Without it, reduce starts from the first element — and an empty array throws TypeError.

[].reduce((acc, x) => acc + x);    // TypeError — nothing to start from
[].reduce((acc, x) => acc + x, 0); // 0 — always pass the initial value

cards.question: Can reduce build something other than a number?
cards.answer:
Yes — the accumulator can be anything (object, Map, array), which makes reduce the general "build something from a list" tool. If a plain loop reads clearer, use the loop.

let byId = users.reduce((map, user) => {
  map[user.id] = user;
  return map;
}, {});

cards.question: What does reduceRight do?
cards.answer: Exactly what reduce does, but walking the array from the end toward the start.

cards.question: What do join and Array.isArray do?
cards.answer:
arr.join(glue) glues the elements into one string; Array.isArray(value) is the reliable way to check that a value is an array.

["Bilbo", "Gandalf", "Nazgul"].join("; "); // "Bilbo; Gandalf; Nazgul"
[1, 2, 3].join("");                        // "123" — empty glue, no separators

join is the mirror of str.split: split turns a string into an array, join turns it back into a string.

A dedicated array check exists because arrays are objects — typeof can't tell them apart from plain objects:

typeof [];          // "object" — useless here
Array.isArray([]);  // true
Array.isArray({});  // false

cards.question: What are the immutable array methods (toSorted, toReversed, toSpliced, with)?
cards.answer:
ES2023 added copy-returning twins for the four mutating operations: toSorted, toReversed, toSpliced, and with (immutable index assignment). Same arguments, same behavior — but the original array is untouched.

let arr = [3, 1, 2];

arr.toSorted();        // [1, 2, 3]   arr is still [3, 1, 2]
arr.toReversed();      // [2, 1, 3]
arr.toSpliced(1, 1);   // [3, 2]      splice semantics, new array
arr.with(0, 99);       // [99, 1, 2]  arr[0] = 99, as a copy

Before these, the idiom was copy-then-mutate — still everywhere in older code:

[...arr].sort((a, b) => a - b);
arr.slice().reverse();

Why it matters: code that shares arrays — React state, props, function arguments — must not mutate them, because every holder of the reference sees the change. setItems(items.toSorted(...)) expresses that directly, with no manual copy to forget.

cards.question: What is a sparse array and why avoid it?
cards.answer:
A sparse array has "holes" — indexes with no element at all, not even undefined. They appear when you skip indexes, stretch length, or call new Array(n) with a single number.

let a = new Array(3); // length 3, zero elements — three holes
let b = [1, , 3];     // hole in the middle
let c = [];
c[5] = "x";           // holes at 0..4

Holes behave inconsistently: reading one gives undefined, but iteration methods skip them:

a[0];                    // undefined
a.map(x => 1);           // [empty × 3] — callback never ran!
a.forEach(x => count++); // count stays 0

They also push the engine off its fast path — a sparse array is stored like a dictionary, not a contiguous block. When you need "an array of n slots", fill it:

new Array(3).fill(0);                    // [0, 0, 0]
Array.from({length: 3}, (_, i) => i * 2); // [0, 2, 4] — no holes, map works

cards.question: What is an array-like object?
cards.answer:
An object with numeric indexes and a length — but none of the array methods. arguments, DOM collections, and strings are the everyday examples.

let arrayLike = {0: "Hello", 1: "World", length: 2};
arrayLike.map;  // undefined — it's a plain object
// querySelectorAll(...) — same story: indexes + length, no map/filter

cards.question: What does Array.from do?
cards.answer:
Makes a true array from an array-like or an iterable, with an optional mapping function as the second argument.

Array.from(arrayLike);               // ["Hello", "World"]
Array.from(arrayLike, s => s + "!"); // ["Hello!", "World!"]
Array.from("𝒳😂");                   // ["𝒳", "😂"] — by characters, pairs intact

cards.question: Why doesn't spread [...x] work on every array-like?
cards.answer:
Spread works through iteration, so it handles iterables only. A plain array-like has no iterator and throws.

[...arrayLike];        // TypeError — not iterable
Array.from(arrayLike); // works — from accepts both kinds

Rule: something index-y but methodless → Array.from; copying or merging real arrays → spread.

cards.question: Why use a Map instead of a plain object?
cards.answer:
A Map is a keyed collection like an object, but its keys can be of any type — objects, numbers, booleans — while an object converts every key to a string.

That string conversion is the killer: every object key collapses into "[object Object]", so objects can't be keys at all.

let john = {name: "John"};

let visits = {};
visits[john] = 123;
Object.keys(visits); // ["[object Object]"] — all objects become one key

let map = new Map();
map.set(john, 123);
map.get(john); // 123 — the object itself is the key

Maps also win on the basics: a real size property instead of counting keys, guaranteed insertion-order iteration, and no collisions with built-in keys like toString or __proto__.

Rule of thumb: object for fixed, string-keyed records; Map when keys are dynamic or not strings.

cards.question: How does a Map compare keys?
cards.answer:
With the SameValueZero rule: roughly ===, except NaN equals NaN — so even NaN works as a key.

No conversion happens, so keys keep their type: the number 1 and the string "1" are two different keys.

let map = new Map();
map.set(1, "num");
map.set("1", "str");
map.set(NaN, "nan");

map.get(1);   // "num"
map.get("1"); // "str"
map.get(NaN); // "nan" — would be unfindable with ===

Objects compare by reference: two objects with identical contents are two different keys. Only the exact same object gets you the value back.

cards.question: What is the core Map API?
cards.answer:
new Map(iterable), then set(key, value), get(key), has(key), delete(key), clear(), and the size property.

get returns undefined for a missing key. set returns the map itself, so calls chain:

let map = new Map([["a", 1]]); // constructor takes [key, value] pairs

map.set("b", 2).set("c", 3);
map.has("b"); // true
map.size;     // 3

Don't use bracket access like map[key] — it works, but treats the map as a plain object: the key becomes a string and none of the Map machinery applies. Always go through set/get.

cards.question: How do you iterate a Map?
cards.answer:
Three methods — keys(), values(), entries() — all walking in insertion order. for..of over the Map itself gives [key, value] pairs.

let recipe = new Map([
  ["cucumber", 500],
  ["tomatoes", 350],
]);

for (let [food, amount] of recipe) { ... } // entries, destructured
for (let food of recipe.keys()) { ... }
for (let amount of recipe.values()) { ... }

These methods return iterators, not arrays — spread when you need an array: [...recipe.keys()].

There's also forEach, with the value first — same signature as arrays:

recipe.forEach((value, key, map) => { ... });

Insertion order is part of the contract — unlike plain objects, where integer-like keys get sorted and you shouldn't rely on ordering at all.

cards.question: How do you convert between a Map and a plain object?
cards.answer:
Object.entries(obj) gives an array of [key, value] pairs — exactly what the Map constructor eats. Object.fromEntries goes the other way.

let map = new Map(Object.entries({name: "John", age: 30}));

let obj = Object.fromEntries(map); // {name: "John", age: 30}
// same thing, explicit: Object.fromEntries(map.entries())

fromEntries works because it accepts any iterable of pairs, and a Map iterates as pairs.

This round-trip is the interop story: keep a Map internally for its key semantics, hand a plain object to code that expects one — JSON.stringify, third-party libraries. Watch one thing: non-string Map keys get stringified on the way to an object.

cards.question: What is a Set and what is its API?
cards.answer:
A Set is a collection of unique values — each value may occur only once. Adding the same value again does nothing.

let set = new Set();
let john = {name: "John"}, mary = {name: "Mary"};

set.add(john).add(mary).add(john).add(john); // add returns the set — chainable
set.size; // 2 — duplicates silently ignored

The API: new Set(iterable), add(value), has(value), delete(value), clear(), size. Uniqueness uses the same SameValueZero rule as Map, so NaN can sit in a Set, and objects are distinct by reference.

Compare the alternative — an array of unique items needs a check before every push. Set replaces that dance with one add.

cards.question: How do you iterate a Set, and why does its forEach take three arguments?
cards.answer:
for..of or forEach, in insertion order. The forEach callback gets the value twice: (value, valueAgain, set).

let set = new Set(["oranges", "apples"]);

for (let value of set) alert(value);

set.forEach((value, valueAgain, set) => {
  alert(value); // valueAgain === value
});

The repeated argument is deliberate: it matches Map's (value, key, map) signature, so the same callback works on either collection.

For the same reason a Set also has keys(), values(), and entries() — keys and values are identical, and entries yields [value, value]. Pure Map compatibility.

cards.question: How do you deduplicate an array?
cards.answer:
Spread a Set built from the array: [...new Set(arr)]. Duplicates drop, original order stays.

The Set constructor takes any iterable and keeps each value once; the spread turns it back into an array:

let values = ["a", "b", "a", "c", "b"];
let unique = [...new Set(values)]; // ["a", "b", "c"]

Uniqueness is SameValueZero, so this works for primitives. Objects compare by reference — two objects with the same contents both survive.

cards.question: When does Set beat array search?
cards.answer:
Whenever you ask "have I seen this before?" many times: set.has(x) answers in constant time, while arr.includes(x) scans the whole array.

Inside a loop that difference compounds — array search makes the loop quadratic, Set keeps it linear:

let seen = new Set();
for (let item of items) {
  if (seen.has(item.id)) continue; // O(1), no matter how many seen
  seen.add(item.id);
  process(item);
}

Visited nodes, processed ids, allowed values: Set, not array.

cards.question: What are the Set composition methods?
cards.answer:
Sets do set theory natively: union, intersection, difference, symmetricDifference. Each returns a new Set; neither operand changes.

let frontend = new Set(["js", "css", "react"]);
let backend  = new Set(["js", "node", "sql"]);

frontend.union(backend);               // {js, css, react, node, sql}
frontend.intersection(backend);        // {js}
frontend.difference(backend);          // {css, react} — in A, not in B
frontend.symmetricDifference(backend); // {css, react, node, sql} — in one, not both

Watch difference: it's one-directional. frontend.difference(backend) and backend.difference(frontend) are different sets — for "in either but not both", that's what symmetricDifference is.

cards.question: What are the Set relationship checks?
cards.answer:
Three boolean checks: isSubsetOf — all my elements are in the other set; isSupersetOf — I contain all of theirs; isDisjointFrom — we share nothing.

let frontend = new Set(["js", "css", "react"]);

new Set(["js", "css"]).isSubsetOf(frontend);  // true
frontend.isSupersetOf(new Set(["css"]));      // true
frontend.isDisjointFrom(new Set(["go", "rust"])); // true — nothing in common

Typical use: permission checks read as a sentence — required.isSubsetOf(granted) instead of an every + has loop.

Edge worth knowing: the empty set is a subset of everything and disjoint from everything, including itself.

cards.question: What can the Set methods take as an argument, and what was the old way?
cards.answer:
The argument doesn't have to be a Set — anything set-like works: an object with size, has(), and keys(). A Map qualifies (its keys act as the elements); an array does not — no size, and its keys() yields indexes.

let ids = new Set([1, 2, 3]);
let roles = new Map([[2, "admin"], [4, "guest"]]);

ids.intersection(roles); // Set {2} — Map keys as elements
ids.union([4, 5]);       // TypeError — array isn't set-like; wrap it: new Set([4, 5])

Before these methods, every operation was a hand-rolled spread + filter + has:

// the old way — intersection
let common = new Set([...a].filter(x => b.has(x)));
// union
let all = new Set([...a, ...b]);

Shipped across all modern engines in 2024–2025; for older targets, keep the filter idiom.

cards.question: What problem does WeakMap solve?
cards.answer:
A regular Map holds its keys alive: as long as an object sits in the map, the garbage collector can't reclaim it. A WeakMap doesn't prevent collection — when a key object loses all other references, the entry vanishes with it.

let john = {name: "John"};

let visits = new Map();
let weakVisits = new WeakMap();
visits.set(john, 123);
weakVisits.set(john, 123);

john = null;
// Map: {name:"John"} is STILL alive — visits holds it
// WeakMap: the object and its entry are both collected

So a WeakMap entry lives exactly as long as its key is reachable from somewhere else. That makes it the tool for attaching data to objects whose lifetime someone else controls — no manual cleanup, no leak.

One constraint follows immediately: keys must be objects. A primitive like "john" isn't garbage-collected, so a weak reference to it makes no sense.

cards.question: Why does WeakMap have no iteration and no size?
cards.answer:
Because the engine decides when garbage collection runs. An entry whose key became unreachable may be gone already or still sitting there — so the current contents are simply unknowable.

Rather than expose an answer that could change between two lines of code, the API just doesn't ask the question: no keys(), no values(), no entries(), no size, no for..of.

let weakMap = new WeakMap();
// only these four exist:
weakMap.set(obj, value);
weakMap.get(obj);
weakMap.has(obj);
weakMap.delete(obj);

Every operation needs a key you already hold — by design, not an oversight. If you need to enumerate the contents, you wanted a Map.

cards.question: What are the use cases for WeakMap?
cards.answer:
Two patterns: extra data — attaching information to objects you don't own — and caches whose entries should die with the object they describe.

Extra data: third-party code owns the user objects; you keep a visit counter on the side. When a user object goes away, its counter goes with it:

let visitCounts = new WeakMap();
function countUser(user) {
  visitCounts.set(user, (visitCounts.get(user) ?? 0) + 1);
}

Caching: memoize a heavy computation per object, with no invalidation code at all — the entry is collected when the object is:

let cache = new WeakMap();
function process(obj) {
  if (!cache.has(obj)) cache.set(obj, heavyCompute(obj));
  return cache.get(obj);
}

With a regular Map, both patterns leak: forgotten entries keep dead objects in memory forever.

cards.question: What is a WeakSet for?
cards.answer:
The same weak idea, membership only: a WeakSet stores objects (no primitives), and an object stays in the set only while it's reachable from somewhere else.

The API is just add, has, delete — like WeakMap, no iteration and no size, for the same reason.

It stores yes/no facts about objects — flags that should never outlive their object:

let visited = new WeakSet();

visited.add(john);
visited.has(john); // true — has he visited?

john = null; // flag cleaned up automatically

"Has this message been read?", "has this object been processed?" — when the answer matters only while the object exists, WeakSet keeps it without leaking.

cards.question: What is an ArrayBuffer?
cards.answer:
An ArrayBuffer is a reference to a fixed-length contiguous memory area — raw bytes. Despite the name it is not an array: no indexes to read, no methods to call, and the size is set once at creation.

let buffer = new ArrayBuffer(16); // 16 bytes, all zeroes
buffer.byteLength; // 16
buffer[0];         // undefined — no direct access

It just holds bytes. What they mean — numbers, text, pixels — is not the buffer's business.

Everything binary in the platform bottoms out here: file contents, network responses, image data are all an ArrayBuffer underneath.

cards.question: How do you read or write the bytes — what is a view?
cards.answer:
You can't touch the bytes directly — you put a view on top: a typed array or DataView that interprets the memory. The buffer stores the bytes, the view decides what they mean.

let buffer = new ArrayBuffer(16);

let view16 = new Uint16Array(buffer); // 8 numbers of 2 bytes
let view8  = new Uint8Array(buffer);  // 16 numbers of 1 byte

view16[0] = 65535;
view8[0]; // 255 — same memory, seen through different glasses
view8[1]; // 255

A view is like eyeglasses: nothing is copied, several views can sit on one buffer at once, and a write through one is instantly visible through the others.

That's the whole model: ArrayBuffer = the memory, views = typed windows into it.

cards.question: What kinds of typed arrays exist?
cards.answer:
A typed array is an array-like view over an ArrayBuffer where every element has the same fixed binary type. The constructor name says exactly what one element is.

ConstructorElement
Uint8Array, Uint16Array, Uint32Arrayunsigned integer, 1 / 2 / 4 bytes
Int8Array, Int16Array, Int32Arraysigned integer, 1 / 2 / 4 bytes
Float32Array, Float64Arrayfloating point, 4 / 8 bytes
BigInt64Array, BigUint64Array8-byte integers, elements are bigints
Uint8ClampedArray1 byte, clamps instead of wrapping — for pixel data

There is no constructor named TypedArray — it's just the common term for the family.

cards.question: How do you construct a typed array, and what are length, byteLength, byteOffset?
cards.answer:
Five forms — and only the first shares existing memory; the rest allocate or copy.

new Uint8Array(buffer, byteOffset, length); // view over existing memory
new Uint8Array([0, 1, 2, 3]);  // from an array — copies the values
new Uint8Array(otherTyped);    // from another typed array — converts values
new Uint8Array(16);            // allocates its own 16-element buffer
new Uint8Array();              // zero length

Every typed array sits on a buffer, reachable as arr.buffer. Three size properties describe the window:

let arr = new Uint16Array(buffer, 4, 2); // 2 elements starting at byte 4
arr.length;     // 2 — elements
arr.byteLength; // 4 — bytes
arr.byteOffset; // 4 — where the view starts in the buffer

Note the difference: new Uint8Array(uint16arr) converts the values (one number → one element), while new Uint8Array(uint16arr.buffer) reinterprets the bytes.

cards.question: What happens when a value doesn't fit the element type?
cards.answer:
Extra bits are silently cut off — only the lowest bits are stored, i.e. the remainder of division by 256 for Uint8Array. No error, no warning.

let arr = new Uint8Array(1);
arr[0] = 256; // binary 100000000 — the ninth bit falls off
arr[0];       // 0
arr[0] = 257; // 100000001
arr[0];       // 1

Uint8ClampedArray is the one exception: it clamps instead of wrapping — anything over 255 becomes 255, negatives become 0. That's the right behavior for pixels, which is why canvas image data uses it.

Floats never throw either — Float32Array just rounds to the nearest value it can represent.

cards.question: Which array methods are missing on typed arrays, and what replaces them?
cards.answer:
Everything that would change the length: no splice, no concat, no push/pop. The underlying buffer is fixed memory — you can't "delete" a byte.

map, find, reduce, fill, sort and friends all work. Two extra methods cover the rest:

arr.set(otherArr, offset);  // bulk-copy values into arr starting at offset
arr.subarray(begin, end);   // a new view on the SAME memory — no copying

The trap is subarray vs slice: they look identical, but slice copies the data into a new buffer, while subarray shares it — writes through the subarray change the original.

let arr = new Uint8Array([1, 2, 3, 4]);

let sub = arr.subarray(0, 2);
sub[0] = 100;
arr[0]; // 100 — same memory

let copy = arr.slice(0, 2);
copy[0] = 7;
arr[0]; // still 100 — the copy is independent

cards.question: How do you convert between binary data and strings — TextDecoder and TextEncoder?
cards.answer:
TextDecoder turns binary data into a string, TextEncoder turns a string into a Uint8Array of its UTF-8 bytes.

let bytes = new Uint8Array([72, 101, 108, 108, 111]);
new TextDecoder().decode(bytes); // "Hello"

new TextEncoder().encode("Hello"); // Uint8Array [72, 101, 108, 108, 111]

The decoder takes the encoding as its first argument — "utf-8" by default, "windows-1251" and others supported — and accepts a buffer or any view, so decoding just a part is a matter of passing a subarray.

The encoder only produces UTF-8. Besides encode it has one more method: encodeInto(str, destination) writes the bytes into an existing Uint8Array instead of allocating a new one.

cards.question: What is a DataView and when do you need it instead of a typed array?
cards.answer:
A DataView is an untyped view over a buffer: instead of fixing one element type at construction, you choose the format per call — getUint8(offset), getUint16(offset), setFloat64(offset, value)…

let view = new DataView(buffer);

view.getUint8(0);        // 1 byte at offset 0
view.getUint16(0);       // the same bytes, read as one 16-bit number
view.setUint32(4, 12345);

That's exactly what mixed-format data needs. A file header like "2 bytes version, 4 bytes length, then pixels" can't be one typed array — but it reads naturally, offset by offset, with a DataView.

Rule of thumb: uniform data (audio samples, pixel arrays) → typed array; structured data (file formats, protocol frames) → DataView.

cards.question: What is endianness, and how do typed arrays and DataView handle it?
cards.answer:
Endianness is the byte order of multi-byte numbers: little-endian stores the lowest byte first, big-endian the highest. The 16-bit number 1 is bytes 01 00 on one and 00 01 on the other.

Typed arrays always use the platform's order (little-endian on practically all current hardware) — you get no say. DataView makes it explicit: every multi-byte method takes a littleEndian flag, and the default is big-endian.

let view = new DataView(new Uint8Array([0, 1]).buffer);

view.getUint16(0);       // 1   — big-endian (the default)
view.getUint16(0, true); // 256 — little-endian

So for foreign binary data — network protocols and many file formats are big-endian — use DataView and state the order. Reading the same bytes through a typed array gives whatever the platform's order says, with no error to warn you.

cards.question: What is a Blob?
cards.answer:
A Blob is binary data plus a MIME type — the unit the browser treats as "a file's worth of content". You build it from parts: strings, buffers, typed arrays, other blobs.

let blob = new Blob(["<html>…</html>"], {type: "text/html"});
blob.size; // bytes
blob.type; // "text/html"

cards.question: Are Blobs mutable? How do you take a piece of one?
cards.answer:
Blobs are immutable — you can't change bytes in place, but slice cuts out a piece as a new blob.

blob.slice(0, 100, "text/plain"); // new Blob with the first 100 bytes

cards.question: Why use a Blob instead of an ArrayBuffer for large files?
cards.answer:
Unlike an ArrayBuffer, a Blob doesn't have to sit in memory all at once — the browser may keep it on disk, which is why blobs handle gigabyte files.

It's also the type web APIs exchange: fetch bodies, file inputs, and canvas exports all speak Blob.

cards.question: How do you turn a canvas drawing into a Blob?
cards.answer:
canvas.toBlob(cb, "image/png") turns the drawn image into a Blob ready to download or upload.

canvas.toBlob(blob => { … }, "image/png");

Because it can draw a placed <img>, this is how you "screenshot" one. It's callback-based because encoding takes time.

cards.question: What does URL.createObjectURL(blob) return?
cards.answer: It registers the blob in a browser-internal map and returns a short blob:<origin>/<uuid> URL, usable anywhere a URL goes — <img src>, <a download>.

cards.question: How do you trigger a Blob download with an object URL?
cards.answer:
let link = document.createElement("a");
link.download = "hello.txt";
link.href = URL.createObjectURL(blob);
link.click();
URL.revokeObjectURL(link.href);

cards.question: Why must you call URL.revokeObjectURL?
cards.answer: Each object URL pins its blob in memory until the document unloads. Call URL.revokeObjectURL(url) as soon as the URL isn't needed — afterwards the URL stops working, but the blob can be freed.

cards.question: Object URL vs base64 data URL — which to use?
cards.answer:
Default to object URLs; reach for a data URL (via FileReader.readAsDataURL) only when you need a self-contained string that carries the data itself.

Object URLData URL
Costinstant, no encodingbase64-encodes everything, ~33% bigger
CleanuprevokeObjectURL needednone

cards.question: What is a File and where do File objects come from?
cards.answer:
A File is a Blob with two extra properties: name and lastModified. Everything a Blob can do, a File can do.

You rarely construct one — the browser hands them to you from <input type="file"> or drag-and-drop:

input.onchange = () => {
  let file = input.files[0]; // files is array-like — could be several
  file.name; // "photo.png"
  file.size; // bytes
  file.type; // "image/png"
};

In drag-and-drop it's event.dataTransfer.files; the constructor new File(parts, name, {lastModified}) exists but is rare in practice.

Since a File is a Blob, the whole Blob toolbox applies as-is: slice it, make an object URL of it, send it in a fetch body.

cards.question: How do you read a File's contents — FileReader and the modern alternatives?
cards.answer:
The classic way is FileReader — an event-based object that reads a Blob or File into one of three shapes.

let reader = new FileReader();
reader.readAsText(file);       // string (UTF-8 by default)
// readAsArrayBuffer(file)     // binary, for processing
// readAsDataURL(file)         // base64 data: URL, for src attributes

reader.onload  = () => console.log(reader.result);
reader.onerror = () => console.log(reader.error);

Modern code mostly skips it — blobs have promise-based methods:

let text   = await file.text();
let buffer = await file.arrayBuffer();
let stream = file.stream(); // piece-by-piece, for huge files

And to display a picked image, URL.createObjectURL(file) beats reading it into a data URL. FileReader survives where you need progress events (onprogress) or broad legacy support.

cards.question: What is a SharedArrayBuffer, and how is it different from sending an ArrayBuffer to a worker?
cards.answer:
A SharedArrayBuffer is an ArrayBuffer whose memory is shared between threads: the page and a Web Worker (or Node worker threads) see the same bytes at the same time.

A regular ArrayBuffer in postMessage is either copied, or transferred — moved to the worker and detached on the sender's side. A SharedArrayBuffer is neither: both sides keep a live view.

let sab = new SharedArrayBuffer(1024);
worker.postMessage(sab);          // not copied, not detached — shared
let shared = new Int32Array(sab); // an ordinary typed-array view over it

You work with it through the same views as any buffer. What changes is that another thread may be reading and writing the same memory at the same moment — which is why Atomics exists.

cards.question: What problem does Atomics solve, and what are its core operations?
cards.answer:
With shared memory, two threads can hit the same element at once. shared[0]++ is three steps — read, add, write — so concurrent runs interleave and lose updates. Atomics makes such operations indivisible.

Atomics.add(shared, 0, 1);    // safe concurrent increment
Atomics.load(shared, 0);      // safe read
Atomics.store(shared, 0, 42); // safe write

It also replaces busy-polling with real sleeping. Atomics.wait and Atomics.notify work only on an Int32Array over a SharedArrayBuffer:

let shared = new Int32Array(new SharedArrayBuffer(4));

// worker: sleep while shared[0] equals 0
Atomics.wait(shared, 0, 0);

// main thread: change the value, wake one waiter
Atomics.store(shared, 0, 1);
Atomics.notify(shared, 0, 1);

Atomics.wait blocks the thread, so it's allowed in workers but forbidden on the main thread — the page must never freeze.

cards.question: Why is SharedArrayBuffer missing unless the page is cross-origin isolated?
cards.answer:
After the Spectre attacks, browsers only expose SharedArrayBuffer to cross-origin-isolated pages — shared memory enables high-precision timers, which made reading foreign memory possible.

The server must opt in with two headers:

Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin
Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp

They cut the page off from non-cooperating cross-origin content. Check at runtime with the global crossOriginIsolated — when it's false, SharedArrayBuffer is simply absent.

Niche in app code, but it's what powers multithreaded WebAssembly — ffmpeg-in-the-browser and the like.

cards.question: What do Object.keys, Object.values, and Object.entries give you?
cards.answer:
The three ways to pull a plain object apart: Object.keys(obj) — array of property names, Object.values(obj) — array of values, Object.entries(obj) — array of [key, value] pairs.

let user = {name: "John", age: 30};

Object.keys(user);    // ["name", "age"]
Object.values(user);  // ["John", 30]
Object.entries(user); // [["name", "John"], ["age", 30]]

for (let [key, value] of Object.entries(user)) { ... }

Note the shape: they're called Object.keys(obj), not obj.keys() — static methods, so they work even when the object defines its own keys method. And they return real arrays, not iterators like map.keys() — array methods apply directly.

Like for..in, all three ignore symbol keys; when those matter, there's Object.getOwnPropertySymbols or Reflect.ownKeys.

cards.question: How do you map or filter an object?
cards.answer:
Objects have no map or filter — so you take a round trip through entries: Object.entries → array methods → Object.fromEntries.

let prices = {banana: 1, orange: 2, meat: 4};

let doubled = Object.fromEntries(
  Object.entries(prices).map(([key, value]) => [key, value * 2])
);
// {banana: 2, orange: 4, meat: 8}

The same pattern filters, renames keys, or inverts the object — any array transform works in the middle:

let cheap = Object.fromEntries(
  Object.entries(prices).filter(([, value]) => value < 3)
);
// {banana: 1, orange: 2}

Object.fromEntries accepts any iterable of pairs, so the same line converts a Map to a plain object — the entries round trip is the universal adapter between keyed shapes.

cards.question: What is array destructuring?
cards.answer:
It "unpacks" an array (or any iterable) into variables, positionally. The array itself is unchanged — it's just a shorter way to read items out.

let [firstName, surname] = "John Smith".split(" ");

cards.question: How do you skip elements or gather the tail in array destructuring?
cards.answer:
Empty commas skip positions; ...rest gathers everything remaining into an array.

let [, , third] = ["a", "b", "c"];   // commas skip elements
let [a, b, ...rest] = [1, 2, 3, 4];  // rest gathers the tail: [3, 4]

cards.question: How do defaults work in array destructuring, and when do they run?
cards.answer:
A default kicks in only when the value is missing (undefined). It can be any expression, even a function call — which runs lazily, only if needed.

let [name = "Guest"] = [];                // default used
let [name2 = prompt("name?")] = ["John"]; // prompt never runs

cards.question: What can appear on the left side of array destructuring?
cards.answer:
Anything assignable — not just fresh variables. It works on any iterable (strings, Sets, Maps).

let user = {};
[user.name, user.age] = ["John", 30];   // assign into properties

for (let [key, value] of map) { ... }   // entries destructured in the loop head

cards.question: How do you swap two variables with destructuring?
cards.answer:
Assign an array of both, reversed — no temporary variable needed.

[guest, admin] = [admin, guest];

cards.question: What is object destructuring?
cards.answer:
It pulls properties out by name — order doesn't matter, unlike arrays.

let options = {title: "Menu", width: 100, height: 200};
let {title, width, height} = options;   // by name

cards.question: How do you rename, default, and collect rest in object destructuring?
cards.answer:
Colon renames property → variable, = sets a default, ...rest collects what's left.

let {width: w, height: h} = options;              // rename
let {title: t = "Untitled", size = 10} = options; // defaults
let {title: only, ...rest} = options;             // rest: {width: 100, height: 200}

Defaults are lazy — the expression on the right of = runs only if the property is missing.

cards.question: How do you destructure nested objects and arrays?
cards.answer:
Mirror the structure on the left to reach inside — patterns nest.

let {size: {width: innerW}, items: [item1]} = {
  size: {width: 100, height: 200},
  items: ["Cake", "Donut"],
};

cards.question: Why do you need parentheses when destructuring into existing variables?
cards.answer:
A statement starting with { reads as a code block, so assigning (without let) needs wrapping parentheses.

let title, width;
{title, width} = options;   // SyntaxError
({title, width} = options); // works

cards.question: What are smart function parameters?
cards.answer:
Destructure an options object right in the parameter list — callers pass one object with only the fields they care about, and defaults fill the rest. This is the standard cure for functions with many optional arguments.

The problem shape — positional parameters force callers to pass placeholders:

function showMenu(title = "Untitled", width = 200, height = 100, items = []) {}
showMenu("My Menu", undefined, undefined, ["Item1"]); // ugly and fragile

The fix — take one object, destructure it with defaults and renames:

function showMenu({title = "Untitled", width: w = 100, items = []} = {}) {
  // title, w, items ready to use
}

showMenu({title: "My Menu", items: ["Item1"]}); // named, order-free, skippable

Note the trailing = {} — the default for the whole parameter. Without it, calling showMenu() with no arguments tries to destructure undefined and throws.

cards.question: What's the difference between rest and spread?
cards.answer:
Same three dots, opposite directions. Rest sits in a parameter list and gathers arguments into an array; spread sits in calls and literals and expands an iterable into individual items.

function sumAll(...args) {        // rest: gathers
  return args.reduce((s, n) => s + n, 0);
}
sumAll(1, 2, 3); // 6

Math.max(...[3, 5, 1]);  // spread: expands → Math.max(3, 5, 1)

cards.question: What can spread expand, beyond arrays?
cards.answer:
Any iterable, and it can be combined with literal items inline.

let arr = [3, 5, 1];
let merged = [0, ...arr, 6]; // [0, 3, 5, 1, 6]
let chars = [..."Hello"];    // any iterable spreads

cards.question: Where must a rest parameter appear in the parameter list?
cards.answer:
Last — it takes everything remaining, so nothing can follow it. Regular parameters before it are fine.

function showName(first, last, ...titles) {} // ok
function broken(...args, last) {}            // SyntaxError

cards.question: Why did rest parameters replace the old arguments object?
cards.answer: arguments is array-like but has no array methods and can't skip leading parameters. Arrow functions don't even have their own arguments — they see the enclosing function's.

cards.question: How do you copy an array or object with spread?
cards.answer:
[...arr] and {...obj} are the idiomatic copies — and you can override keys in the same object literal.

let copy = [...arr];            // array copy
copy.push(4);                  // arr untouched

let clone = {...user, age: 30}; // copy + override in one literal

cards.question: How do you merge objects with spread?
cards.answer:
Spread several sources into one literal — later spreads win on key conflicts.

let merged = {...defaults, ...userOptions}; // userOptions overrides defaults

cards.question: How does Object.assign differ from object spread?
cards.answer: Object.assign(dest, ...sources) does the same merge but copies into its first argument and returns it; spread builds a new object — usually what you want.

cards.question: Why are spread and Object.assign called shallow copies?
cards.answer:
They copy only one level. Nested objects are copied by reference, so copy and original share them.

let a = {sizes: {w: 100}};
let b = {...a};
b.sizes.w = 9;
a.sizes.w; // 9 — same inner object

cards.question: What's the asymmetry between array spread and object spread inputs?
cards.answer: Array spread iterates, so it needs an iterable; object spread copies own properties, so it works on anything.

cards.question: How do you deep clone an object?
cards.answer:
structuredClone(obj) clones the whole tree — every nested object in the copy is independent of the original.

let user = {name: "John", sizes: {height: 182}};
let deep = structuredClone(user);
deep.sizes === user.sizes; // false — fully independent

cards.question: When do you need a deep clone instead of a shallow copy?
cards.answer: When the copy must own its nested data. Spread and Object.assign are shallow — nested objects stay shared between copy and original.

cards.question: What can structuredClone handle, including tricky cases?
cards.answer:
Most data — objects, arrays, primitives, Dates, Maps, Sets, typed arrays — and even circular references.

let obj = {};
obj.me = obj;
structuredClone(obj).me; // points at the clone, not the original

cards.question: What can't structuredClone clone?
cards.answer: Functions — a property holding one makes it throw. Class instances and callback-bearing configs need a hand-written clone or lodash's _.cloneDeep.

cards.question: Why is JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj)) a bad deep-clone idiom?
cards.answer: It's strictly worse than structuredClone: it silently drops undefined and functions, mangles Dates into strings, and dies on circular references.

cards.question: What does Object.freeze do?
cards.answer:
Object.freeze(obj) makes an object read-only: no adding, removing, or changing properties.

Writes fail silently — or throw a TypeError in strict mode:

let config = Object.freeze({api: "/v1", retries: 3});
config.retries = 5;      // ignored (TypeError in strict mode)
Object.isFrozen(config); // true

It's shallow — nested objects stay mutable:

let frozen = Object.freeze({limits: {max: 10}});
frozen.limits.max = 99; // works — only the top level is frozen

The softer sibling Object.seal blocks adding and removing properties but still allows changing existing values.

cards.question: What does Object.hasOwn do?
cards.answer:
Object.hasOwn(obj, key) answers "does the object itself have this key" — own properties only, the prototype chain is ignored.

It replaces obj.hasOwnProperty(key), which breaks on objects created with Object.create(null) — they have no prototype, so the method simply isn't there:

let dict = Object.create(null);
dict.hasOwnProperty;        // undefined — no prototype, no method
Object.hasOwn(dict, "key"); // works on anything

Being a static method, it also can't be shadowed by the object's own hasOwnProperty property — safe by construction.

cards.question: What does Object.groupBy do?
cards.answer:
Object.groupBy(items, fn) turns a list into a keyed object of groups: fn returns a group name for each item, and items with the same name land in the same array.

let people = [{name: "Ann", age: 17}, {name: "Bob", age: 30}];

Object.groupBy(people, p => p.age >= 18 ? "adult" : "minor");
// {minor: [{Ann}], adult: [{Bob}]}

The result is a null-prototype object — no inherited methods like hasOwnProperty, so check keys with Object.hasOwn or just read them directly.

cards.question: What does for..of actually do — what is the iterable protocol?
cards.answer:
for..of knows nothing about arrays — it works by a contract. It calls the object's Symbol.iterator method, gets back an iterator (an object with next()), and calls next() for each step until it returns {done: true}.

Each next() call returns {done: Boolean, value: any}: value is the next item, done: true means the iteration is finished.

for (let item of obj) { ... }

// what the loop really does:
let iterator = obj[Symbol.iterator]();
while (true) {
  let result = iterator.next(); // {done, value}
  if (result.done) break;
  let item = result.value;
  // ...
}

That contract is the whole story: any object that honors it works in for..of. Such objects are called iterables.

cards.question: Which built-ins are iterable?
cards.answer:
Arrays, strings, Map, Set, and most DOM collections are iterable out of the box.

And for..of is just one consumer. The same protocol powers spread, destructuring, Array.from, new Map(pairs) / new Set(items), Promise.all — make something iterable once and all of these accept it.

let set = new Set(["a", "b"]);

[...set];         // ["a", "b"] — spread
let [x, y] = set; // destructuring, same protocol

Plain objects are not iterable: for (let x of {}) throws TypeError. For object keys use Object.keys / Object.entries — for..of and for..in are different things.

cards.question: How does a string iterate?
cards.answer:
Character by character — and "character" means a real code point, so emojis and other surrogate pairs come out whole.

Indexing and length work on 16-bit units instead, so for such characters they give wrong results:

let str = "😂!";

str.length; // 3 — counts 16-bit units, not characters
str[0];     // broken half of the emoji

for (let ch of str) alert(ch); // 😂, then ! — correct
[...str];                      // ["😂", "!"] — correct

So when characters matter, iterate — for..of, spread, or Array.from(str) — instead of indexing.

cards.question: What's the difference between an iterable and an array-like?
cards.answer:
An iterable has Symbol.iterator; an array-like has indexes and length. Two separate contracts — an object can be either, both, or neither.

let arrayLike = {0: "Hello", 1: "World", length: 2};

for (let x of arrayLike) {} // TypeError — no Symbol.iterator
arrayLike[0];               // "Hello" — indexing is fine

Strings are both. A Set is iterable but not array-like. arguments and old DOM collections are array-like only (modern DOM collections are also iterable).

And neither contract gives you array methods — arrayLike.push(...) fails either way. For that you need a real array.

cards.question: What does Array.from do?
cards.answer:
Array.from(obj) takes an iterable or an array-like and makes a real array from it — the universal converter, and the point where array methods become available.

let arrayLike = {0: "Hello", 1: "World", length: 2};

Array.from(arrayLike);            // ["Hello", "World"] — works where [...arrayLike] throws
Array.from([1, 2, 3], n => n * n); // [1, 4, 9] — mapping fn

The optional second argument maps each value before adding it — Array.from(x, fn) is convert + map in one pass.

On strings it iterates by code points, which gives surrogate-safe operations:

Array.from("😂!").length;  // 2 — real character count
// vs "😂!".length === 3

cards.question: How do you make your own object iterable — the range example?
cards.answer:
Add a Symbol.iterator method that returns an object with next(). next() returns {done: false, value} while there are values, then {done: true}.

let range = {
  from: 1,
  to: 5,

  [Symbol.iterator]() {
    return {
      current: this.from,
      last: this.to,
      next() {
        return this.current <= this.last
          ? {done: false, value: this.current++}
          : {done: true};
      }
    };
  }
};

for (let num of range) alert(num); // 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
[...range];                        // spread works too — same protocol

Symbol.iterator is called once, when the loop starts; the object it returns carries all the iteration state (current, last).

In practice a generator usually replaces the hand-written iterator: *[Symbol.iterator]() { for (let i = this.from; i <= this.to; i++) yield i; } — same result, three lines (see Generators).

cards.question: Does the iterator have to be a separate object?
cards.answer:
No — an object can be its own iterator: put next() on it and return this from Symbol.iterator. The price: the iteration state lives on the object itself, so only one loop can run over it at a time.

let range = {
  from: 1,
  to: 5,

  [Symbol.iterator]() {
    this.current = this.from;
    return this; // I am my own iterator
  },

  next() {
    return this.current <= this.to
      ? {done: false, value: this.current++}
      : {done: true};
  }
};

With a separate iterator, every for..of asks Symbol.iterator for a fresh one, so two loops over the same range — even nested — don't interfere. That independence is why the separate-iterator form is the default; self-iteration is a simplification you pay for with shared state.

cards.question: Can you call an iterator explicitly, without for..of?
cards.answer:
Yes — get it with obj[Symbol.iterator]() and call next() yourself. That gives more control than for..of: take values at your own pace, pause, resume later, or split one iteration between different parts of the code.

let iterator = "Hi"[Symbol.iterator]();

iterator.next(); // {value: "H", done: false}
// ... do other work, come back later ...
iterator.next(); // {value: "i", done: false}
iterator.next(); // {value: undefined, done: true}

An iterator is single-pass: once done, it stays done. To iterate again, ask the iterable for a new one.

cards.question: What are iterator helpers?
cards.answer:
The familiar array methods, available directly on iterators: .map, .filter, .take, .drop, .flatMap, .reduce, .some, .every, .find, .forEach, .toArray.

They work on anything that produces an iterator: generator results, map.values(), set.keys(), str.matchAll(...) — no converting to an array first.

let firstBig = map.values().find(v => v.size > 100);
// before: [...map.values()].find(...) — full copy just to search

Shipped in all modern engines (2024–2025).

cards.question: Why are iterator helpers lazy, and what does that buy?
cards.answer:
Each helper returns a new iterator without computing anything — work happens only when a value is pulled, and each value travels through the whole chain one at a time. No intermediate arrays, ever.

function* numbers() { let n = 0; while (true) yield n++; } // infinite!

numbers()
  .map(n => n * n)
  .filter(n => n % 2 === 0)
  .take(3)
  .toArray(); // [0, 4, 16] — computed only what take(3) asked for

The same chain on an array would build a full new array per step — and an infinite source would be impossible: map over it would never finish. Laziness makes huge and endless sequences as cheap as small ones.

cards.question: What is Iterator.from?
cards.answer:
Iterator.from(obj) wraps any iterable or iterator into a helper-capable one — the bridge for plain iterables like a custom range, which has Symbol.iterator but no helper methods of its own.

Iterator.from(range)
  .map(n => n * n)
  .take(2)
  .toArray(); // [1, 4]

Rule of thumb: data already sitting in an array → array methods. Data arriving from an iterator, or a huge/infinite sequence → helpers, straight on the source, no copy.

cards.question: What are the four ways to create a Date?
cards.answer:
Four ways: new Date() — the current date and time; new Date(ms) — from a timestamp, milliseconds since Jan 1 1970 UTC; new Date(string) — parsed from a string; new Date(year, month, date, hours, minutes, seconds, ms) — from components.

new Date();                 // now
new Date(0);                // 01.01.1970 UTC
new Date(24 * 3600 * 1000); // 02.01.1970 — one day later
new Date("2026-06-12");     // parsed from a string
new Date(2026, 5, 12);      // 12 June 2026

A timestamp can be negative — dates before 1970 are just negative milliseconds. In the component form only year and month are required; the rest default to date = 1 and zeros for the time.

The trap: the month counts from 0. 0 is January, 11 is December — so new Date(2026, 0, 1) is the 1st of January.

cards.question: How do you read a Date's components — and what's the difference between local and UTC getters?
cards.answer:
Component getters: getFullYear(), getMonth() (0–11), getDate() (day of month, 1–31), getHours() … getMilliseconds(), and getDay() — the weekday, 0 = Sunday through 6 = Saturday.

All of them return values in the local time zone. Each has a UTC twin — getUTCFullYear(), getUTCHours(), getUTCDay()… — for the same moment in UTC:

let date = new Date();
date.getHours();    // the hour in your time zone
date.getUTCHours(); // the hour in UTC

Two methods have no UTC variant because they don't depend on the zone: getTime() — the timestamp — and getTimezoneOffset() — the difference between UTC and local time, in minutes (e.g. -120 for UTC+2).

Never use getYear() — broken legacy that can return a two-digit year. Always getFullYear().

cards.question: How do you change a Date, and what is autocorrection?
cards.answer:
Every getter has a setter — setFullYear, setMonth, setDate, setHours… plus setTime(ms) — and autocorrection fixes out-of-range values: components roll over automatically.

let date = new Date(2026, 0, 32); // there's no Jan 32
// → 1 February 2026

That makes "add N days/hours" trivial — set the component out of range and let the Date sort it out, month lengths and leap years included:

let date = new Date(2026, 0, 28);
date.setDate(date.getDate() + 5);        // 2 February 2026
date.setSeconds(date.getSeconds() + 70); // +70 seconds, just works

Zero and negatives work too: date.setDate(0) gives the last day of the previous month. Note that setters mutate the Date in place — a shared date object changing under you is a classic bug, and one of the reasons Temporal exists.

cards.question: How do you subtract dates and measure how long code takes?
cards.answer:
Converting a Date to a number gives its timestamp — the same as getTime(). So subtracting two dates yields the difference in milliseconds.

let start = new Date();
// ... do the job ...
let end = new Date();
alert(`The job took ${end - start} ms`);

If you only need the current timestamp, Date.now() returns it without creating an object — faster, and no garbage for the collector, which matters when measuring in a loop:

let start = Date.now();
for (let i = 0; i < 100000; i++) doSomething();
let elapsed = Date.now() - start; // ms

The benchmarking caveat: modern engines optimize hot code on the fly, so the first runs of a function are slower than later ones, and other activity on the machine skews results. To compare two implementations honestly, run each benchmark many times in alternation, with a heat-up run before measuring — a single-run microbenchmark is noise.

cards.question: What string format does Date.parse read, and what does it return?
cards.answer:
Date.parse(str) reads the ISO format YYYY-MM-DDTHH:mm:ss.sssZ and returns a timestamp (ms) — or NaN if the string is unreadable.

Date.parse("2012-01-26T13:51:50.417-07:00"); // 1327611110417

T is the delimiter; Z means UTC+0, or use a zone offset as +hh:mm.

cards.question: What shorter ISO variants does Date.parse accept?
cards.answer: The date part can be truncated: YYYY-MM-DD, YYYY-MM, even just YYYY.

cards.question: Does the Date constructor parse strings the same way as Date.parse?
cards.answer:
Yes — new Date(str) uses the same algorithm as Date.parse.

new Date(Date.parse("2026-06-12"));
new Date("2026-06-12"); // same result

cards.question: What time zone is a date-only string like "2026-06-12" parsed in?
cards.answer:
A date-only string is treated as UTC midnight, not local. West of UTC, local getters then show the previous day.

let d = new Date("2026-06-12"); // 2026-06-12T00:00:00Z
// in New York (UTC-4):
d.getDate(); // 11 — the day before!

cards.question: What time zone is a date+time string with no zone like "2026-06-12T00:00" parsed in?
cards.answer: It's parsed as local time — unlike a date-only string, which is UTC.

cards.question: What happens if you parse a non-ISO date string like "06/12/2026"?
cards.answer: Any non-ISO format is engine-dependent — "06/12/2026" may parse differently across browsers, or not at all. Stick to ISO strings.

cards.question: What does JSON.stringify do, and which values does it skip?
cards.answer:
JSON.stringify(value) converts a value into a JSON string. It handles objects, arrays, strings, numbers, booleans and null — and silently skips what JSON can't express: functions, symbolic keys, and properties with undefined.

JSON.stringify({
  sayHi() {},           // skipped
  [Symbol("id")]: 123,  // skipped
  something: undefined, // skipped
  name: "John"
});
// '{"name":"John"}' — no error, the rest is just gone

Nested objects are converted automatically, any depth. The output is stricter than a JS literal: strings and property names get double quotes only, no comments, no trailing commas — that strictness is why JSON parses fast in every language.

JSON.stringify("test");          // '"test"' — quotes added
JSON.stringify({a: {b: [1, 2]}}); // whole tree serialized

cards.question: Why does JSON.stringify throw on circular references, and how does replacer solve it?
cards.answer:
A circular structure can't be written as a tree, so JSON.stringify throws TypeError: Converting circular structure to JSON. The fix is the second argument — replacer — which filters what gets serialized.

let room = {number: 23};
let meetup = {title: "Conference", place: room};
room.occupiedBy = meetup; // circular

JSON.stringify(meetup); // TypeError

replacer is either an array of keys to keep, or a (key, value) function that returns the value to use — return undefined to drop the property:

JSON.stringify(meetup, ["title", "place", "number"]);
// '{"title":"Conference","place":{"number":23}}'

JSON.stringify(meetup, (key, value) =>
  key == "occupiedBy" ? undefined : value
);

The function runs for every nested key/value pair too — the very first call gets the empty key "" with the whole object as the value.

cards.question: What do the space parameter and the toJSON method do?
cards.answer:
The third argument space indents the output for readability; a toJSON() method lets an object decide its own JSON form.

JSON.stringify(user, null, 2); // pretty-printed, 2-space indent
// for logs and debugging; network payloads go without it

If an object has toJSON(), JSON.stringify calls it and serializes the result instead of the object. Date has one built in — that's why dates become ISO strings:

JSON.stringify({date: new Date(Date.UTC(2026, 5, 12))});
// '{"date":"2026-06-12T00:00:00.000Z"}'

let room = {
  number: 23,
  toJSON() { return this.number; }
};
JSON.stringify(room); // '23' — used when nested in other objects too

cards.question: How does JSON.parse work, and what is the reviver for?
cards.answer:
JSON.parse(str) turns a JSON string back into a value. Its second argument, reviver, is a (key, value) function that transforms each value as it's read.

The classic use is dates: JSON has no Date type, so a serialized date arrives as a plain string — the reviver turns it back:

let str = '{"title":"Conference","date":"2026-06-12T10:00:00.000Z"}';

let meetup = JSON.parse(str, (key, value) =>
  key == "date" ? new Date(value) : value
);
meetup.date.getDate(); // works — a real Date again

It reaches nested objects too — every value passes through the reviver. And JSON.parse is strict: single quotes, unquoted keys, or new Date(...) inside the string all throw, because JSON is data only, no code.

cards.question: Why is Date considered broken — what does Temporal fix?
cards.answer:
Temporal is the modern replacement for Date, fixing its core flaws: values are immutable, months are 1-based, parsing is strictly defined, and time zones are first-class.

Date's problems run deep: setters mutate the object in place (a shared date can change under you), months count from 0, non-ISO parsing differs between engines, and one timestamp has to stand in for every concept — a birthday, a meeting time, an exact moment.

// Date: mutation surprise
function nextDay(date) {
  date.setDate(date.getDate() + 1); // caller's date changed too!
  return date;
}

// Temporal: arithmetic returns a new value
let date = Temporal.PlainDate.from("2026-01-31");
date.add({months: 1}); // 2026-02-28 — clamped, well-defined; date unchanged
date.month;            // 1 = January

cards.question: What are Temporal's main types?
cards.answer:
Temporal gives a separate type for each concept, instead of cramming everything into one timestamp.

TypeMeans
PlainDatea calendar date — no time, no zone (a birthday)
PlainTimea wall-clock time (13:30)
PlainDateTimedate + time, still no zone
ZonedDateTimean exact moment in a named time zone
Instanta precise moment — the timestamp's successor
Durationa length of time (2h 30m)
Temporal.PlainDate.from("2026-06-12");
Temporal.ZonedDateTime.from("2026-06-12T13:30[Europe/Berlin]");
Temporal.Duration.from({hours: 2, minutes: 30});

Choosing the type forces you to say what you mean — a date-only value can't pick up a time-zone bug, because it has no zone at all.

cards.question: How does Temporal handle time-zone arithmetic, and can you use it today?
cards.answer:
A ZonedDateTime carries its time zone, so arithmetic respects DST: adding a day means "same wall-clock time tomorrow", even if that day has 23 or 25 hours.

let meeting = Temporal.ZonedDateTime.from("2026-03-28T09:00[Europe/Berlin]");
meeting.add({days: 1}); // 09:00 next day — across the DST switch
// with Date you'd add 24h of milliseconds and land on 10:00

Differences come out as Durations — date.until(other), date.since(other) — no hand-rolled millisecond math.

Status: the proposal is finalized; Firefox ships it enabled, Chrome and Safari are implementing. For production today, use the @js-temporal/polyfill. Worth learning now — new code and interview answers increasingly assume it.

cards.question: Why does Intl exist, and what is a locale string?
cards.answer:
Intl is the built-in internationalization namespace: formatters that know each language's rules for numbers, dates, sorting and plurals, so you don't hand-build "$1,234.50" or ship a library for it.

Every locale does things differently — 1,234.5 in English is 1.234,5 in German; date parts flip order; accented letters sort differently. The browser ships all that data; you only name the locale: a language code, optionally with a region — "en", "en-US", "de-DE", "uz-UZ".

new Intl.NumberFormat("en-US").format(1234.5); // "1,234.5"
new Intl.NumberFormat("de-DE").format(1234.5); // "1.234,5"

new Intl.NumberFormat(undefined).format(1234.5); // the visitor's own locale
new Intl.NumberFormat(["uz", "ru"]);             // fallback list: first supported wins

In a UI you usually pass undefined (or nothing) — format for the user's locale, not one you hardcoded.

cards.question: What can Intl.NumberFormat format — the four styles?
cards.answer:
new Intl.NumberFormat(locale, options).format(n) returns the formatted string; style says what the number is: "decimal" (default), "currency", "percent" or "unit".

new Intl.NumberFormat("en-US", {style: "currency", currency: "EUR"}).format(1234.5);
// "€1,234.50" — symbol, position and digits per locale

new Intl.NumberFormat("en-US", {style: "percent"}).format(0.25); // "25%"

new Intl.NumberFormat("en-US", {style: "unit", unit: "megabyte"}).format(512);
// "512 MB"

currency style requires the currency code — omit it and the constructor throws. And percent expects a fraction: it multiplies by 100, so format(25) gives "2,500%".

cards.question: How do you control digits and compact notation — and what are the narrow-space and formatter-reuse gotchas?
cards.answer:
minimumFractionDigits/maximumFractionDigits pin how many decimals appear; notation: "compact" abbreviates big numbers to "1.2M".

new Intl.NumberFormat("en-US", {maximumFractionDigits: 1}).format(3.14159); // "3.1" — rounds
new Intl.NumberFormat("en-US", {minimumFractionDigits: 2}).format(5);       // "5.00"

new Intl.NumberFormat("en-US", {notation: "compact"}).format(1234567); // "1.2M"
new Intl.NumberFormat("de-DE", {notation: "compact"}).format(1234567); // "1,2 Mio."

Two gotchas. The output can contain special characters — French groups digits with a narrow no-break space, not a normal one — so never compare formatted strings to hand-typed ones in tests.

And constructing a formatter is expensive while format() is cheap — true for the whole Intl family. Create one formatter outside the loop and reuse it.

cards.question: How does Intl.DateTimeFormat format dates — presets and component options?
cards.answer:
Intl.DateTimeFormat renders a Date by the locale's conventions — part order, month names, 12- vs 24-hour clock. The quick setup is the dateStyle/timeStyle presets: "full", "long", "medium", "short".

let date = new Date(2026, 5, 12, 14, 30);

new Intl.DateTimeFormat("en-US").format(date); // "6/12/2026"
new Intl.DateTimeFormat("de-DE").format(date); // "12.6.2026"

new Intl.DateTimeFormat("en-GB", {dateStyle: "medium", timeStyle: "short"})
  .format(date); // "12 Jun 2026, 14:30"

For exact control, pick components instead — weekday, year, month, day, hour, minute — each with its verbosity: "numeric", "2-digit", "long", "short".

new Intl.DateTimeFormat("en-US", {
  weekday: "long", year: "numeric", month: "long", day: "numeric",
}).format(date); // "Friday, June 12, 2026"

Presets and component options don't mix — dateStyle together with month throws.

cards.question: How do you display a date in another time zone?
cards.answer:
Pass timeZone in the options — the only built-in way to show a moment in another zone. A Date stores a UTC timestamp and normally renders in the system zone; the formatter overrides that at output time.

let date = new Date("2026-06-12T10:00:00Z");

date.toLocaleString("en-GB", {
  timeZone: "Asia/Tashkent", dateStyle: "medium", timeStyle: "short",
}); // "12 Jun 2026, 15:00" — UTC+5, the Date itself unchanged

date.toLocaleString(locale, options) (and toLocaleDateString/toLocaleTimeString) is the one-off shorthand — the same engine without an explicit formatter object. For many dates, build one formatter and reuse it.

cards.question: What is formatToParts for?
cards.answer:
formatToParts(date) returns the same output as format, but as an array of {type, value} pieces — so you can style, rearrange or pick parts without parsing the string.

let f = new Intl.DateTimeFormat("en-US", {month: "long", day: "numeric"});

f.formatToParts(new Date(2026, 5, 12));
// [{type: "month", value: "June"}, {type: "literal", value: " "}, {type: "day", value: "12"}]

f.formatToParts(date).map(p =>
  p.type === "month" ? `<b>${p.value}</b>` : p.value
).join(""); // "<b>June</b> 12"

Intl.NumberFormat has the same method — handy for styling a currency symbol differently from the amount.

cards.question: How does Intl.RelativeTimeFormat handle "3 days ago"?
cards.answer:
Intl.RelativeTimeFormat is a sibling formatter to Intl.DateTimeFormat — its own constructor in the same family. It produces "3 days ago" / "in 2 hours" in any language: you pass a signed amount and a unit.

let rtf = new Intl.RelativeTimeFormat("en", {numeric: "auto"});

rtf.format(-3, "day"); // "3 days ago"
rtf.format(2, "hour"); // "in 2 hours"
rtf.format(-1, "day"); // "yesterday" — numeric: "auto" uses a word when one exists

It only formats — it doesn't compute. Working out that two timestamps are 3 days apart, and which unit reads best, is your code's job.

cards.question: Why does plain sort() get strings wrong, and how does Intl.Collator fix it?
cards.answer:
Default comparison (<, plain sort()) compares character codes, not letters — every uppercase letter is "smaller" than every lowercase one, and accented letters land after "z". Intl.Collator compares the way a dictionary in that language would.

let words = ["résumé", "zebra", "apple", "Über"];

words.sort();
// ["apple", "résumé", "zebra", "Über"] — code order, wrong: Ü (220) lands after z (122)

words.sort(new Intl.Collator("en").compare);
// ["apple", "résumé", "Über", "zebra"]

collator.compare returns negative/zero/positive — exactly what sort expects, so it plugs straight in. The shorthand str.localeCompare(other, locale, options) is a one-shot collator; for big arrays create one collator and reuse it — much faster.

cards.question: What do the sensitivity and numeric options do?
cards.answer:
sensitivity chooses which differences count as differences; numeric: true compares digit runs as numbers, giving "natural" sort.

sensitivitytreats as equal
"base"a = á = A — only base letters differ
"accent"a = A, but a ≠ á
"case"a = á, but a ≠ A
"variant"nothing — every difference counts (the sorting default)
new Intl.Collator("en", {sensitivity: "base"}).compare("a", "Á"); // 0

new Intl.Collator("en", {numeric: true}).compare("file2", "file10"); // < 0
// without numeric: "file10" sorts before "file2" — "1" < "2" as characters

compare(...) === 0 with sensitivity: "base" is the right tool for case- and accent-insensitive matching — toLowerCase() comparisons miss the accents. There's also caseFirst: "upper" | "lower" for which case wins ties.

cards.question: How do you correctly split a string into characters when it may contain emoji?
cards.answer:
Intl.Segmenter with granularity: "grapheme" splits a string into what a human sees as single characters — the correct way to count or split text with emoji.

JS strings are sequences of 16-bit units, and the usual tools count those, not characters: "😀".length is 2. Spreading fixes simple emoji, but composed ones are several emoji joined by invisible joiners — [..."👨‍👩‍👦"] tears the family apart.

let seg = new Intl.Segmenter("en", {granularity: "grapheme"});

[...seg.segment("Hi👨‍👩‍👦!")].map(s => s.segment);
// ["H", "i", "👨‍👩‍👦", "!"]

[...seg.segment(str)].length; // the human-visible length

The ladder: .length counts 16-bit units, [...str] counts code points, the segmenter counts graphemes — only the last matches what the user sees.

cards.question: What else can Intl.Segmenter split — words and sentences?
cards.answer:
granularity: "word" and "sentence" split by real language boundaries. Each word segment carries isWordLike — true for actual words, false for spaces and punctuation.

let seg = new Intl.Segmenter("en", {granularity: "word"});

[...seg.segment("Hello, world!")]
  .filter(s => s.isWordLike)
  .map(s => s.segment); // ["Hello", "world"]

This matters most where split(" ") is hopeless: Chinese and Japanese write without spaces, and the segmenter is the only built-in that knows where their words begin and end.

cards.question: How does Intl.PluralRules pick the right plural form?
cards.answer:
Intl.PluralRules tells which plural form a number takes in a language: select(n) returns a category — "one", "few", "many", "other" — and you map categories to message strings.

The n === 1 ? "item" : "items" hack only works in English. Russian has three forms, Arabic six — the rules are too irregular to hand-write, and the browser already knows them.

let pr = new Intl.PluralRules("en");
pr.select(1); // "one"
pr.select(5); // "other"

let forms = {one: `${n} item`, other: `${n} items`};
forms[pr.select(n)]; // correct in any language — just translate the forms

{type: "ordinal"} handles "1st / 2nd / 3rd".

cards.question: What do Intl.ListFormat and Intl.DisplayNames do?
cards.answer:
Intl.ListFormat joins an array into a natural-language list — the commas and the final "and"/"or" the way each locale writes them. Intl.DisplayNames translates codes — region, language, currency — into human-readable names.

Hand-joining with ", " gets the conjunction wrong everywhere outside English; the formatter knows each language's rules.

new Intl.ListFormat("en").format(["a", "b", "c"]); // "a, b, and c"
new Intl.ListFormat("de").format(["a", "b", "c"]); // "a, b und c"

new Intl.ListFormat("en", {type: "disjunction"}).format(["a", "b"]); // "a or b"

Intl.DisplayNames takes the target locale and a type, then of(code) returns the name — so you never ship your own code-to-name tables.

new Intl.DisplayNames("en", {type: "region"}).of("DE");   // "Germany"
new Intl.DisplayNames("de", {type: "language"}).of("en"); // "Englisch"

cards.question: What happens under the hood when a function calls itself — what is the execution context stack?
cards.answer:
Every function call gets an execution context — the engine's record of its local variables and the current position in the code. When a call makes a nested call, the running context is paused and pushed onto the execution context stack; the nested call runs in a fresh one, and when it returns, the old context is popped and resumed exactly where it left off.

Recursion is just this machinery feeding the same function:

function pow(x, n) {
  return n == 1 ? x : x * pow(x, n - 1);
}
pow(2, 3);
// stack: pow(2,3) paused → pow(2,2) paused → pow(2,1) returns 2
// then unwinds: 2 → 4 → 8

The number of nested calls, including the first, is the recursion depth. Each context takes memory, so depth is limited — engines reliably allow about 10 000 levels. Past the limit: RangeError: Maximum call stack size exceeded.

cards.question: Recursion or a loop — how do you choose?
cards.answer:
Any recursion can be rewritten as a loop, and the loop is usually cheaper — it keeps one context instead of a stack of them. Use recursion when it makes the code clearly simpler, which usually means the data itself is recursive.

function pow(x, n) {          // iterative: one context, no depth limit
  let result = 1;
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) result *= x;
  return result;
}

For a flat computation like this, the loop wins on memory and speed with no loss of clarity. For variable-depth structures — trees, nested departments, file systems — the recursive version mirrors the shape of the data, while the loop needs a hand-managed stack to do the same job.

Don't count on the engine to flatten recursion for you: JavaScript engines don't reliably optimize tail calls, so deep recursion can overflow even when the recursive call is the last thing in the function.

cards.question: How do you write a recursive traversal of a nested structure?
cards.answer:
Two parts: a base case — the simple situation where the answer is direct — and a recursive step that delegates to smaller pieces of the same shape. The call structure ends up mirroring the data structure.

// a department is either an array of people, or an object of subdepartments
function sumSalaries(department) {
  if (Array.isArray(department)) {                          // base case: a team
    return department.reduce((sum, p) => sum + p.salary, 0);
  }
  return Object.values(department)                          // recursive step
    .reduce((sum, sub) => sum + sumSalaries(sub), 0);
}

The function never asks how deep the nesting goes — each call handles one level and trusts the recursive calls with the rest. HTML trees, JSON, comment threads all fit the same two-part pattern.

cards.question: What is a linked list, and when is it better than an array?
cards.answer:
A linked list is a chain of objects, each holding a value and a next reference to the following element (null at the end). It's the textbook recursive structure: a list is a value plus a smaller list.

let list = { value: 1, next: {
  value: 2, next: {
    value: 3, next: null
} } };

list = { value: 0, next: list };   // prepend: O(1), just rewire
list.next = list.next.next;        // delete the second element: O(1)

That's the advantage over arrays: inserting or deleting at the front of an array renumbers every element after it, while a list just changes one reference.

The price: no cheap access by index — reaching the n-th element means walking the chain from the start. So lists fit fast queue/deque operations at the ends; arrays stay better when you need element [i] directly.

cards.question: How does a rest parameter ...args work?
cards.answer:
...args at the end of the parameter list gathers all remaining arguments into a real array — so a function can accept any number of arguments and still use map, reduce, and friends on them.

function sumAll(...args) {
  return args.reduce((sum, n) => sum + n, 0);
}
sumAll(1, 2, 3); // 6

function showName(first, last, ...titles) {
  // first two go to named parameters, the rest is gathered
}

The rest parameter must be last — function f(...args, last) is a SyntaxError, because "the rest" only makes sense at the end.

This is also what makes universal wrappers possible: function(...args) accepts whatever the caller sends and can hand it on unchanged.

cards.question: What does spread do in a function call, and what exactly can be spread?
cards.answer:
Spread is the mirror of rest: f(...arr) expands an iterable into a list of separate arguments. Rest gathers on the receiving side, spread unpacks on the calling side.

let arr = [3, 5, 1];
Math.max(...arr);                 // 5
Math.max(1, ...arr, 2, ...other); // mixes and combines freely

Math.max.apply(null, arr);        // the old pre-spread way

Spread works on any iterable — arrays, strings, Sets — because it uses the same iteration mechanism as for..of: [..."abc"] gives ["a","b","c"].

The catch: array-likes (objects with indexes and length but no Symbol.iterator) can't be spread — the engine has no way to iterate them. Array.from(obj) is the universal converter: it accepts both iterables and array-likes.

cards.question: How does spread work in array literals — merging and copying?
cards.answer:
Inside [ ], ...arr expands the array's elements in place — the standard way to merge arrays and to make copies.

let arr = [3, 5, 1], arr2 = [8, 9];

let merged = [0, ...arr, 2, ...arr2]; // [0, 3, 5, 1, 2, 8, 9]

let copy = [...arr];   // new array with the same elements
copy === arr;          // false — independent push/pop

let objCopy = { ...obj };  // the same idea exists for objects

The copy is shallow: the new array is independent, but objects inside it are shared references — mutate copy[0].name and arr[0].name changes too.

cards.question: When does a default parameter value kick in?
cards.answer:
Only when the argument is missing or is exactly undefined. Every other value passes through — including the falsy ones: null, 0, "".

function showMessage(from, text = "no text given") {
  alert(from + ": " + text);
}

showMessage("Ann");            // "Ann: no text given"
showMessage("Ann", undefined); // same — undefined triggers the default
showMessage("Ann", null);      // "Ann: null" — null is a value!
showMessage("Ann", "");        // "Ann: " — empty string is a value too

That undefined-only rule is the trap to remember. Old code uses text = text || "default" instead, which swallows all falsy values — the modern way to express that intent is ??, which, like defaults, only reacts to null/undefined.

cards.question: When is a default value evaluated?
cards.answer:
At call time, freshly on each call where the parameter is missing — a default is an ordinary expression, not a value computed once at definition.

function log(message, time = new Date()) {}       // a new Date per call

function greet(name, greeting = `Hi, ${name}`) {} // can use earlier parameters

function required(name) { throw new Error(name + " is required"); }
function save(data = required("data")) {}         // mandatory-argument idiom

Parameters initialize left to right, so a default may read the ones before it — but not the ones after.

And it's lazy: when the argument is provided, the default expression doesn't run at all. That's what makes the required() idiom work — the throw only fires for callers who omit the argument.

cards.question: What is the arguments object, and why did rest parameters replace it?
cards.answer:
arguments is a special array-like available inside every regular function, holding everything the caller passed. It predates rest parameters, which replaced it.

function showName() {
  arguments.length;    // works
  arguments[0];        // works
  arguments.map(...);  // TypeError — array-like, not a real array
}

Two limitations: it has indexes and length but no array methods, and it always grabs everything — there's no way to take only the tail after some named parameters, which ...rest does naturally.

One behavioral fact still worth knowing: arrow functions have no own arguments — they see the enclosing function's, just as with this. That transparency is exactly why arrows make clean forwarding wrappers. Today arguments matters mainly for reading older code.

cards.question: What is new.target?
cards.answer:
Inside a function, new.target is undefined for a regular call and equals the function itself when called with new — so a function can tell how it was invoked.

function User(name) {
  if (!new.target) {         // called without new
    return new User(name);   // fix it up — or throw
  }
  this.name = name;
}

User("John");  // works anyway, redirects to new User("John")

Class constructors run this check internally — that's precisely why calling a class without new throws. In your own constructor functions it's mostly a guard against the silent bug where a forgotten new makes this point at the wrong thing.

cards.question: What is a higher-order function?
cards.answer:
A function that takes a function as an argument, returns one, or both. It's what lets behavior travel around as a value.

users.map(u => u.name);          // map takes a function
f.bind(ctx);                      // bind returns one
slow = cachingDecorator(slow);    // a decorator does both

This pattern is everywhere in JavaScript: array methods, event handlers, callbacks, decorators, setTimeout — all of them are higher-order functions at work. Instead of writing the loop and the comparison and the bookkeeping every time, you hand a small function to a bigger one that knows the pattern.

cards.question: What makes a function pure, and why does purity matter?
cards.answer:
A pure function returns the same result for the same arguments and has no side effects — it reads no changing outside state and changes nothing outside itself: no shared variables, no I/O, no mutating its arguments.

let total = 0;
function addToTotal(x) { total += x; }  // impure — hidden state, order matters

function add(a, b) { return a + b; }    // pure — a fact, not an event

Purity is what makes a function trivially testable (call it, check the return value — no setup), safe to cache (same input, same output — the basis of the caching decorator), and safe to reorder or rerun.

Its companion idea is immutability: produce new values instead of mutating — map/filter/toSorted over splice/sort, spread copies over in-place edits. Shared references then stop changing under your feet.

cards.question: What is function composition?
cards.answer: Composition combines functions so the output of one becomes the input of the next: composing f and g gives a new function x => f(g(x)). Small single-purpose functions become one pipeline.

cards.question: How do you implement compose and pipe?
cards.answer:
const compose = (...fns) => x => fns.reduceRight((v, fn) => fn(v), x);
const pipe    = (...fns) => x => fns.reduce((v, fn) => fn(v), x);
const trim  = s => s.trim();
const lower = s => s.toLowerCase();
const dash  = s => s.replaceAll(" ", "-");
