const slugify = pipe(trim, lower, dash);  // dash(lower(trim(x)))
slugify("  Hello World "); // "hello-world"

cards.question: compose vs pipe — which direction does each apply?
cards.answer: compose applies right to left, matching the math notation f(g(x)); pipe applies left to right, reading in execution order. Each function must take one argument — the previous one's result.

cards.question: Why does function composition require pure functions?
cards.answer: Only side-effect-free functions can be combined without the order silently mattering. The working rule: a functional core with side effects at the edges — compute results with pure pipelines, then do the DOM update or DB write in one obvious place.

cards.question: What is a decorator? Walk through the caching decorator.
cards.answer:
A decorator is a function that takes another function and returns a wrapper with altered behavior — caching, logging, rate-limiting — while the original stays untouched. Write the logic once, apply it to any function.

function cachingDecorator(func) {
  let cache = new Map();
  return function(x) {
    if (cache.has(x)) return cache.get(x);
    let result = func(x);
    cache.set(x, result);
    return result;
  };
}

slow = cachingDecorator(slow);
// callers notice nothing — except repeated calls are now instant

The wrapper keeps cache in its closure and delegates the real work to func. Because the decorator is separate from the function, it's reusable, removable, and stackable — you can wrap one decorator in another.

This plain version has a hidden flaw, though: it only works for standalone functions. Decorate a method with it and things break — that's the next card.

cards.question: Why does a decorated method lose this, and how do call/apply fix it?
cards.answer:
Inside the wrapper, plain func(x) calls the original as a standalone function — so its this is undefined, and the first property access inside the method fails.

let worker = {
  someMethod() { return 1; },
  slow(x) { return x * this.someMethod(); }
};
worker.slow = cachingDecorator(worker.slow);
worker.slow(2); // Error: Cannot read properties of undefined

The fix: pass this through explicitly — func.call(context, ...args) and func.apply(context, args) run func with the this you choose (their full mechanics live in the this & Binding topic).

// in the decorator:
let result = func.call(this, x);  // worker.slow(2) → this === worker

It works because the wrapper was called as worker.slow(2), so the wrapper's own this is worker — it just has to hand it on instead of dropping it.

cards.question: What is call forwarding?
cards.answer:
Call forwarding is passing everything — this and all arguments — through to another function, so the wrapper is indistinguishable from the original from the outside.

let wrapper = function() {
  return func.apply(this, arguments);  // the canonical form
};

apply fits here because arguments is already an array-like — it goes through as one piece, whatever the caller sent.

This is the skeleton of every decorator: whatever extra behavior the wrapper adds, the actual call is a transparent forward — same this, same arguments, same return value.

cards.question: What is method borrowing?
cards.answer:
Method borrowing is taking a method from one object and running it on another via call — useful when the target has the right shape but lacks the method.

function hash() {
  return [].join.call(arguments);  // join an array-like that has no join
}

It works because join doesn't care what this really is — it only needs indexes and length. Most built-in array methods are written this generically, so array-likes like arguments can borrow them.

cards.question: What is currying, and what is it useful for?
cards.answer:
Currying transforms a function callable as f(a, b, c) into one callable as f(a)(b)(c). It doesn't call the function — it restructures how arguments arrive, so they can be supplied in stages.

The practical payoff is partial application: fix the first arguments once and get a specialized function back, instead of repeating them at every call site.

function log(date, importance, message) { ... }
log = curry(log);

let logNow = log(new Date());    // date fixed
let debugNow = logNow("DEBUG");  // date + importance fixed

debugNow("message");             // one argument left to supply

When you need just one partial and not the whole staged form, bind does it directly: send.bind(null, "admin@site.com") (binding mechanics live in the this topic).

cards.question: How is curry implemented, lodash-style?
cards.answer:
Collect arguments until there are at least as many as the function declares (func.length): enough — call it; not yet — return a function that collects more.

function curry(func) {
  return function curried(...args) {
    if (args.length >= func.length) {
      return func.apply(this, args);
    }
    return (...args2) => curried.apply(this, [...args, ...args2]);
  };
}

This "advanced" curry keeps the function callable normally too — log(new Date(), "INFO", "hi") hits the arity check immediately and just runs. Each partial call closes over the arguments gathered so far and merges in the new ones.

The limitation is baked into the check: it needs a fixed arity. A function declared with rest parameters has a useless length (rest doesn't count), so it can't be curried this way.

cards.question: Debounce vs throttle — what's the core difference?
cards.answer: Both tame a flood of calls. Debounce waits for a pause: the function runs once, ms after the calls stop. Throttle enforces a rhythm: at most one run per ms while calls keep coming.

cards.question: When do you use debounce vs throttle?
cards.answer:
Match the tool to the question.

QuestionToolExample
"What did the user end up with?"debouncesearch-as-you-type: one request after typing stops
"Where are things now, regularly?"throttlescroll-position updates, capped in frequency

cards.question: How do debounce and throttle behave on a burst of calls?
cards.answer:
// f called every 100ms for 1s, ms = 1000:
// calls:     f(1) f(2) f(3) ... f(10)
// debounce:  ......................... f(10)   once, 1s after the last call
// throttle:  f(1) .............. f(10)         twice: first immediately,
//                                              latest when the cooldown ends

Throttle doesn't miss the final state — it just refuses to run more often than the limit, while debounce skips everything but the last call of a burst.

cards.question: How do you implement debounce?
cards.answer:
It's a decorator around setTimeout: every call cancels the pending timer and schedules a new one — so only the last call in a burst actually runs, with its arguments and this intact.

function debounce(func, ms) {
  let timeout;
  return function(...args) {
    clearTimeout(timeout);
    timeout = setTimeout(() => func.apply(this, args), ms);
  };
}

input.oninput = debounce(sendRequest, 1000);
// fires once, 1s after typing stops

The pieces: timeout lives in the closure, shared across calls; clearTimeout wipes the previous schedule; the arrow inside setTimeout has no own this, so func.apply(this, args) forwards the wrapper's — the standard decorator forwarding.

The subtle point: if calls never pause for ms, the function never runs at all. That's by design — but it's also why debounce is wrong for "give me periodic updates", where throttle belongs.

cards.question: How do you implement throttle?
cards.answer:
A decorator with a cooldown flag: the first call runs immediately and starts the cooldown; calls during the cooldown aren't run, but the latest one is saved and runs when the cooldown ends.

function throttle(func, ms) {
  let isThrottled = false, savedArgs, savedThis;

  return function wrapper(...args) {
    if (isThrottled) {
      savedArgs = args;      // remember only the latest call
      savedThis = this;
      return;
    }
    func.apply(this, args);  // run now
    isThrottled = true;
    setTimeout(() => {
      isThrottled = false;
      if (savedArgs) {       // a call arrived during cooldown
        wrapper.apply(savedThis, savedArgs);
        savedArgs = savedThis = null;
      }
    }, ms);
  };
}

cards.question: In throttle, why must the trailing call go through wrapper, not func?
cards.answer: Calling wrapper (not func) both delivers the final state and restarts the cooldown, so the rhythm holds for any further calls.

cards.question: How does throttle preserve arguments and this for the trailing call?
cards.answer: savedArgs/savedThis live in the closure, and forwarding via apply keeps arguments and this intact — the standard decorator pattern.

cards.question: How does single-threaded JavaScript run code — what is the call stack?
cards.answer:
JavaScript executes one piece of code at a time. The call stack tracks where it is: every function call pushes a frame on top, every return pops it.

Code runs to completion: whatever started executing keeps going until the stack is empty again. Nothing can interrupt a running function halfway — no event, no timer, no other code.

function a() { b(); }   // stack: a
function b() { c(); }   // stack: a → b
function c() {}         // stack: a → b → c, then pops back down

The stack has a limit: recursion without a base case keeps pushing frames until RangeError: Maximum call stack size exceeded.

cards.question: What is the event loop?
cards.answer:
The event loop is the engine's endless cycle: take the oldest task from the queue, run it until the stack is empty, take the next — and sleep when there are none.

Tasks arrive while the engine is busy — a click, a timer firing, data from the network — and wait in a queue (first in, first out). Each one gets its turn only when the previous task has fully finished.

Examples of tasks: the script itself loading, an event handler run, a setTimeout callback. Most of the time the engine just sits idle, waiting for the next one.

cards.question: Why does a long task freeze the page?
cards.answer:
Because a task is never interrupted, and rendering and event handling only happen between tasks.

el.textContent = "Loading...";
heavyLoop(); // 3 seconds of CPU
// "Loading..." never appears — paint waits for the task to finish

DOM changes accumulate and paint only after the current task ends. While it runs, no clicks are handled, no scrolling, nothing — and if it runs long enough, the browser shows the "Page Unresponsive" dialog.

For computation that genuinely needs seconds of CPU, move it off the main thread with a Web Worker — it runs in a parallel thread and cannot block rendering or events.

cards.question: What is a macrotask?
cards.answer:
A macrotask is one unit of work the event loop picks from the task queue: the initial script, an event handler run, a setTimeout/setInterval callback, a network response handler.

They run strictly one per loop turn, in the order they were queued. Between two macrotasks the browser gets a chance to render.

The whole initial script is itself one macrotask — so a timer scheduled at the top still waits for the entire script to finish:

setTimeout(() => console.log("later")); // queued for the next turn
heavyWork();                            // still the same macrotask
console.log("first");
// first → later

cards.question: What is a microtask, and where do microtasks come from?
cards.answer:
A microtask is a job that runs immediately after the current code finishes — before rendering and before any other macrotask.

They come almost exclusively from promises: .then/.catch/.finally handlers, and the code after await. To queue one directly, without a promise:

queueMicrotask(() => { ... });

Use them when something must happen "right after this code, before the world changes" — e.g. before the next render or event.

cards.question: In what order do macrotasks, microtasks, and rendering run?
cards.answer:
One loop turn: run the oldest macrotask → drain the entire microtask queue → render if needed → take the next macrotask.

"Drain" means all of them — including microtasks queued by other microtasks. Hence the canonical ordering:

setTimeout(() => alert("timeout"));            // macrotask — next turn
Promise.resolve().then(() => alert("promise")); // microtask — right after this code
alert("code");

// code → promise → timeout

“Render if needed” is literal: the browser does not paint after every macrotask. It paints only when something changed and only about as often as the screen refreshes (~60 times a second) — between paints, many macrotasks may run.

cards.question: Why is a promise handler never called synchronously?
cards.answer:
Even if a promise is already resolved, its handler goes to the microtask queue — the current code always finishes first.

Promise.resolve().then(() => alert("promise"));
alert("code");
// code → promise, always

This makes ordering predictable: a .then behaves the same whether the promise settled long ago or just now. You never get a handler firing in the middle of your own code.

cards.question: Can microtasks starve rendering and events?
cards.answer:
Yes. The microtask queue drains completely before anything else, so a microtask that queues another microtask keeps the loop there — the same freeze as one long task.

function again() { queueMicrotask(again); }
again(); // page is dead: no rendering, no events, ever

Microtasks mean "finish this logical job before anything else happens", not "do this later". For "later", use setTimeout — it yields to rendering and events between runs.

cards.question: How do setTimeout and clearTimeout work?
cards.answer:
setTimeout(func, delay, ...args) schedules func to run once after at least delay ms and returns a timer id; clearTimeout(id) cancels it.

const id = setTimeout(sayHi, 1000, "John"); // args passed to sayHi
clearTimeout(id);                            // never runs

Pass a function reference, not a call: setTimeout(sayHi(), 1000) runs sayHi immediately and schedules its result — usually undefined.

Until it runs or is cancelled, the scheduled function (with its closure) is kept in memory — forgotten timers are a classic leak.

cards.question: What's wrong with setInterval for periodic work?
cards.answer:
setInterval(func, 100) fires every 100ms by the clock, ignoring how long func takes — so the real gap between the end of one run and the start of the next is smaller than 100ms.

setInterval(func, 100); // func itself takes 30ms

// clock:  0ms          100ms         200ms
//         |--func 30--|    |--func 30--|
//              gap ≈ 70ms      gap ≈ 70ms   — not 100

If func takes longer than the interval, runs go back-to-back with no gap at all. Stop it with clearInterval(id) — a forgotten interval runs (and holds its closure) forever.

cards.question: Why is nested setTimeout better for repeating work?
cards.answer:
Schedule the next run at the end of the current one — then the delay measures from finish to start, so the gap is guaranteed no matter how long the work takes.

function tick() {
  doWork();
  setTimeout(tick, 100); // next run scheduled only when this one is done
}
setTimeout(tick, 100);

It's also flexible: the next delay can depend on results — e.g. polling a server and backing off when it's overloaded.

cards.question: Is the setTimeout delay guaranteed?
cards.answer:
No — it's a minimum. The callback is a macrotask: it waits for the current code to finish and for its turn in the queue.

The browser adds its own limits: after five nested timers the minimum delay becomes ~4ms, and in background tabs timers may be throttled to once per second or less.

cards.question: What is setTimeout(f, 0) for?
cards.answer:
It runs f as soon as possible — right after the current script and its microtasks — not immediately.

The standard use: split CPU-heavy work into chunks so events and rendering squeeze in between, keeping the page responsive:

function countChunk() {
  // ...do a slice of the work...
  if (!done) setTimeout(countChunk); // yield, then continue
}

cards.question: Can you predict the output order of mixed sync, promise, and timer code?
cards.answer:
Apply the loop turn mechanically: 1) run the current script to the end, 2) drain all microtasks, 3) take macrotasks one by one.

console.log(1);

setTimeout(() => console.log(2));

Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log(3));
Promise.resolve().then(() => setTimeout(() => console.log(4)));
Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log(5));

setTimeout(() => console.log(6));

console.log(7);
// 1 7 3 5 2 6 4

Walk it: sync code prints 1, 7. The microtask drain prints 3, 5 — the middle handler just schedules a timer, which lands behind the existing macrotasks. Then macrotasks in queue order: 2, 6, and finally the late-scheduled 4.

cards.question: Where does await fit in the ordering?
cards.answer:
An async function runs synchronously until the first await; the rest of its body is queued as an ordinary microtask, in line with .then handlers.

Extend the previous puzzle with an async function:

console.log(1);

setTimeout(() => console.log(2));

(async () => {
  console.log(3);              // sync — runs immediately
  await Promise.resolve();
  console.log(4);              // microtask, queued first
})();

Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log(5)); // microtask, queued second

console.log(6);
// 1 3 6 4 5 2

Walk it: sync code prints 1, 3, 6 — the async body is plain synchronous code until the await. The drain then runs microtasks in arrival order: 4 before 5. The timer macrotask 2 is last.

Why await splits the function this way is covered in async/await — here only the timing matters.

cards.question: When are unhandled rejections detected?
cards.answer:
unhandledrejection — the event that fires when a rejected promise has no error handler (the event itself is covered in Promises) — fires when the microtask queue empties and the rejection is still unhandled.

So a .catch attached later in the same drain is still in time. One attached from a setTimeout arrives a turn too late — the event has already fired:

const p = Promise.reject(new Error("boom"));
setTimeout(() => p.catch(() => {}), 0); // too late
// unhandledrejection fires first, at the end of this turn

cards.question: Why do promises exist — what was wrong with callbacks?
cards.answer:
Before promises, async results were delivered through callbacks: you pass a function in, and it's called later with the result. It works for one step — but a sequence of async steps nests deeper and deeper, and the error check repeats at every level.

By convention the callback's first argument is reserved for the error — the error-first callback. Chain three steps and you get the "pyramid of doom":

loadScript("1.js", function(err, script) {
  if (err) return handleError(err);
  loadScript("2.js", function(err, script) {
    if (err) return handleError(err);
    loadScript("3.js", function(err, script) {
      if (err) return handleError(err);
      // ...one level deeper per step
    });
  });
});

Promises fix both problems: steps chain flat instead of nesting, and one .catch at the end handles an error from any step. That's the motivation for everything in this topic.

cards.question: What is a promise, and when does the executor run?
cards.answer:
A promise is an object that links "producing code" (which takes time to finish) with "consuming code" (which wants the result when it's ready). The function passed to new Promise — the executor — runs immediately, the moment the promise is created.

The executor receives two callbacks from JavaScript itself: call resolve(value) when the job succeeds, reject(error) when it fails. Reject with Error objects — code downstream expects them.

let promise = new Promise(function(resolve, reject) {
  // runs right now, not "later"
  setTimeout(() => resolve("done"), 1000);
  // or: setTimeout(() => reject(new Error("whoops")), 1000);
});

The executor usually starts something asynchronous, but it doesn't have to — resolve can be called at once if the result is already at hand.

cards.question: What are a promise's internal state and result?
cards.answer:
A promise holds two internal properties: state starts as "pending" and result as undefined; resolve(value) moves them to "fulfilled" + the value, reject(error) to "rejected" + the error.

A fulfilled or rejected promise is settled — and settling is final. Only the first resolve or reject counts; every later call is ignored:

let promise = new Promise(function(resolve, reject) {
  resolve("done");           // wins
  reject(new Error("nope")); // ignored
  resolve("again");          // ignored
});

state and result are internal — you can't read them directly. To get the outcome, you subscribe with .then/.catch/.finally.

cards.question: How do then, catch, and finally work?
cards.answer:
.then(onFulfilled, onRejected) subscribes to the outcome: the first handler gets the value, the second gets the error. .catch(fn) is shorthand for .then(null, fn). .finally(fn) runs either way.

promise.then(
  result => alert(result), // runs on resolve
  error => alert(error)    // runs on reject
);

promise.catch(alert); // errors only

A finally handler is for cleanup — stopping a spinner, closing a connection. It gets no arguments (it doesn't know how the promise settled) and passes the result or error through to the next handler untouched:

new Promise(resolve => resolve("value"))
  .finally(() => alert("cleanup")) // result flows past it
  .then(result => alert(result));  // "value"

cards.question: What if you attach a handler after the promise has already settled?
cards.answer:
It still runs — immediately, with the stored result. A promise remembers its outcome, so a handler added a second or an hour after settling gets the same value.

let promise = new Promise(resolve => resolve("done")); // settled at once

promise.then(alert); // "done" — runs right away (as a microtask)

This is the practical win over callbacks. With a callback you must hand the function over before starting the action, and you get exactly one. With promises the order flips: start the action first, decide what to do with the result later — and attach as many handlers as you like, whenever you like.

cards.question: Why can .then calls be chained — what does .then return?
cards.answer:
Because every .then returns a new promise, resolved with whatever the handler returns. The next .then subscribes to that new promise — so results flow down a pipeline, each handler feeding the next.

new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(() => resolve(1), 1000))
  .then(result => result * 2)     // 1 → returns 2
  .then(result => result * 2)     // 2 → returns 4
  .then(result => alert(result)); // 4

Return a plain value and the new promise fulfills with it right away. Return nothing and the next handler gets undefined.

cards.question: What happens when a handler returns a promise?
cards.answer:
The chain waits for it to settle and continues with its result. This is the rule that lets async steps run one after another without nesting.

new Promise(resolve => resolve(1))
  .then(result => {
    // chain pauses here for one second
    return new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(() => resolve(result * 2), 1000));
  })
  .then(result => alert(result)); // 2 — shown after the second

Precisely, the chain accepts any thenable — any object with a .then method is treated like a promise. That's why promise-like objects from libraries plug into chains seamlessly.

cards.question: What's the difference between chaining .then and attaching several .then to one promise?
cards.answer:
Only chaining forms a sequence. Several .then attached to the same promise don't pass results to each other — each independently receives the same original result.

let promise = new Promise(resolve => resolve(1));

// attaching — NOT a chain:
promise.then(r => r * 2); // gets 1
promise.then(r => r * 2); // also gets 1, not 2

// chaining — a real pipeline:
promise.then(r => r * 2).then(r => alert(r)); // 2

Attaching several handlers is occasionally what you want — independent reactions to one result. But for a sequence of steps it's the classic mistake: the code looks similar and behaves completely differently.

cards.question: How does a real fetch chain look?
cards.answer:
fetch(url) returns a promise of the response; response.json() returns another promise — of the parsed body. Chain them, returning each promise so the next step waits for it.

fetch("/article/user.json")
  .then(response => response.json()) // wait for the body
  .then(user => fetch(`https://api.github.com/users/${user.name}`))
  .then(response => response.json())
  .then(githubUser => showAvatar(githubUser.avatar_url));

A function that performs an async action should return its promise. Even if nobody chains on it today, someone will want to tomorrow — and splitting the steps into small promise-returning functions keeps each one reusable on its own.

cards.question: What is the invisible try..catch around executors and handlers?
cards.answer:
The code of the executor and of every .then/.catch handler runs inside a hidden try..catch: if it throws, the throw becomes a rejection — exactly as if reject were called with that error.

new Promise(() => { throw new Error("Whoops!"); })
  .catch(alert); // Error: Whoops!

// same for handlers — even programming errors:
Promise.resolve("ok")
  .then(() => JSON.parse("{bad json")) // SyntaxError → rejection
  .catch(alert);

The boundary: it only catches synchronous throws. An error thrown later — inside a setTimeout scheduled by the handler — happens after the handler has already returned. The chain never sees it, and no .catch will fire.

cards.question: Where does a rejection go in a chain — and where should .catch be placed?
cards.answer:
A rejection skips every .then success handler and jumps to the nearest rejection handler further down the chain. So one .catch at the end covers everything above it.

fetch("/user.json")
  .then(response => response.json())
  .then(user => fetch(`/avatars/${user.name}`))
  .then(showAvatar)
  .catch(error => alert(error.message)); // any step failed → lands here

The analogy: the .then handlers are the try block, .catch is the catch. Place it where you can actually do something about the error — at the end for "report and stop", in the middle when a failed step has a fallback and the chain should continue.

cards.question: How does a .catch recover from an error — or pass it on?
cards.answer:
If .catch handles the error and returns normally, the error is considered dealt with — the chain continues into the next .then. If it can't handle this particular error, it rethrows, and the error travels on to the next .catch.

fetchData()
  .catch(error => {
    if (error instanceof NetworkError) {
      return cachedData; // handled — chain resumes with this value
    }
    throw error;         // not mine — pass it down the chain
  })
  .then(data => render(data))
  .catch(error => reportFatal(error));

This recover-or-rethrow pattern is the promise version of catching only the exceptions you know how to fix and letting the rest bubble up.

cards.question: What happens to a rejection nobody catches?
cards.answer:
unhandledrejection is a global event the browser fires when a promise rejects and no handler catches the error. The rejection isn't silently lost — the engine tracks it and reports it.

window.addEventListener("unhandledrejection", event => {
  // event.promise — the rejected promise
  // event.reason  — the error
  sendToMonitoring(event.reason);
});

new Promise(() => { throw new Error("Whoops!"); }); // no .catch anywhere

The event object carries two properties: event.promise, the promise that rejected, and event.reason, the error itself. The error also shows up in the console; in Node the counterpart is the unhandledRejection process event. (When exactly the engine decides "nobody will handle this" is event-loop timing — covered in the Event Loop topic.)

Use the handler to log what slipped through — the real fix is always a .catch at the place that can handle the error.

cards.question: What does Promise.all do?
cards.answer:
Promise.all(iterable) runs promises in parallel and waits for all of them, resolving with an array of results — in the order of the input, no matter which finished first.

let urls = ["/user/john", "/user/ann", "/user/pete"];

Promise.all(urls.map(url => fetch(url)))
  .then(responses => {
    // responses[0] is john's — input order, not finish order
  });

It is fail-fast: if any promise rejects, Promise.all rejects immediately with that error, and the other results are ignored. (The other promises keep running — they're just no longer watched; there's no cancellation.)

Convenience: non-promise values in the iterable are passed to the result array as-is.

cards.question: How is Promise.allSettled different from Promise.all?
cards.answer:
Promise.allSettled waits for all promises to settle and never rejects — each slot of the result describes one outcome: {status: "fulfilled", value} or {status: "rejected", reason}.

Promise.allSettled(urls.map(url => fetch(url)))
  .then(results => {
    results.forEach((result, i) => {
      if (result.status === "fulfilled") render(result.value);
      else logError(urls[i], result.reason);
    });
  });

Use it when the tasks are independent and one failure shouldn't discard everyone else's results — fetching several users where one missing profile is fine. Promise.all is "all or nothing"; allSettled is "report every outcome".

cards.question: What do Promise.race and Promise.any do — and how do they differ?
cards.answer:
Promise.race settles with the first settled promise — success or error, whichever comes first. Promise.any waits for the first fulfilled one, ignoring rejections along the way.

// race: classic timeout pattern — an early error wins too
Promise.race([fetch(url), timeout(5000)]);

// any: first success — fallback mirrors
Promise.any(mirrors.map(url => fetch(url)));

If all promises reject, Promise.any rejects with an AggregateError — a special error holding every individual failure in its errors array:

Promise.any([
  Promise.reject(new Error("a")),
  Promise.reject(new Error("b"))
]).catch(error => alert(error.errors[1].message)); // "b"

cards.question: What are Promise.resolve and Promise.reject for?
cards.answer:
Promise.resolve(value) wraps a known value into an already-fulfilled promise; Promise.reject(error) makes an already-rejected one.

They matter when an API must always return a promise but sometimes has the answer synchronously — a cache hit, an early exit:

function loadCached(url) {
  if (cache.has(url)) {
    return Promise.resolve(cache.get(url)); // same interface as the slow path
  }
  return fetch(url)
    .then(response => response.text())
    .then(text => { cache.set(url, text); return text; });
}

In modern code async functions cover most of this — whatever they return is wrapped automatically — so Promise.resolve appears rarely and Promise.reject almost never.

cards.question: What is promisification?
cards.answer:
Promisification is converting a function that accepts a callback into a function that returns a promise — so old callback-style APIs can join chains and await.

The two styles for the same job:

// callback-style: result delivered to a function you pass in
loadScript("script.js", (err, script) => { ... });

// promise-style: result comes out as a promise
loadScriptPromise("script.js").then(script => ...);

Much of Node's standard library and many older APIs are callback-based with the error-first convention: the callback's first argument is the error (or null), the rest are results. Promisification is the bridge between those and modern promise code.

cards.question: How do you write a promisify helper?
cards.answer:
promisify(f) returns a wrapper that returns a promise: the wrapper appends its own error-first callback to the arguments, and that callback settles the promise.

function promisify(f) {
  return function(...args) {
    return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
      args.push((err, result) => { // our callback, appended last
        if (err) reject(err);
        else resolve(result);
      });
      f.call(this, ...args);       // call the original, keeping this
    });
  };
}

let loadScriptPromise = promisify(loadScript);
loadScriptPromise("path/script.js").then(script => ...);

That's the whole trick: the original function thinks it's talking to a normal callback; the wrapper translates the call into resolve/reject.

cards.question: What about callbacks with several results — and what can't be promisified?
cards.answer:
If the callback delivers several results — callback(err, res1, res2) — make a variant like promisify(f, true) that resolves with an array of results, since a promise carries a single value.

args.push((err, ...results) => {
  if (err) reject(err);
  else resolve(manyArgs ? results : results[0]);
});

You rarely write this by hand: Node ships util.promisify, and modern Node exposes promise APIs directly (e.g. fs/promises).

The structural limit: a promise settles once. Promisification fits "call me back with the result" functions only — something that calls its callback repeatedly (an event listener, a watcher) loses every call after the first. Those need events or async iteration, not a promise.

cards.question: How would you implement Promise.all yourself?
cards.answer:
Count completions, store each result at its input index, resolve when the counter hits the total, reject on the first error.

function promiseAll(iterable) {
  return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    let promises = [...iterable];
    let results = new Array(promises.length);
    let remaining = promises.length;
    if (remaining === 0) return resolve(results); // empty input

    promises.forEach((p, i) => {
      Promise.resolve(p).then(value => {
        results[i] = value;
        if (--remaining === 0) resolve(results);
      }, reject);
    });
  });
}

cards.question: In a hand-rolled Promise.all, why store results by input index instead of pushing them?
cards.answer: Storing at results[i] pins output order to input order, no matter which promise finishes first. Pushing would order results by finish time.

cards.question: In a hand-rolled Promise.all, why use a counter instead of results.length to detect completion?
cards.answer: Slots fill out of order, so the array is sparse until the end — results.length would lie. A separate remaining counter (decremented per settle) is the honest signal.

cards.question: Why wrap each item in Promise.resolve(p) inside Promise.all?
cards.answer: Promise.resolve(p) normalizes non-promise values into promises, so plain values in the iterable are handled the same way as real promises.

cards.question: In a hand-rolled Promise.all, why is it safe that a later rejection still calls reject?
cards.answer: Settling is final: the first rejection settles the outer promise, and every later resolve/reject call is silently ignored. So the first rejection wins.

cards.question: How would you implement Promise.allSettled?
cards.answer:
Like Promise.all, but never reject: both handlers store a result object, and the counter alone decides completion.

function allSettled(iterable) {
  return new Promise(resolve => {
    let promises = [...iterable];
    let results = new Array(promises.length);
    let remaining = promises.length;
    if (remaining === 0) return resolve(results);

    promises.forEach((p, i) => {
      Promise.resolve(p).then(
        value  => { results[i] = { status: "fulfilled", value }; },
        reason => { results[i] = { status: "rejected", reason }; }
      ).then(() => {
        if (--remaining === 0) resolve(results);
      });
    });
  });
}

cards.question: How would you implement Promise.race?
cards.answer:
Drop the counter entirely — wire every promise straight to resolve/reject.

function race(iterable) {
  return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    for (let p of iterable) Promise.resolve(p).then(resolve, reject);
  });
}

cards.question: Why does the one-line Promise.race implementation work?
cards.answer: Because settling is final: every promise tries to settle the result, but only the first one to settle succeeds — the rest are ignored.

cards.question: How would you implement Promise.any?
cards.answer: any is the mirror of all: resolve on the first success, count failures, and reject with an AggregateError when all of them fail.

cards.question: What does the async keyword do?
cards.answer:
async before a function does two things: the function always returns a promise, and await becomes usable inside its body. A returned plain value is wrapped in a resolved promise automatically.

async function f() {
  return 1;
}

f().then(alert); // 1 — same as returning Promise.resolve(1)

To the caller it's an ordinary promise — chain it, pass it to Promise.all, await it from another async function.

If the function returns a promise itself, there's no double wrapping — the async function's promise simply follows it to the same result.

cards.question: How do results and errors leave an async function?
cards.answer:
Through the returned promise, always: return resolves it, throw rejects it. An async function never throws synchronously to the caller.

async function fail() {
  throw new Error("oops");  // no sync throw —
}

fail().catch(alert);        // — the promise rejects instead

This symmetry is the whole contract: success and failure travel through one channel, so the caller handles an async function like any other promise-returning one.

It holds even for errors thrown before the first await — they still arrive as a rejection, never as an exception at the call site.

cards.question: Which function forms can be async?
cards.answer:
All of them: declarations, expressions, arrows, object and class methods. The contract is the same everywhere — returns a promise, can use await inside.

async function f() {}              // declaration
const g = async () => {};          // arrow
const api = { async load() {} };   // object method
class User {
  async save() {}                  // class method — also returns a promise
}

The exceptions: constructors and getters/setters can't be async — a constructor must return the instance, not a promise. The usual workaround is a static factory method: static async create() { ... }.

cards.question: What does await do?
cards.answer:
await promise pauses the function until the promise settles, then returns its result. Async code reads top-to-bottom, like sync code.

async function showAvatar() {
  let response = await fetch("/user.json");
  let user = await response.json();   // runs only after fetch settled
  let img = await loadImage(user.avatar);
  img.show();
}

Under the hood it is the same mechanism as promise.then — no callbacks, no chain. Each await line gets the fulfilled value directly instead of receiving it in a .then(user => ...) handler.

cards.question: Does await block the thread?
cards.answer:
No. It suspends only this function — the engine keeps running other code, handling events, and rendering while it waits.

await splits the function in two: everything before it runs synchronously with the caller, everything after is queued as a microtask when the promise settles.

async function f() {
  console.log("start");   // synchronous — runs with the caller
  await null;             // even awaiting nothing yields here
  console.log("after");   // microtask
}

f();
console.log("caller");
// start → caller → after

So the first await is the exact point where control returns to the caller — the detail ordering puzzles love to test.

cards.question: What happens when you await a non-promise or a thenable?
cards.answer:
Both work. A plain value is converted to a resolved promise, so await 1 just gives 1. An object with a .then method (a thenable) is awaited like a real promise.

class Thenable {
  constructor(num) { this.num = num; }
  then(resolve, reject) {
    setTimeout(() => resolve(this.num * 2), 1000);
  }
}

let result = await new Thenable(1); // 2, after 1 second

Thenable support means await accepts promise-like objects from third-party libraries without conversion.

Note that even await 1 still yields once before continuing — it never continues synchronously.

cards.question: Where is await allowed?
cards.answer:
Inside async functions, and at the top level of modules. In a regular function it's a syntax error.

function f() {
  let x = await fetch("/data"); // SyntaxError — the function isn't async
}

// at the top level of a module — fine, no wrapper needed:
let response = await fetch("/config.json");
let config = await response.json();

Top-level await makes the whole module wait: anything that imports it pauses until the awaits finish. Handy for loading config once; risky for slow requests — they delay every importer.

Outside modules (plain scripts, CommonJS) the old workaround still applies: wrap the code in an async IIFE — (async () => { ... })().

cards.question: What happens when an awaited promise rejects?
cards.answer:
await throws the rejection at that line — exactly as if a throw statement stood there. So ordinary try..catch handles async failures like sync ones.

async function loadUser() {
  try {
    let response = await fetch("/no-such-user.json");
    let user = await response.json();
    return user;
  } catch (err) {
    // network error from fetch OR parse error from json() — one place
    alert(err);
  }
}

One try..catch can wrap any number of awaits — the first failure jumps straight to catch, skipping the rest. That's the big win over attaching a .catch to every step.

cards.question: What if there's no try..catch inside — who handles the error?
cards.answer:
The error rejects the async function's own promise. The caller handles it — typically with .catch at the outermost level.

async function loadUser() {
  let response = await fetch("/no-such-user.json"); // throws here...
  // ...
}

loadUser().catch(alert); // ...and lands here

The classic leak is fire-and-forget: calling an async function with no await and no .catch. The returned promise is dropped, and any failure becomes an unhandled rejection — unhandledrejection fires, and in Node the process may crash.

button.onclick = () => saveDraft();                  // failure vanishes
button.onclick = () => saveDraft().catch(showError); // handled

cards.question: return fetchData() vs return await fetchData() — does the await matter?
cards.answer:
Inside try it matters: return fetchData() leaves the try block before the promise rejects, so the catch never fires. return await keeps the rejection inside.

try {
  return fetchData();        // rejection happens AFTER we left the try
} catch (e) { ... }          // never catches it

try {
  return await fetchData();  // await surfaces the rejection right here
} catch (e) { ... }          // works

return await also keeps this function in the async stack trace — without it, the error skips this frame and looks like it came from nowhere.

Outside a try, with no cleanup to run after, the two behave the same for the caller — there a plain return fetchData() is fine.

cards.question: Should you mix async/await with .then/.catch?
cards.answer:
Sparingly. Inside an async function, await + try..catch reads better; .then/.catch belongs at the boundary — places where you can't await.

// top level of a plain (non-module) script — can't await:
loadUser().catch(alert);

Mixing styles mid-function — await fetch(url).then(r => r.json()) — works but makes the flow zigzag between two idioms. Pick one per function.

Since async functions return regular promises, the styles compose freely across function boundaries: an await-style function can be consumed with .then, and vice versa.

cards.question: Why do sequential awaits make independent work slow?
cards.answer:
Each await waits for the previous line to finish — so two independent 1-second requests take 2 seconds for no reason. Independent work belongs in Promise.all.

// sequential — 2 seconds:
let user = await fetchUser();    // 1s
let posts = await fetchPosts();  // 1s, starts only after user arrived

// parallel — 1 second:
let [user2, posts2] = await Promise.all([fetchUser(), fetchPosts()]);

The test: does the second call need the first one's result? If yes, sequential is correct. If no, awaiting in a row is accidental serialization — the most common async/await performance bug.

If any promise rejects, Promise.all rejects immediately with that error — and await throws it, catchable as usual.

cards.question: How do you start work early and await it later?
cards.answer:
Call the functions first — a promise starts running the moment it's created — then await the results where you need them.

let userPromise = fetchUser();    // both requests
let postsPromise = fetchPosts();  // already in flight

let user = await userPromise;     // just collects results
let posts = await postsPromise;

Same parallelism as Promise.all, useful when the awaits live in different places — start a fetch at the top, consume it only down one branch.

The gotcha: if postsPromise rejects while you're still awaiting userPromise, that rejection sits without a handler for a moment — enough to trigger unhandledrejection. For "all or nothing", Promise.all is the safer spelling.

cards.question: How does await behave in loops?
cards.answer:
await inside for..of processes items strictly one at a time. Sometimes that's wanted — order matters, a rate-limited API — but often it's accidental serialization.

for (let url of urls) {
  results.push(await fetch(url));  // serial — n × latency
}

let results2 = await Promise.all(urls.map(url => fetch(url))); // concurrent

forEach(async ...) is the broken third option: it neither waits nor collects — the promises just escape, and their errors become unhandled rejections.

for await..of is a different tool — it consumes async iterables, covered under Generators.

cards.question: How do you limit concurrency — neither one-by-one nor all at once?
cards.answer: Use a worker pool: n loops share one iterator, each pulling the next item as it finishes — so at most n operations are ever in flight.

cards.question: Why limit concurrency instead of just running everything in parallel?
cards.answer: Full parallelism isn't always safe — a thousand simultaneous fetches will exhaust sockets, trip rate limits, or hammer the server. Serial is too slow. The pool is the middle ground.

cards.question: Write a concurrency-limited pool in ~10 lines.
cards.answer:
async function pool(items, n, worker) {
  let it = items.values();                    // shared iterator = the queue
  await Promise.all(Array.from({length: n}, async () => {
    for (let item of it) await worker(item);  // each loop pulls the next free item
  }));
}

await pool(urls, 5, url => fetch(url));       // max 5 concurrent

cards.question: What makes the worker-pool trick work — why no item runs twice and no worker idles?
cards.answer: The shared iterator: every for..of pulls from the same sequence, so no item runs twice and no worker sits idle while items remain.

cards.question: In real projects, do you hand-write a concurrency pool?
cards.answer: Usually no — you reach for a library like p-limit or p-map. But the ten-line version is a favorite interview exercise.

cards.question: What is a generator function, and what happens when you call it?
cards.answer:
A generator is a function that can pause and resume, producing values one at a time. It's declared with function*, and calling it runs no code — it returns a special generator object that controls the execution.

The main method is next(): it runs the body to the nearest yield, pauses there, and returns {value, done} — the yielded value and whether the function has finished.

function* generateSequence() {
  yield 1;
  yield 2;
  return 3;
}

let gen = generateSequence(); // nothing ran yet

gen.next(); // {value: 1, done: false} — paused at the first yield
gen.next(); // {value: 2, done: false}
gen.next(); // {value: 3, done: true}  — return ends it
gen.next(); // {value: undefined, done: true} — done stays done

Between calls the generator object keeps everything: local variables and the exact position in the body — so each next() resumes exactly where the last one paused.

cards.question: How do for..of and spread work on a generator?
cards.answer:
Generators are iterable: for..of and spread consume them directly, calling next() under the hood.

function* generateSequence() {
  yield 1;
  yield 2;
  return 3;
}

for (let value of generateSequence()) alert(value); // 1, 2 — not 3!
[...generateSequence()];                            // [1, 2]

The catch: iteration stops as soon as it sees done: true and drops that value — so the return value never shows up. If every value should be seen, yield them all.

Also: a generator object can be iterated once. After it's done it stays done — call the generator function again for a fresh one.

cards.question: Why are generators good for lazy and infinite sequences?
cards.answer:
A generator computes values only when asked: each next() runs just to the next yield. So an infinite sequence is fine — it costs nothing until consumed.

function* pseudoRandom(seed) {
  let value = seed;
  while (true) {
    value = value * 16807 % 2147483647;
    yield value;
  }
}

let gen = pseudoRandom(1);
gen.next().value; // 16807
gen.next().value; // 282475249 — same seed, same sequence, on demand

That's lazy evaluation: the state lives in the paused generator, and you pull exactly as many values as you need.

The trap: never feed an infinite generator to spread or to for..of without a break — [...gen] tries to collect all values and never finishes.

cards.question: How do generators simplify writing custom iterables?
cards.answer:
Make Symbol.iterator a generator — yield replaces all the bookkeeping a hand-written iterator needs.

A manual iterator is a separate object with a next() method tracking its own state. A generator does the same job in a few lines:

let range = {
  from: 1,
  to: 5,
  *[Symbol.iterator]() { // shorthand for [Symbol.iterator]: function*()
    for (let value = this.from; value <= this.to; value++) {
      yield value;
    }
  }
};

alert([...range]); // 1,2,3,4,5

It works because a generator returns exactly what for..of expects: an object with next() returning {value, done}. This is the standard way to make an object iterable.

cards.question: What does yield* do?
cards.answer:
yield* delegates to another generator (or any iterable): its values are yielded out as if they were written inline.

function* generateSequence(start, end) {
  for (let i = start; i <= end; i++) yield i;
}

function* generatePasswordCodes() {
  yield* generateSequence(48, 57);  // 0..9
  yield* generateSequence(65, 90);  // A..Z
  yield* generateSequence(97, 122); // a..z
}

The composition is transparent: values flow straight from the inner generator to the consumer, one at a time — no intermediate arrays, no extra memory, even if the inner sequence is huge.

yield* gen is just a shorter way to write for (let value of gen) yield value;

cards.question: How does next(arg) pass a value into a generator?
cards.answer:
yield is a two-way street: it passes a value out, and generator.next(arg) passes arg back in — it becomes the result of the paused yield expression.

function* gen() {
  let result = yield "2 + 2 = ?"; // out: the question; in: the answer

  alert(result); // 4
}

let generator = gen();

let question = generator.next().value; // "2 + 2 = ?" — paused at yield
generator.next(4);                     // resumes; yield evaluates to 4

Mind the offset: the first next() only starts the generator — there's no paused yield for its argument to land in, so call it without one. Each later next(value) answers the previous yield.

This turns a generator into a conversation: the outer code can wait as long as it likes before answering — the generator just stays paused.

cards.question: What do generator.throw and generator.return do?
cards.answer:
They inject control instead of data. generator.throw(err) makes the paused yield throw err inside the generator; generator.return(value) finishes it immediately with {value, done: true}.

function* gen() {
  try {
    let result = yield "2 + 2 = ?";
  } catch(e) {
    alert(e.message); // "No answer" — caught like a normal error
  }
}

let g = gen();
g.next();
g.throw(new Error("No answer"));

If the generator doesn't catch it, the error falls back out of the throw(...) call — same as any uncaught error.

g.return("done"); // {value: "done", done: true} — body abandoned

return is for stopping a generator you no longer need. Together, next/throw/return let outer code resume, feed, error, or abort a paused computation — the protocol async/await is built on.

cards.question: What is async iteration?
cards.answer: A protocol for consuming data that arrives over time: the object provides Symbol.asyncIterator, its next() returns a promise of {value, done}, and for await..of awaits each step.

cards.question: How does the async iteration protocol differ from the sync one?
cards.answer:
Three changes: the symbol, what next() returns, and the loop keyword.

IterablesAsync iterables
methodSymbol.iteratorSymbol.asyncIterator
next() returns{value, done}a promise of {value, done}
loopfor..offor await..of

cards.question: How do you write an object with Symbol.asyncIterator?
cards.answer:
Return an iterator whose next() is async, so it can await inside before returning {value, done}.

let range = {
  from: 1, to: 5,
  [Symbol.asyncIterator]() {
    return {
      current: this.from,
      last: this.to,
      async next() {
        await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 1000)); // can await inside
        return this.current <= this.last
          ? { done: false, value: this.current++ }
          : { done: true };
      }
    };
  }
};

for await (let value of range) alert(value); // 1..5, one per second

cards.question: Where can for await..of be used?
cards.answer: Only in an async context — an async function or the module top level. It awaits each next() result before continuing.

cards.question: What is an async generator?
cards.answer:
An async generator — async function* — is a generator that may await between yields. It's the practical way to write an async iterable: the hand-written Symbol.asyncIterator object shrinks to a few lines.

async function* generateSequence(start, end) {
  for (let i = start; i <= end; i++) {
    await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, 1000));
    yield i;
  }
}

for await (let value of generateSequence(1, 5)) {
  alert(value); // 1..5, one per second
}

Its next() returns a promise of {value, done} — so manual stepping needs await generator.next().

To make an object asynchronously iterable, supply Symbol.asyncIterator as an async generator:

let range = {
  from: 1, to: 5,
  async *[Symbol.asyncIterator]() {
    for (let value = this.from; value <= this.to; value++) {
      await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 1000));
      yield value;
    }
  }
};

cards.question: How do async generators flatten a paginated API?
cards.answer:
Wrap the page-following loop in an async generator that yields items one by one — consumers see a flat stream and the pagination disappears.

async function* fetchCommits(repo) {
  let url = `https://api.github.com/repos/${repo}/commits`;

  while (url) {
    const response = await fetch(url);
    const body = await response.json(); // one page of commits

    // the URL of the next page is in the headers
    let nextPage = response.headers.get("Link").match(/<(.*?)>; rel="next"/);
    url = nextPage?.[1];

    for (let commit of body) {
      yield commit; // hand them out one at a time
    }
  }
}

cards.question: Why does breaking out of an async generator early avoid wasted requests?
cards.answer:
The generator fetches the next page only when the current one runs out, so a consumer that breaks early never triggers extra requests.

let count = 0;
for await (let commit of fetchCommits("javascript-tutorial/en.javascript.info")) {
  console.log(commit.author.login);
  if (++count >= 100) break; // stop early — no wasted requests
}

cards.question: What is the signature use case for async generators?
cards.answer: Anything that arrives chunk by chunk: paginated APIs, file and network streams.

cards.question: Which regular iteration features don't work with async iterables?
cards.answer:
Anything that expects Symbol.iterator: spread, Array.from, destructuring, plain for..of. Async iterables only have Symbol.asyncIterator, so these fail.

[...asyncRange]; // TypeError: asyncRange is not iterable

It can't work even in principle: those features are synchronous, and the values don't exist yet — they arrive as promises resolve.

To collect an async sequence into an array, gather it in a loop:

let results = [];
for await (let value of gen) results.push(value);
// or the modern one-liner: await Array.fromAsync(gen)

cards.question: How does try..catch work, and what's inside the error object?
cards.answer:
try runs the code; if an error happens there, the rest of try is skipped and catch (err) runs with the error object. No error — catch is skipped entirely. Either way the script survives and continues after the block.

The error object has three main properties: name — the error class ("ReferenceError"), message — the human-readable text, and stack — the call trace at the moment of the error, used for logging.

try {
  lalala;                  // error — jumps to catch
  alert("never reached");
} catch (err) {
  alert(err.name);    // ReferenceError
  alert(err.message); // lalala is not defined
}
alert("execution continues");

If the error details aren't needed, the binding is optional — a bare catch { ... } without (err) works.

cards.question: Which errors can try..catch not catch?
cards.answer:
try..catch only catches runtime errors in synchronous code. Out of its reach: parse errors and errors thrown later in scheduled callbacks. One exception: try { await f() } catch works, because await turns a promise rejection into a regular throw at that line.

Parse errors: code with broken syntax never starts running, so there's nothing to catch — the engine fails while reading the code, before try even exists.

Scheduled callbacks: by the time a setTimeout callback throws, the engine has already left the try..catch block. The handler must live inside the callback:

try {
  setTimeout(() => { noSuchVariable; }, 1000);
} catch (err) { /* never runs */ }

setTimeout(() => {
  try { noSuchVariable; } catch (err) { /* works */ }
}, 1000);

cards.question: What can you throw in JavaScript, and what should you throw?
cards.answer:
throw can raise any value — a string, a number, anything — but the convention is to throw Error objects, so every error carries name, message, and a stack.

throw "oops";              // legal, but bad
throw new Error("oops");    // do this

cards.question: What is the discipline for writing a catch block?
cards.answer: Catch only what you know, rethrow the rest. A catch sees every error from its try — expected failures and genuine bugs alike — so one that handles everything also swallows bugs: the bug stops the work but nothing reports it.

cards.question: How do you handle a known error type but rethrow the rest?
cards.answer:
Check the type inside catch; handle the ones you expect, and throw err for the rest so real bugs aren't swallowed.

try {
  let user = JSON.parse(json);
  if (!user.name) throw new SyntaxError("Incomplete data: no name");
  blabla(); // a real bug — must not vanish
} catch (err) {
  if (err instanceof SyntaxError) {
    alert("JSON Error: " + err.message); // expected — handle
  } else {
    throw err;                           // not ours — pass it up
  }
}

cards.question: Where does a rethrown error go?
cards.answer: A rethrown error behaves like any throw: it travels to the next enclosing try..catch up the call stack, or to the global handler if nothing catches it.

cards.question: What does finally guarantee?
cards.answer:
finally runs on any exit from the block: after success, after catch, and even when try leaves early with return or throw.

That makes it the home for cleanup that must happen no matter what — stop a timer, close a connection, release a resource:

function f() {
  try {
    return 1;          // return is scheduled...
  } finally {
    alert("finally");  // ...but this runs first
  }
}

try..finally without catch is valid and useful: "I don't handle errors here, but my cleanup must run." The error continues up after finally finishes.

Gotcha: a return inside finally overrides the result (or error) from try — don't return from finally.

cards.question: What happens to an error that nothing catches?
cards.answer:
It kills the current execution and reaches the environment's global handler: window.onerror in the browser, process.on("uncaughtException") in Node.

The global handler is for logging, not recovery — by the time it fires, the script that threw has already died. Its job is to report the failure so developers see it:

window.onerror = function(message, url, line, col, error) {
  // send to an error-tracking service
};

That's exactly how error-tracking services work: their snippet sets the global handler and ships every uncaught error to a dashboard.

cards.question: What built-in error types exist?
cards.answer:
The built-in types tell you what kind of mistake happened: ReferenceError — unknown identifier; TypeError — a value used the wrong way; SyntaxError — unparseable code or JSON; RangeError — a value out of allowed range.

undefinedVar;          // ReferenceError
null.foo;              // TypeError
JSON.parse("{bad");    // SyntaxError
new Array(-1);         // RangeError

All of them extend Error, so err instanceof Error catches the whole family. AggregateError (thrown by Promise.any) bundles several errors into one, exposed as err.errors.

You can throw and extend them like any error class — but a built-in type thrown where you didn't expect one usually signals a bug, not an input problem.

cards.question: What's the difference between operational and programmer errors?
cards.answer:
Operational errors are expected failures of a correct program — network down, file missing, invalid user input. Programmer errors are bugs — undefined where an object was promised, a broken invariant.

They demand opposite treatment:

— Operational: handle them — catch, retry, show a message, fall back. This is what catch blocks and custom error classes exist for.

— Programmer: don't handle them at the catch site — there's nothing sensible to do, and "handling" only hides the bug. Let them crash loudly or reach the global logger so they get fixed.

This split is the reasoning behind "catch what you know, rethrow the rest": your custom errors mark the operational vocabulary you handle; everything else is presumed a bug and passes through.

cards.question: How do you create a custom error class?
cards.answer:
Extend Error, call super(message), and set this.name — that's the whole recipe.

super(message) is what fills this.message; the parent constructor does it for you. And without setting this.name, the error keeps the inherited name "Error" — the most common mistake.

class ValidationError extends Error {
  constructor(message) {
    super(message);                // sets this.message
    this.name = "ValidationError"; // otherwise stays "Error"
  }
}

function readUser(json) {
  let user = JSON.parse(json);
  if (!user.name) throw new ValidationError("No field: name");
  return user;
}

Why bother with a class at all: it gives the error an identity that handling code can test for, instead of guessing from message text.

cards.question: Why check errors with instanceof instead of err.name?
cards.answer:
instanceof keeps working when the error class gets extended later; comparing err.name strings only matches the exact class.

If tomorrow PropertyRequiredError extends ValidationError appears, the instanceof ValidationError branch catches it automatically — the name comparison silently misses it.

try {
  readUser('{"age": 25}');
} catch (err) {
  if (err instanceof ValidationError) {
    alert("Invalid data: " + err.message); // ours, incl. subclasses
  } else if (err instanceof SyntaxError) {
    alert("JSON Error: " + err.message);   // bad JSON
  } else {
    throw err;                             // unknown — rethrow
  }
}

Same rethrowing rule as always: the final else throw err lets unknown errors — likely bugs — pass through instead of being swallowed.

cards.question: How do deeper custom-error hierarchies work?
cards.answer:
Custom errors are ordinary classes — extend your own error to add detail, the same way you extended Error.

class PropertyRequiredError extends ValidationError {
  constructor(property) {
    super("No property: " + property);
    this.name = "PropertyRequiredError";
    this.property = property; // extra data for handlers
  }
}

Now a handler can read err.property instead of parsing the message — and err instanceof ValidationError is still true.

Setting this.name in every subclass gets repetitive. A small base class removes the boilerplate for the whole family:

class MyError extends Error {
  constructor(message) {
    super(message);
    this.name = this.constructor.name; // each subclass names itself
  }
}

class ValidationError extends MyError {}

cards.question: What problem does wrapping exceptions solve?
cards.answer:
A function that can fail in many low-level ways shouldn't force every caller to check each type. Instead it throws one higher-level error meaning "this operation failed", with the low-level one kept inside.

The problem shape: readUser can hit a SyntaxError (bad JSON) or a ValidationError (wrong data) — and tomorrow a third kind. Without wrapping, every caller grows a new instanceof branch each time the internals change.

With wrapping, callers test a single type — ReadError, "reading the user failed" — and the function is free to change how it fails internally.

It's the error-side of abstraction layers: each layer speaks its own error vocabulary and translates the errors of the layer below.

cards.question: How do you wrap a low-level error in a higher-level one without losing detail?
cards.answer:
Catch the known low-level errors inside the function, throw a higher-level error, and store the original in its cause option — detail is never lost, just moved one level down.

class ReadError extends Error {
  constructor(message, options) {
    super(message, options); // forwards { cause }
    this.name = "ReadError";
  }
}

function readUser(json) {
  try {
    let user = JSON.parse(json);
    validateUser(user);
  } catch (err) {
    if (err instanceof SyntaxError)
      throw new ReadError("Syntax Error", { cause: err });
    if (err instanceof ValidationError)
      throw new ReadError("Validation Error", { cause: err });
    throw err; // unknown stays unwrapped — still rethrow!
  }
}

cards.question: How does a caller use a wrapped error and reach the original?
cards.answer:
The caller checks the one high-level type and drills into e.cause only when it needs the original.

try {
  readUser(json);
} catch (e) {
  if (e instanceof ReadError) console.log("Original:", e.cause);
  else throw e;
}

{ cause } is the standard ES2022 slot for the original error.

cards.question: Which errors should you wrap, and which should stay unwrapped?
cards.answer: Only wrap the errors you anticipated. An unknown error wrapped into a higher-level type disguises a bug as an expected failure — rethrow it untouched instead.

cards.question: What is the error cause option?
cards.answer:
Since ES2022, every Error constructor takes an options object with cause — the standard slot for "the error that led to this one". It's the wrapping-exceptions pattern, built into the language.

try {
  await fetch("/api/user");
} catch (err) {
  throw new Error("Failed to load user profile", { cause: err });
}

// upstream:
catch (e) {
  e.message; // "Failed to load user profile" — this layer's story
  e.cause;   // the original TypeError: Failed to fetch
}

Why a standard slot beats an ad-hoc property: every layer agrees where to look, and tooling cooperates — Node and browser consoles print the cause chain along with the stack.

Rule of thumb: any catch that throws a new error should attach the old one as cause — rewrapping without it deletes the evidence.

cards.question: How do cause chains work with custom error classes?
cards.answer:
Causes chain: each layer wraps the one below, and e.cause.cause walks back down through the layers to the root failure.

To support it in a custom class, pass the options bag through to the parent — super(message, options):

class ReadError extends Error {
  constructor(message, options) {
    super(message, options); // forwards { cause }
    this.name = "ReadError";
  }
}

throw new ReadError("Validation Error", { cause: err });

Walking a chain to find a specific original:

function findCause(err, Type) {
  for (let e = err; e; e = e.cause)
    if (e instanceof Type) return e;
  return null;
}

Gotcha: a custom constructor that only calls super(message) silently drops the cause — forward the options.

cards.question: What is a Proxy — how does wrapping an object work?
cards.answer:
A Proxy wraps another object and intercepts operations on it: new Proxy(target, handler). The handler holds traps — methods that catch reads, writes, deletion and so on. If a trap is missing, the operation goes straight to the target.

With an empty handler the proxy is a transparent wrapper:

let target = {};
let proxy = new Proxy(target, {}); // no traps

proxy.test = 5;     // write goes to target
alert(target.test); // 5
alert(proxy.test);  // 5 — read comes from target

The whole idea: you hand out the proxy instead of the original, so every operation passes through your traps first. The proxy has no properties of its own — it should fully replace the target everywhere in the code.

cards.question: Which traps cover the main operations, and what are invariants?
cards.answer:
Almost every internal operation has a trap with its own name:

TrapFires on
getreading a property
setwriting a property
hasthe in operator
deletePropertydelete
applycalling the target as a function
constructnew
ownKeysObject.keys, for..in, spread of keys
getPrototypeOf / setPrototypeOfprototype access

apply and construct only make sense when the target is a function:

function sum(a, b) { return a + b; }
let logged = new Proxy(sum, {
  apply(target, thisArg, args) {
    console.log(`call with ${args}`);
    return target.apply(thisArg, args);
  }
});

Traps must follow invariants — rules the language enforces, promises a proxy can't break. The ones you'll meet: set and deleteProperty must return true on success and false on failure, and get on a non-writable, non-configurable property must return its real value. Violate one and you get a TypeError.

cards.question: How does a get trap provide default values?
cards.answer:
A get trap can return something useful instead of undefined when a property is missing.

let dictionary = new Proxy({hello: "hola"}, {
  get: (target, phrase) => phrase in target ? target[phrase] : phrase
});

dictionary.hello;   // "hola"
dictionary.welcome; // "welcome" — unknown phrase echoed back

The trap fires on every read, checks whether the key exists, and substitutes a fallback when it doesn't. Same trick gives arrays a default of 0 for missing indexes.

The gotcha: it only works if all code reads through the proxy. Keep a reference to the raw target around, and reads through it skip the trap.

cards.question: How does a set trap validate writes?
cards.answer:
A set trap checks the value at write time and accepts or rejects it — validation at the gate, before the property ever lands on the object.

let numbers = new Proxy([], {
  set(target, prop, val) {
    if (typeof val != "number") return false; // TypeError in strict mode
    target[prop] = val;
    return true; // set MUST report success
  }
});

numbers.push(1);     // works
numbers.push("test"); // TypeError

Note that array methods like push still work: they write by index under the hood, and every such write goes through the trap. No method patching needed.

The gotcha: set must return true. Forget it, and every write throws — even valid ones.

cards.question: Which proxy traps work together to fully hide _-prefixed properties?
cards.answer:
One trap is never enough — you need four, each covering a different angle of access:

TrapBlocks
getreads (user._x)
hasthe in operator
ownKeysObject.keys, for..in
deletePropertydelete

Miss has and "_password" in user still says true.

cards.question: How do you write a proxy that hides _-prefixed properties from every angle?
cards.answer:
let user = { name: "John", _password: "***" };

user = new Proxy(user, {
  get(target, prop) {
    if (prop.startsWith("_")) throw new Error("Access denied");
    return target[prop];
  },
  has(target, prop) { // "in" operator
    return !prop.startsWith("_");
  },
  deleteProperty(target, prop) { // delete
    if (prop.startsWith("_")) throw new Error("Access denied");
    delete target[prop];
    return true;
  },
  ownKeys(target) { // Object.keys, for..in
    return Object.keys(target).filter(key => !key.startsWith("_"));
  }
});

user._password;      // Error: Access denied
"_password" in user; // false
Object.keys(user);   // ["name"]

cards.question: Why do object methods that read this._password break through a hiding proxy?
cards.answer:
Called through the proxy, this is the proxy, so the method's this._password read hits the get trap and throws.

Fix: in the get trap, bind returned functions to the real target so their this bypasses the trap.

cards.question: Why can Object.keys ignore what your ownKeys trap returns?
cards.answer:
Because Object.keys only lists properties with the enumerable flag — and to check that flag it calls getOwnPropertyDescriptor for every key ownKeys returned.

let user = new Proxy({}, {
  ownKeys(target) { return ["a", "b", "c"]; }
});
Object.keys(user); // [] — keys reported, but no descriptors

user = new Proxy({}, {
  ownKeys(target) { return ["a", "b", "c"]; },
  getOwnPropertyDescriptor(target, prop) {
    return { enumerable: true, configurable: true };
  }
});
Object.keys(user); // ["a", "b", "c"]

When the keys actually exist on the target (as in the hiding-_private pattern), the default descriptor lookup finds them and ownKeys alone is enough. The trap pair is only needed when you report keys the target doesn't really have.

cards.question: What are the three things a proxy can't fully wrap?
cards.answer: Built-ins with internal slots (Map, Set, Date), private class #fields, and identity — a proxy is never === its target.

cards.question: Why does proxying a Map and calling proxy.set(...) throw?
cards.answer:
Map keeps its data in internal slots and its methods reach them via this. Through a proxy this is the proxy, which has no slots.

let map = new Map();
let proxy = new Proxy(map, {});
proxy.set("test", 1); // Error

Private #fields fail the same way. Plain arrays have no slots, so they proxy fine.

cards.question: How do you make a proxied Map work, and what's the catch?
cards.answer:
Bind its methods to the target in the get trap — but then calls bypass the proxy.

let fixed = new Proxy(map, {
  get(target, prop, receiver) {
    let value = Reflect.get(target, prop, receiver);
    return typeof value == "function" ? value.bind(target) : value;
  }
});
fixed.set("test", 1); // works — but bypasses the proxy

cards.question: Can a proxy be made to compare === equal to its target?
cards.answer: No — === can't be trapped. Any code comparing objects by identity will tell the proxy and the original apart.

cards.question: What is Proxy.revocable?
cards.answer:
Proxy.revocable(target, handler) returns a proxy plus a revoke() function that switches it off: after revoke(), any operation on the proxy throws.

let object = { data: "secret" };
let { proxy, revoke } = Proxy.revocable(object, {});

alert(proxy.data); // "secret"
revoke();
alert(proxy.data); // TypeError: proxy revoked

The use case: hand the proxy to code you don't fully control, keep revoke for yourself. Later you can cut off access in one call — no need to hunt down every reference to the object.

After revocation the proxy drops its internal link to the target, so the target can be garbage-collected even while revoked proxies are still referenced.

cards.question: What is Reflect?
cards.answer: Reflect is a built-in object whose methods are the internal object operations exposed as plain function calls — Reflect.get, Reflect.set, Reflect.has, Reflect.deleteProperty, Reflect.construct…

cards.question: How does Reflect mirror Proxy traps?
cards.answer:
For every Proxy trap there's a Reflect method with the same name and arguments that performs the default behavior — so a forwarding trap is one line: do your work, then delegate.

let user = new Proxy({name: "John"}, {
  get(target, prop, receiver) {
    console.log(`GET ${prop}`);
    return Reflect.get(target, prop, receiver);
  },
  set(target, prop, val, receiver) {
    console.log(`SET ${prop}=${val}`);
    return Reflect.set(target, prop, val, receiver); // returns true/false
  }
});

cards.question: What is Reflect good for outside of proxies?
cards.answer:
It gives you operators as functions — handy when an operation must be passed around or applied dynamically:

OperatorReflect call
inReflect.has(obj, "key")
deleteReflect.deleteProperty(obj, "key")
newReflect.construct(F, args)

cards.question: What is the receiver argument in a get trap?
cards.answer: receiver is the object the read actually started on. Reflect.get(target, prop, receiver) runs a getter with this = receiver; plain target[prop] runs it with this = target and breaks inheritance.

cards.question: Why does forwarding with target[prop] instead of Reflect.get break inheritance?
cards.answer:
let user = {
  _name: "Guest",
  get name() { return this._name; }
};

let userProxy = new Proxy(user, {
  get(target, prop, receiver) {
    return Reflect.get(target, prop, receiver); // not target[prop]!
  }
});

let admin = { __proto__: userProxy, _name: "Admin" };
admin.name; // "Admin" — with target[prop] it would be "Guest"

The read starts on admin, climbs to the proxy, fires the trap. The getter lives on user, but its this must stay admin to read admin._name. Only receiver carries that in.

cards.question: What's the rule for finishing any proxy trap?
cards.answer:
Finish with the matching Reflect call, passing all the trap's arguments through.

It's correct by construction — defaults, invariants, and this are all handled for you.

cards.question: What are well-known symbols?
cards.answer:
Well-known symbols are predefined symbol keys — Symbol.iterator, Symbol.toPrimitive, Symbol.hasInstance… — that the language itself looks up on your objects. Each one is a hook into a built-in behavior: implement the symbol, and the language treats your object as a native participant of that protocol.

Being symbols, they can never collide with ordinary property names. The most used one is Symbol.iterator — what for..of and spread call:

let range = {
  [Symbol.iterator]() { /* return an iterator */ }
};
[...range]; // works — range is now iterable

How to write the iterator itself is covered in the Iterables topic. Its async twin Symbol.asyncIterator drives for await..of the same way.

cards.question: How does Symbol.toPrimitive work?
cards.answer:
