Symbol.toPrimitive is a single method for object-to-primitive conversion. The language calls it with a hint — "string", "number" or "default" — saying what kind of primitive the operation wants.

let money = {
  amount: 100,
  [Symbol.toPrimitive](hint) {
    return hint == "string" ? "$100" : 100;
  }
};

`${money}`;  // "$100" — string context, hint "string"
+money;      // 100    — math, hint "number"
money + 50;  // 150    — binary +, hint "default"

The hints: "string" for string contexts like alert and template literals; "number" for math; "default" when the operator isn't sure — binary + (could concatenate or add) and == with a primitive.

It replaces the old toString/valueOf pair with one method and has priority over both.

cards.question: What does Symbol.toStringTag do?
cards.answer:
Symbol.toStringTag sets the label that Object.prototype.toString reports for your object — the part after [object .

class Db {
  get [Symbol.toStringTag]() { return "Db"; }
}
alert( {}.toString.call(new Db()) ); // "[object Db]"

This is how environments brand their objects — [object Map], [object Window] — and a cheap type label for your own classes.

cards.question: What does Symbol.hasInstance customize?
cards.answer:
It customizes instanceof. obj instanceof Klass first checks for Klass[Symbol.hasInstance] and calls it if present; only without it does it walk the prototype chain.

class Stack {
  static [Symbol.hasInstance](value) { return Array.isArray(value); }
}
[] instanceof Stack; // true

cards.question: What does Symbol.species customize?
cards.answer:
It tells built-in methods like map/filter/slice which constructor to use for derived objects. By default a subclass's results stay the subclass; override species to make them plain.

class PowerArray extends Array {
  static get [Symbol.species]() { return Array; }
}
let arr = new PowerArray(1, 2, 3);
arr.filter(x => x > 1) instanceof PowerArray; // false — plain Array

cards.question: What's the mental model for well-known symbols?
cards.answer:
The spec's extension points, published as symbol keys.

Beyond hasInstance and species, narrower niches exist — e.g. Symbol.dispose for the new using declarations.

cards.question: What does eval do, and why is it avoided?
cards.answer:
eval(code) runs a string as JavaScript and returns the value of its last statement. Its defining quirk: the code runs in the current lexical environment — it can read and change your local variables.

let x = 5;
eval("x = 10");
alert(x); // 10 — outer variable changed

let result = eval("1 + 1"); // 2

That scope access is exactly why it's shunned:

— Minifiers break around it. Locals get renamed to a, b — but a string mentioning x can't be safely renamed, so tools stop optimizing near eval.

— Hidden dependencies. Code in a string that reaches into surrounding scope is invisible to readers and refactoring.

— Injection risk. Evaluating anything built from user input means executing the user's code with your privileges. CSP headers commonly forbid eval outright.

Small mercy: in strict mode eval gets its own mini-scope, so variables declared inside the string don't leak out.

cards.question: How is new Function different from eval?
cards.answer:
new Function("a", "b", "return a + b") compiles a string into a function in the global scope — it sees no local variables, so everything it needs must be passed in as parameters.

let sum = new Function("a", "b", "return a + b");
sum(1, 2); // 3

let value = "test";
let f = new Function("alert(value)");
f(); // Error: value is not defined — globals only

That isolation is the point: no hidden scope capture, dependencies are explicit arguments, and minified code keeps working. It's the contained choice when dynamic code is genuinely required — templates compiled at runtime, code received from a server.

In modern code both are near-extinct: dynamic behavior comes from functions as values, JSON.parse for data, and dynamic import() for code.

cards.question: What decides whether a value stays in memory — what is reachability?
cards.answer:
Memory management in JavaScript is automatic, and it rests on one criterion: reachability. A value stays in memory while it can be reached from a root through some chain of references; everything unreachable is removed by the garbage collector.

The roots are: the currently running function's local variables and parameters, the locals of every function up the call chain, and global variables.

let user = { name: "John" };
let admin = user;

user = null;   // still reachable through admin — kept
admin = null;  // now nothing reaches it — collected

That's the whole contract: you can't trigger or block collection from code — the only thing you control is references.

cards.question: Can objects that reference each other still be collected?
cards.answer:
Yes. Being referenced is not the same as being reachable — what counts is a path from a root. A whole cluster of interlinked objects becomes garbage the moment its last outside link is cut.

function marry(man, woman) {
  man.wife = woman;
  woman.husband = man;
  return { father: man, mother: woman };
}
let family = marry({ name: "John" }, { name: "Ann" });

family = null;
// John and Ann still point at each other, but the whole
// "island" is cut off from the roots — all of it is collected

Only incoming references keep an object alive; its outgoing ones don't matter. Delete family.father and family.mother.husband, and John is collectible even while he still references Ann.

cards.question: How does the garbage collector actually work?
cards.answer:
The basic algorithm is mark-and-sweep: start at the roots and mark them, follow every reference and mark each object found, repeat until nothing new is reachable — then remove everything unmarked.

Picture paint poured on the roots, flowing along references. Whatever the paint never touches is garbage.

Engines like V8 layer optimizations on top so this doesn't stall your code:

Generational — most objects die young, so new objects live in a small "nursery" scanned often; survivors graduate to an old generation scanned rarely. Incremental — instead of one long pause, the marking work is split into many tiny slices interleaved with your code. Idle-time — the collector prefers to run while the CPU is idle anyway.

All of it is invisible and uncontrollable from JavaScript — there's no API to run or postpone GC. You tune memory by managing reachability, nothing else.

cards.question: If the GC frees everything unreachable, how can JavaScript leak memory?
cards.answer:
A leak in a garbage-collected language is unwanted reachability: objects you're done with that something still references — so the GC must keep them. The collector frees what's unreachable; it can't know what's unneeded.

The most common source: forgotten timers and listeners. A live setInterval references its callback, and the callback's closure references everything it uses — forever, until cleared. Same for handlers on long-lived targets like window or a global emitter.

setInterval(() => render(bigData), 1000);
// bigData stays alive until clearInterval

window.addEventListener("resize", handler);
// remove on teardown — or pass { signal } and abort()

The fix is always the same move: break the reference when usefulness ends — clearInterval, removeEventListener, an AbortController signal on component teardown.

cards.question: How do closures cause memory leaks?
cards.answer:
A long-lived callback that closed over a large structure keeps all of it alive — even if it only reads one field.

Fix: extract the field into its own variable and let the big structure be collected.

cards.question: Why are detached DOM nodes a leak?
cards.answer:
An element removed from the page but still referenced from JS keeps itself and its entire subtree in memory.

let el = document.getElementById("panel");
el.remove();   // off the page...
// ...but el still references the node — it and its
// whole subtree stay in memory until el = null

cards.question: Why do caches that only grow leak memory, and how do you fix it?
cards.answer:
A module-level Map cache has no way to shrink — keys nobody will ask for again stay reachable forever.

const cache = new Map();
function handle(req) {
  cache.set(req.id, heavyResult(req)); // only ever grows
}

Fix: bound the size and evict, or — when entries belong to an object — key by that object in a WeakMap so the entry dies with its key.

cards.question: How do you find a memory leak with DevTools?
cards.answer:
Compare heap snapshots: take one, exercise the suspected flow a few times, take another, and diff — object counts that only ever grow are your leak.

In DevTools → Memory:

Heap snapshot captures everything alive right now. The Comparison view between two snapshots shows what was allocated and never freed. For any suspicious object, its retainer chain shows exactly which reference is keeping it alive — that reference is the bug.

Allocation timeline records allocations as you interact with the page: bars that stay blue (still alive) after the action is long over point straight at the leaking code.

Two habits keep you honest: click the bin icon to force GC before each snapshot, and trust growth across repeated cycles — one cycle's growth may just be caches warming up.

cards.question: What is a WeakRef?
cards.answer:
A WeakRef holds an object without keeping it alive — the GC is free to collect the target anyway. deref() returns the object if it's still in memory, or undefined if it's gone.

let user = { name: "John" };
let ref = new WeakRef(user);

user = null; // the WeakRef alone doesn't protect it

let obj = ref.deref();
if (obj) {
  // still alive — and obj is now a normal strong reference
} else {
  // collected
}

So a WeakRef says "give me the object if it happens to still exist" — the opposite of every normal reference, which guarantees existence.

cards.question: When is a WeakRef actually useful?
cards.answer:
For caches that shouldn't pin their values: keep big objects available while they happen to be in memory, but let the GC evict them under pressure.

The other use: pointing at DOM nodes without blocking their cleanup after removal.

cards.question: How does a WeakRef cache avoid pinning its values?
cards.answer:
Store new WeakRef(value) and read with .deref() — a hit works while the value lives, a miss just rebuilds it.

const cache = new Map(); // name → WeakRef

function getImage(name) {
  const cached = cache.get(name)?.deref();
  if (cached) return cached;          // alive — free hit

  const img = createImage(name);      // gone — rebuild
  cache.set(name, new WeakRef(img));
  return img;
}

cards.question: What does a WeakRef cache leave behind, and what cleans it up?
cards.answer: When a value is collected, its stale WeakRef entry still sits in the Map — cleaning those up is exactly what FinalizationRegistry is for.

cards.question: If your cache key is an object, do you need a WeakRef?
cards.answer: No — when the key itself is an object, WeakMap is the right tool, not WeakRef.

cards.question: What is a FinalizationRegistry?
cards.answer:
It runs a callback after a registered object has been collected — typically to delete its stale entry from a WeakRef cache.

const registry = new FinalizationRegistry(
  key => cache.delete(key)        // receives the "held value"
);
registry.register(img, "user-42"); // when img is collected,
                                   // the callback gets "user-42"

cards.question: Why can't you rely on a FinalizationRegistry for correctness?
cards.answer: GC timing is unpredictable: collection may happen much later than expected, or never. Callbacks aren't guaranteed to run at all (the program exits first, or the registry itself becomes unreachable). And between losing its last reference and being collected, an object can even come back through deref().

cards.question: What should you never put on a finalizer callback?
cards.answer:
Critical cleanup — closing connections, releasing locks, saving data. The spec's own advice is avoid if possible.

WeakRef and FinalizationRegistry exist for one narrow pattern: "nice to keep, fine to lose" caches with best-effort tidying.
